title: Moderation
text:
By the end of this tutorial you will be able to:

describe what moderation is and how it differs from mediation
fit a moderated regression model with lm() and interpret an interaction term
centre predictors at their mean and compute simple slopes
plot an interaction with ggplot2
fit a multigroup SEM in lavaan to test moderation by a categorical variable
recognise the syntax for latent variable interaction (advanced, beyond MSc scope)

title: Moderation
text:
By the end of this tutorial you will be able to:

describe what moderation is and how it differs from mediation
fit a moderated regression model with lm() and interpret an interaction term
centre predictors at their mean and compute simple slopes
plot an interaction with ggplot2
fit a multigroup SEM in lavaan to test moderation by a categorical variable
recognise the syntax for latent variable interaction (advanced, beyond MSc scope)

title: Moderation
text:
Setup


Setup code (you have seen this before)


library(haven)
library(lavaan)
library(ggplot2)

title: Moderation
text:
What is moderation?
Moderation addresses the question “when or for whom does \(X\) affect \(Y\)?” A moderator \(W\) changes the size or direction of the \(X \rightarrow Y\) relationship. Unlike a mediator, the moderator is not in the causal chain from \(X\) to \(Y\) — it modifies the relationship itself.

Examples

The effect of job control on well-being may be stronger for employees with high self-efficacy than for those with low self-efficacy. Self-efficacy moderates the job control → well-being relationship.
The effect of training on performance may depend on gender: the training may be more effective for one gender than the other. Gender moderates the training → performance relationship.



Moderation versus mediation









Mediation
Moderation




Question
How does \(X\) affect \(Y\)?
When does \(X\) affect \(Y\)?


Third variable
In the causal chain (\(X \rightarrow M \rightarrow Y\))
Not in the causal chain; modifies the \(X \rightarrow Y\) path


Statistical model
Indirect effect \(ab\)
Interaction \(X \times W\)


Tutorial
11, 12
16

title: Moderation
text:
Moderated regression with lm()
The simplest moderation analysis is a moderated regression: you predict \(Y\) from \(X\), \(W\), and their interaction \(X \times W\). In R, the interaction is specified with the * operator, which expands to both main effects plus the product:

fit_mod <- lm(creat ~ con * gender, data = ps)
summary(fit_mod)

## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = creat ~ con * gender, data = ps)
## 
## Residuals:
##      Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
## -2.07002 -0.73321 -0.06803  0.59963  2.00284 
## 
## Coefficients:
##                  Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)       3.45216    0.36066   9.572   <2e-16 ***
## con              -0.09414    0.09416  -1.000    0.318    
## genderFemale     -0.39408    0.52905  -0.745    0.457    
## con:genderFemale  0.09712    0.13858   0.701    0.484    
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.8585 on 328 degrees of freedom
##   (11 observations deleted due to missingness)
## Multiple R-squared:  0.003309,   Adjusted R-squared:  -0.005807 
## F-statistic: 0.363 on 3 and 328 DF,  p-value: 0.7798


The con * gender term expands to con + gender + con:gender, where con:gender is the interaction. The model has three coefficients beyond the intercept:

con — the effect of job control on creativity for the reference group of gender (Male, since it is the first level of the factor)
genderFemale — the difference in creativity between females and males when job control is zero (which is not meaningful if job control is on a 1–5 scale — see centring below)
con:genderFemale — the moderation effect: how much the slope of job control on creativity differs for females relative to males

If the interaction term is significant, gender moderates the relationship — the slope differs between males and females.

Centring predictors
The con coefficient and the genderFemale coefficient are interpreted at con = 0, which is not meaningful for a 1–5 Likert scale. To make the main effects interpretable, centre the continuous predictor at its mean:

ps$con_c <- scale(ps$con, scale = FALSE)   # mean-centre (subtract the mean)
attr(ps$con_c, "scaled:center")            # show the mean that was subtracted

## [1] 3.760447

fit_mod_c <- lm(creat ~ con_c * gender, data = ps)
summary(fit_mod_c)

## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = creat ~ con_c * gender, data = ps)
## 
## Residuals:
##      Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
## -2.07002 -0.73321 -0.06803  0.59963  2.00284 
## 
## Coefficients:
##                    Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)         3.09816    0.06435  48.146   <2e-16 ***
## con_c              -0.09414    0.09416  -1.000    0.318    
## genderFemale       -0.02885    0.09449  -0.305    0.760    
## con_c:genderFemale  0.09712    0.13858   0.701    0.484    
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.8585 on 328 degrees of freedom
##   (11 observations deleted due to missingness)
## Multiple R-squared:  0.003309,   Adjusted R-squared:  -0.005807 
## F-statistic: 0.363 on 3 and 328 DF,  p-value: 0.7798


After centring:

con_c — the effect of job control on creativity for males, at the mean of job control (i.e. at con_c = 0)
genderFemale — the difference in creativity between females and males at the mean of job control
con_c:genderFemale — the moderation effect (unchanged by centring — only the main effects change interpretation)







NoteWhy centring matters



Centring does not change the interaction term or the model’s overall fit. It changes only the interpretation of the main effects. Without centring, the main effects are evaluated at con = 0 (meaningless). With centring, they are evaluated at the sample mean of con (meaningful). Always centre continuous predictors before interpreting main effects in a moderation model.




Simple slopes
A significant interaction means the slope of con on creat differs by gender. To understand how it differs, compute the simple slopes — the slope of con on creat separately for each gender group.
For a two-level moderator, the simplest approach is to fit separate regressions for each group:

# Slope of con on creat for males
fit_male <- lm(creat ~ con_c, data = subset(ps, gender == "Male"))
summary(fit_male)$coefficients

##                Estimate Std. Error   t value      Pr(>|t|)
## (Intercept)  3.09815770 0.06331666 48.931158 2.093875e-104
## con_c       -0.09413823 0.09265061 -1.016056  3.109973e-01

# Slope of con on creat for females
fit_female <- lm(creat ~ con_c, data = subset(ps, gender == "Female"))
summary(fit_female)$coefficients

##                Estimate Std. Error     t value     Pr(>|t|)
## (Intercept) 3.069308185 0.07045775 43.56239069 9.093927e-88
## con_c       0.002985641 0.10353237  0.02883775 9.770318e-01


Compare the con_c coefficients: if they differ substantially (and the interaction was significant), the moderation is practically meaningful. The con_c coefficient for males is the slope at the reference level; for females it is the slope at the other level.


Plotting the interaction

ggplot(ps, aes(x = con_c, y = creat, colour = gender)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE) +
  labs(x = "Job control (centred at mean)",
       y = "Creativity (mean of 3 items)",
       colour = "Gender",
       title = "Job control and creativity by gender") +
  theme_minimal()




Interaction plot: the relationship between job control (centred) and creativity, separately for males and females. Each line is the simple slope for one gender group.




The plot shows two fitted lines — one for each gender group. If the lines are parallel, there is no moderation (the slopes are the same). If the lines diverge or cross, moderation is present (the slopes differ).






TipTry it



Fit a moderated regression of creat on pa (centred) and gender. Is the interaction significant? Plot the interaction with ggplot2. Do males and females differ in how strongly positive affect predicts creativity?








CautionSolution






ps$pa_c <- scale(ps$pa, scale = FALSE)
fit_pa_mod <- lm(creat ~ pa_c * gender, data = ps)
summary(fit_pa_mod)

## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = creat ~ pa_c * gender, data = ps)
## 
## Residuals:
##      Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
## -2.02495 -0.62345 -0.01414  0.56888  2.02102 
## 
## Coefficients:
##                   Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)        3.07009    0.06345  48.386  < 2e-16 ***
## pa_c               0.18596    0.06179   3.009  0.00282 ** 
## genderFemale       0.02836    0.09320   0.304  0.76111    
## pa_c:genderFemale -0.01388    0.08584  -0.162  0.87161    
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.8378 on 328 degrees of freedom
##   (11 observations deleted due to missingness)
## Multiple R-squared:  0.05062,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.04194 
## F-statistic:  5.83 on 3 and 328 DF,  p-value: 0.0006853

ggplot(ps, aes(x = pa_c, y = creat, colour = gender)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE) +
  labs(x = "Positive affect (centred at mean)",
       y = "Creativity",
       colour = "Gender") +
  theme_minimal()








Check the pa_c:genderFemale coefficient in the summary. If it is significant, gender moderates the PA → creativity relationship. The plot shows whether the two gender groups’ slopes diverge.

title: Moderation
text:
Multigroup SEM in lavaan
When the moderator is a categorical variable (like gender) and you have latent variables in your model, you can test moderation with multigroup SEM. Instead of adding an interaction term, you fit the model separately for each group and test whether the parameters are equal across groups.

Specifying a multigroup model
Pass the group argument to cfa() or sem():

model_cfa <- '
  PA  =~ pa1 + pa2 + pa3
  neg =~ na1 + na2 + na3
  PA  ~~ neg
'
fit_config <- cfa(model_cfa, data = ps, group = "gender", estimator = "MLR")
summary(fit_config, standardized = TRUE, fit.measures = TRUE)

## lavaan 0.6-21 ended normally after 37 iterations

##    .na2               0.453    0.067    6.732    0.000    0.453    0.406
##    .na3               0.301    0.057    5.238    0.000    0.301    0.254
##     PA                0.687    0.119    5.755    0.000    1.000    1.000
##     neg               0.964    0.126    7.680    0.000    1.000    1.000


By default, lavaan fits the configural invariance model — the same factor structure in both groups, but all parameters (loadings, variances, covariances) are freely estimated in each group. The output shows parameter estimates for each group separately.


Testing measurement invariance
To test whether the factor structure is the same across groups, you progressively constrain parameters to be equal and compare the models:

Configural invariance — same structure, all parameters free (fitted above)
Weak invariance — factor loadings equal across groups
Strong invariance — loadings and intercepts equal
Strict invariance — loadings, intercepts, and residual variances equal


# Weak invariance (equal loadings)
fit_weak <- cfa(model_cfa, data = ps, group = "gender",
                group.equal = "loadings", estimator = "MLR")

lavTestLRT(fit_config, fit_weak)

## 
## Scaled Chi-Squared Difference Test (method = "satorra.bentler.2001")
## 
## lavaan->lavTestLRT():  
##    lavaan NOTE: The "Chisq" column contains standard test statistics, not the 
##    robust test that should be reported per model. A robust difference test is 
##    a function of two standard (not robust) statistics.
## 
##            Df    AIC    BIC  Chisq Chisq diff   RMSEA Df diff Pr(>Chisq)  
## fit_config 16 5265.4 5409.7 17.150                                        
## fit_weak   20 5268.1 5397.2 27.835     12.714 0.10534       4    0.01276 *
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1


A non-significant result means the loadings are equivalent across groups — weak invariance holds. If weak invariance holds, you can proceed to test strong and strict invariance. If it does not hold, the factor structure is not equivalent across groups, and comparing latent means across groups would be misleading.






NoteMeasurement invariance is a prerequisite for group comparison



Before you can compare latent means or structural paths across groups, you must establish that the measurement model is equivalent — that the same items measure the same factors in the same way in both groups. This is what measurement invariance testing does. Without it, observed group differences could be due to measurement artefacts rather than true group differences.




Testing moderation of structural paths
Once measurement invariance is established, you can test whether the structural paths differ across groups. In the context of our mediation model (Tutorial 12), you could test whether the indirect effect of con on creat through pa differs between males and females:

model_med <- '
  con   =~ p1 + p2 + p3
  pa    =~ pa1 + pa2 + pa3
  creat =~ creat1 + creat2 + creat3

  creat ~ b * pa + c * con
  pa    ~ a * con

  indirect := a * b
  direct   := c
  total    := c + (a * b)
'

# Fit with equal measurement model across groups
# but free structural paths
fit_med_group <- sem(model_med, data = ps, group = "gender",
                     group.equal = "loadings", estimator = "MLR")

# Now constrain structural paths to be equal
fit_med_group_eq <- sem(model_med, data = ps, group = "gender",
                        group.equal = c("loadings", "regressions"),
                        estimator = "MLR")

lavTestLRT(fit_med_group, fit_med_group_eq)

A significant result means the structural paths differ across groups — gender moderates the mediation. A non-significant result means the paths are equivalent — no moderation.






WarningMultigroup SEM requires sufficient sample size per group



Each group should have enough participants to estimate the model reliably. A rough rule of thumb is 100+ per group. The PS dataset has 178 males and 154 females — adequate but not generous. With smaller groups, the model may not converge or may produce unstable estimates.

title: Moderation
text:
Latent variable interaction (advanced)
When both the predictor and the moderator are latent variables (factors measured by multiple indicators), testing moderation requires modelling the interaction between two latent variables. This is more complex than a product term between observed variables.
lavaan supports latent interactions via the ~*~ operator (the product-indicator approach), but this is a PhD-level technique and beyond the scope of an MSc dissertation. We mention it here so you are aware it exists:

model_int <- '
  pa   =~ pa1 + pa2 + pa3
  con  =~ p1 + p2 + p3
  pa ~*~ con   # latent interaction between pa and con

  creat ~ pa + con + pa.con    # pa.con is the latent interaction term
'







ImportantLatent interaction is beyond MSc scope



Modelling interactions between latent variables requires the product-indicator approach (Klein & Moosbrugger, 2000) or the latent moderated structural equations (LMS) method. These are computationally demanding and theoretically advanced. For MSc work, we recommend:

If both predictor and moderator are latent: compute factor scores from a CFA, then use the product of the factor scores as an observed interaction term in lm() — a simpler but approximate approach.
If one variable is categorical: use multigroup SEM (as demonstrated above).
If both are observed: use lm() with a product term (as demonstrated above).

Latent interaction is a topic for PhD-level work.

title: Moderation
text:
Common errors






WarningInterpreting main effects in the presence of an interaction



When an interaction term is in the model, the main effects are conditional effects — they apply only at the reference level of the moderator (for a categorical moderator) or at zero (for a continuous moderator). Do not interpret main effects as “overall” effects when an interaction is present. Centre continuous moderators to make the main effects interpretable at the mean.

Standardising the moderator before centring
scale(x, scale = TRUE) both centres and standardises (divides by the SD). This changes the scale of the coefficient — it is now in SD units, not original units. Use scale = FALSE for centring only; use scale = TRUE only if you want standardised coefficients.


Multigroup SEM without measurement invariance
If you compare structural paths across groups without first establishing measurement invariance, any differences you find could be due to the items measuring different things in the two groups, not to true group differences in the structural paths. Always test measurement invariance first.

title: Moderation
text:
Exercises

Fit a moderated regression of creat on con (centred) and gender. Is the interaction significant? Compute the simple slopes for each gender group. Plot the interaction with ggplot2.
Fit a multigroup CFA on the two-factor PA/neg model with gender as the grouping variable. Test configural, weak, and strong invariance using lavTestLRT(). Does measurement invariance hold?
If measurement invariance holds in Exercise 2, fit a multigroup SEM with the mediation model (Tutorial 12) and test whether the structural paths are equal across groups. Is the mediation moderated by gender?
A researcher wants to test whether age moderates the effect of job control on creativity. Age is categorical (5 levels) in the PS dataset. Describe two ways they could test this: (a) with lm() and (b) with multigroup lavaan. What are the trade-offs?







CautionSolutions





Exercise 1

fit_ex1 <- lm(creat ~ con_c * gender, data = ps)
summary(fit_ex1)

## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = creat ~ con_c * gender, data = ps)
## 
## Residuals:
##      Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
## -2.07002 -0.73321 -0.06803  0.59963  2.00284 
## 
## Coefficients:
##                    Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)         3.09816    0.06435  48.146   <2e-16 ***
## con_c              -0.09414    0.09416  -1.000    0.318    
## genderFemale       -0.02885    0.09449  -0.305    0.760    
## con_c:genderFemale  0.09712    0.13858   0.701    0.484    
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.8585 on 328 degrees of freedom
##   (11 observations deleted due to missingness)
## Multiple R-squared:  0.003309,   Adjusted R-squared:  -0.005807 
## F-statistic: 0.363 on 3 and 328 DF,  p-value: 0.7798

coef_male   <- coef(lm(creat ~ con_c, data = subset(ps, gender == "Male")))["con_c"]
coef_female <- coef(lm(creat ~ con_c, data = subset(ps, gender == "Female")))["con_c"]
coef_male

##       con_c 
## -0.09413823

coef_female

##       con_c 
## 0.002985641

ggplot(ps, aes(x = con_c, y = creat, colour = gender)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE) +
  labs(x = "Job control (centred)", y = "Creativity", colour = "Gender") +
  theme_minimal()








The interaction is significant if the con_c:genderFemale p-value is below .05. The simple slopes (coef_male, coef_female) show how the effect of job control differs by gender.
Exercise 2

fit_config <- cfa(model_cfa, data = ps, group = "gender", estimator = "MLR")
fit_weak   <- cfa(model_cfa, data = ps, group = "gender",
                  group.equal = "loadings", estimator = "MLR")
fit_strong <- cfa(model_cfa, data = ps, group = "gender",
                  group.equal = c("loadings", "intercepts"), estimator = "MLR")

lavTestLRT(fit_config, fit_weak)

## 
## Scaled Chi-Squared Difference Test (method = "satorra.bentler.2001")
## 
## lavaan->lavTestLRT():  
##    lavaan NOTE: The "Chisq" column contains standard test statistics, not the 
##    robust test that should be reported per model. A robust difference test is 
##    a function of two standard (not robust) statistics.
## 
##            Df    AIC    BIC  Chisq Chisq diff   RMSEA Df diff Pr(>Chisq)  
## fit_config 16 5265.4 5409.7 17.150                                        
## fit_weak   20 5268.1 5397.2 27.835     12.714 0.10534       4    0.01276 *
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

lavTestLRT(fit_weak, fit_strong)

## 
## Scaled Chi-Squared Difference Test (method = "satorra.bentler.2001")
## 
## lavaan->lavTestLRT():  
##    lavaan NOTE: The "Chisq" column contains standard test statistics, not the 
##    robust test that should be reported per model. A robust difference test is 
##    a function of two standard (not robust) statistics.
## 
##            Df    AIC    BIC  Chisq Chisq diff    RMSEA Df diff Pr(>Chisq)
## fit_weak   20 5268.1 5397.2 27.835                                       
## fit_strong 24 5266.0 5379.9 33.735     5.9235 0.053879       4     0.2049


Configural → weak: if non-significant, loadings are equal (weak invariance holds). Weak → strong: if non-significant, intercepts are also equal (strong invariance holds). If strong invariance holds, you can compare latent means across groups.
Exercise 3

ps$p1 <- (ps$con9 + ps$con10) / 2
ps$p2 <- (ps$con1 + ps$con8)  / 2
ps$p3 <- (ps$con3 + ps$con6)  / 2

model_med <- '
  con   =~ p1 + p2 + p3
  pa    =~ pa1 + pa2 + pa3
  creat =~ creat1 + creat2 + creat3
  creat ~ b * pa + c * con
  pa    ~ a * con
  indirect := a * b
  direct   := c
  total    := c + (a * b)
'

fit_free <- sem(model_med, data = ps, group = "gender",
                group.equal = "loadings", estimator = "MLR")
fit_eq   <- sem(model_med, data = ps, group = "gender",
                group.equal = c("loadings", "regressions"), estimator = "MLR")
lavTestLRT(fit_free, fit_eq)

A significant result means the structural paths differ by gender — the mediation is moderated. A non-significant result means the paths are equivalent — no moderation.
Exercise 4
(a) With lm(): Recode age as a factor and fit lm(creat ~ con * age, data = ps). The interaction terms con:age2, con:age3, etc. test whether the slope of con differs for each age band relative to the reference band. This is simple but does not separate measurement error from structural effects.
(b) With multigroup lavaan: Fit the latent SEM mediation model with group = "age". This tests whether the structural paths among the latent factors differ across age groups. This is more appropriate if you want to remove measurement error, but it requires sufficient sample size per age band (the PS dataset has only 21 participants in age band 1, which is too few for a stable multigroup SEM).
Trade-offs: lm() is simpler, faster, and works with small per-group samples, but uses manifest scores (with measurement error). Multigroup lavaan removes measurement error but requires larger per-group samples and is more complex to specify and interpret.

title: Moderation
text:
Summary
You have now completed the full tutorial series. You can:

Fit CFA, path analysis, SEM, and mediation models in lavaan (Tutorials 05–12)
Report results in APA format with texreg tables and tidySEM diagrams (Tutorial 13)
Extend to multilevel and growth curve models (Tutorials 14–15)
Test moderation with lm() and multigroup SEM (Tutorial 16)

These are the core tools you need for a quantitative dissertation in organisational psychology. The cheat sheet, glossary, and notation key are there for quick reference. When you encounter a technique we have not covered — latent profile analysis, EFA, categorical outcome SEM — the lavaan documentation and the further reading lists in each tutorial are your next stop.
Good luck with your dissertation.

title: Moderation
text:
Further reading

Hayes (2018) — the standard reference for moderation, mediation, and conditional process analysis.
Hoyle (2012) — the Handbook of Structural Equation Modeling includes chapters on multigroup SEM and latent interactions.
Kelloway (2015) — the textbook basis for the original teaching materials; covers moderation in an SEM framework.

title: Multilevel models
text:
By the end of this tutorial you will be able to:

describe what a multilevel model is and when it is appropriate
fit a random-intercept and random-slope model with lme4::lmer()
interpret VarCorr() output and compute the intraclass correlation coefficient (ICC)
describe how lavaan extends to two-level SEM and recognise the syntax
decide when to use multilevel regression versus multilevel SEM

title: Multilevel models
text:
By the end of this tutorial you will be able to:

describe what a multilevel model is and when it is appropriate
fit a random-intercept and random-slope model with lme4::lmer()
interpret VarCorr() output and compute the intraclass correlation coefficient (ICC)
describe how lavaan extends to two-level SEM and recognise the syntax
decide when to use multilevel regression versus multilevel SEM

title: Multilevel models
text:
Setup


Setup code


library(lme4)
library(lavaan)
library(ggplot2)

title: Multilevel models
text:
What is a multilevel model?
Multilevel models (also called hierarchical linear models or mixed-effects models) are for data with a nested structure — observations clustered within higher-level units. Common examples in organisational psychology:

Employees nested within teams
Teams nested within departments
Repeated measures nested within individuals (longitudinal data — see Tutorial 15)

The defining feature is that observations within the same cluster are not independent — two employees in the same team tend to be more similar than two employees from different teams. Standard regression assumes independence, so applying it to nested data gives biased standard errors and incorrect p-values.
Multilevel models handle this by modelling the clustering explicitly. They estimate:

Fixed effects — the average relationship across all clusters (analogous to regression coefficients)
Random effects — how individual clusters deviate from the average (variance components)

title: Multilevel models
section: When to use a multilevel model
text:
When to use a multilevel model
Use a multilevel model when:

Your data has a nested structure (e.g. employees within teams, repeated measures within individuals)
There is meaningful variation between clusters (test this with the ICC — see below)
You have enough clusters (a rough rule of thumb: 20+ clusters with 5+ observations each)

Do not use a multilevel model if:

Your data is single-level (no clustering) — use lm() or lavaan instead
You have very few clusters (fewer than 10) — the random effects estimates will be unstable
The clustering is incidental (e.g. data collected on two different days, with no substantive meaning to the day) — you can often ignore it

title: Multilevel models
text:
The teaching dataset
The PS dataset we have used throughout this series is single-level — one row per participant, no clustering. To demonstrate multilevel modelling, we use a simulated dataset of employees nested within teams, generated by data-raw/simulate-multilevel.R.

ml <- read.csv("data/multilevel_sim.csv")
head(ml)

##   employee_id team_id     team_x      ind_x        y
## 1       E0001       1 -0.1102855 -0.5817275 1.697048
## 2       E0002       1 -0.1102855  0.7000802 2.313822
## 3       E0003       1 -0.1102855  1.4921766 3.106420
## 4       E0004       1 -0.1102855  0.5265534 1.570512
## 5       E0005       1 -0.1102855  1.0377721 1.971969
## 6       E0006       1 -0.1102855 -1.8607164 2.839831

nrow(ml)

## [1] 348

length(unique(ml$team_id))

## [1] 30


The dataset has 348 employees across 30 teams, with an average team size of about 12. Each row has:

employee_id — a unique employee identifier
team_id — the team the employee belongs to (1–30)
team_x — a team-level predictor (a characteristic of the team, shared by all members)
ind_x — an individual-level predictor (a characteristic of the employee)
y — the outcome variable

title: Multilevel models
text:
Multilevel regression with lme4
The lme4 package fits multilevel models in R. The main function is lmer() — “linear mixed-effects regression”.

Random intercept model
The simplest multilevel model adds a random intercept for each cluster. This allows each team to have its own baseline level of y, while keeping the slopes (the effects of ind_x and team_x) the same across teams.
The syntax extends the lm() formula with a (1 | group) term:

fit_ri <- lmer(y ~ ind_x + team_x + (1 | team_id), data = ml)
summary(fit_ri)

## Linear mixed model fit by REML ['lmerMod']
## Formula: y ~ ind_x + team_x + (1 | team_id)
##    Data: ml
## 
## REML criterion at convergence: 842.9
## 
## Scaled residuals: 
##      Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
## -2.88841 -0.65618 -0.03109  0.60856  2.68643 
## 
## Random effects:
##  Groups   Name        Variance Std.Dev.
##  team_id  (Intercept) 0.371    0.6091  
##  Residual             0.537    0.7328  
## Number of obs: 348, groups:  team_id, 30
## 
## Fixed effects:
##             Estimate Std. Error t value
## (Intercept)  2.51366    0.13162  19.098
## ind_x        0.44701    0.04169  10.722
## team_x       0.37428    0.12879   2.906
## 
## Correlation of Fixed Effects:
##        (Intr) ind_x 
## ind_x  -0.013       
## team_x  0.440 -0.029


Reading the output:

Fixed effects — the Estimate column gives the coefficients for ind_x and team_x, interpreted exactly as in lm(). The intercept is the predicted y when all predictors are zero, for a team with an average random effect.
Random effects — the (Intercept) variance under team_id:(Intercept) tells you how much team means vary around the grand mean. The Residual variance is the within-team variance.



The intraclass correlation coefficient (ICC)
The ICC tells you what proportion of the total variance in y is between teams rather than within teams:
\[
\text{ICC} = \frac{\sigma^2_{\text{between}}}{\sigma^2_{\text{between}} + \sigma^2_{\text{within}}}
\tag{1}\]
Extract the variance components:

vc <- VarCorr(fit_ri)
vc

##  Groups   Name        Std.Dev.
##  team_id  (Intercept) 0.60906 
##  Residual             0.73279


Compute the ICC manually:

var_between <- attr(vc$team_id, "stddev")^2
var_within  <- attr(vc, "sc")^2
icc <- var_between / (var_between + var_within)
icc

## (Intercept) 
##   0.4085704


An ICC above .05 suggests meaningful clustering — a multilevel model is warranted. An ICC near 0 means the clustering explains very little variance and a single-level model may suffice.






NoteInterpreting the ICC



The ICC is the proportion of variance in the outcome that is due to differences between clusters rather than differences within clusters. An ICC of .20 means 20% of the variance in y is between teams, and 80% is within teams. Higher ICC means stronger clustering — and a stronger need for a multilevel model.




Random slope model
To allow the effect of ind_x to vary across teams — some teams may show a stronger relationship between ind_x and y than others — add a random slope:

fit_rs <- lmer(y ~ ind_x + team_x + (1 + ind_x | team_id), data = ml)
summary(fit_rs)

## Linear mixed model fit by REML ['lmerMod']
## Formula: y ~ ind_x + team_x + (1 + ind_x | team_id)
##    Data: ml
## 
## REML criterion at convergence: 834.2
## 
## Scaled residuals: 
##      Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
## -2.68860 -0.67861  0.00018  0.64023  2.43519 
## 
## Random effects:
##  Groups   Name        Variance Std.Dev. Corr 
##  team_id  (Intercept) 0.36606  0.6050        
##           ind_x       0.03118  0.1766   0.71 
##  Residual             0.50904  0.7135        
## Number of obs: 348, groups:  team_id, 30
## 
## Fixed effects:
##             Estimate Std. Error t value
## (Intercept)  2.50883    0.12962  19.355
## ind_x        0.43392    0.05245   8.273
## team_x       0.34687    0.11956   2.901
## 
## Correlation of Fixed Effects:
##        (Intr) ind_x 
## ind_x   0.353       
## team_x  0.425 -0.042


The (1 + ind_x | team_id) term says “allow each team to have its own intercept AND its own slope for ind_x”. The random effects section now shows:

The variance of the team intercepts ((Intercept))
The variance of the team slopes (ind_x)
The correlation between intercepts and slopes (Corr)







TipTry it



Fit a random-intercept model with ind_x as the only predictor (no team_x). Compare the ICC to the model with team_x included. Does adding team_x change the ICC? Why?








CautionSolution






fit_ri2 <- lmer(y ~ ind_x + (1 | team_id), data = ml)
vc2 <- VarCorr(fit_ri2)
var_b2 <- attr(vc2$team_id, "stddev")^2
var_w2 <- attr(vc2, "sc")^2
var_b2 / (var_b2 + var_w2)

## (Intercept) 
##   0.4718956


Adding team_x (a team-level predictor) should reduce the between-team variance, because some of what looked like “team differences” is actually explained by team_x. The ICC should be smaller in the model with team_x included.





Comparing random-intercept and random-slope models
Compare the two models with a likelihood-ratio test:

anova(fit_ri, fit_rs)

## Data: ml
## Models:
## fit_ri: y ~ ind_x + team_x + (1 | team_id)
## fit_rs: y ~ ind_x + team_x + (1 + ind_x | team_id)
##        npar    AIC    BIC  logLik -2*log(L)  Chisq Df Pr(>Chisq)  
## fit_ri    5 843.61 862.87 -416.80    833.61                       
## fit_rs    7 838.97 865.93 -412.48    824.97 8.6428  2    0.01328 *
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1


A significant result means the random slope improves the model — the effect of ind_x does vary across teams. A non-significant result means the simpler random-intercept model is sufficient.

title: Multilevel models
text:
lavaan for two-level SEM
lavaan can also fit multilevel models, but it goes beyond what lme4 does: it allows latent variables at each level. In a two-level SEM, you can model:

A measurement model at Level 1 (within individuals)
A structural model at Level 2 (between teams)
Different factor structures at each level

This is powerful but complex. We provide a conceptual overview and a small worked example.

The level: syntax
lavaan’s two-level syntax uses the level: keyword to separate within-cluster and between-cluster model specifications:

model_2level <- '
  level: 1
    fw =~ y1 + y2 + y3

  level: 2
    fb =~ y1 + y2 + y3
    fb ~ team_x
'

The level: 1 block specifies the within-cluster model (how individual-level indicators co-vary within teams). The level: 2 block specifies the between-cluster model (how team-level aggregates co-vary across teams). The same observed variables can have different factor structures at each level.


Fitting a two-level model
To fit a two-level model, pass cluster = "team_id" to sem():

fit_2level <- sem(model_2level, data = ml, cluster = "team_id",
                  estimator = "MLR")







WarningTwo-level SEM requires a different dataset



The simulated multilevel_sim.csv has only one outcome (y) per individual, which is not enough to demonstrate a two-level SEM with latent variables at each level. A proper two-level SEM needs at least three indicators per individual to define a within-level factor.
For a real two-level SEM in your dissertation, you would need multivariate data — multiple items per employee, nested within teams. The syntax above shows how such a model would be specified; fitting it requires a dataset with the right structure.




When to use lme4 versus lavaan for multilevel modelling







Use lme4 (lmer()) when…
Use lavaan (sem() with cluster =) when…




You have a single outcome variable
You have multiple indicators per individual


You are interested in regression coefficients
You want to model latent variables at either level


You do not need a measurement model
You want to separate within-cluster and between-cluster factor structures


Your data is continuous and roughly normal
You need fit indices to compare models



For most MSc dissertation applications, lme4 is sufficient. lavaan’s two-level SEM is a PhD-level technique, but it is worth knowing that the capability exists.

title: Multilevel models
text:
Common errors






Warningboundary (singular) fit: see ?isSingular



The random effects variance is estimated as zero or near-zero — the clusters do not differ enough to justify a random effect. Try removing the random effect and using lm() instead, or simplify the random-effects structure (e.g. remove a random slope if you have one).

number of levels of each grouping factor must be < number of observations
You have more clusters than observations — usually means your grouping variable is unique per row (e.g. using employee_id as the cluster when each employee has only one observation). Check that your grouping variable actually identifies clusters with multiple members.


Convergence warnings
The model is too complex for the data. Reduce the number of random effects, collect more data, or use a simpler model. Convergence problems in lmer() are common with small numbers of clusters.

title: Multilevel models
text:
Exercises

Fit a random-intercept model predicting y from ind_x with team_id as the cluster. Compute the ICC. Is a multilevel model justified?
Add team_x to the model from Exercise 1. Does the between-team variance decrease? Why? Compare the two models with anova().
Fit a random-slope model allowing the effect of ind_x to vary across teams. Compare it to the random-intercept model from Exercise 2 with anova(). Is the random slope justified?
Write a one-paragraph APA-style results summary for the model from Exercise 2, reporting the fixed effects, the variance components, and the ICC.







CautionSolutions





Exercise 1

fit1 <- lmer(y ~ ind_x + (1 | team_id), data = ml)
summary(fit1)

## Linear mixed model fit by REML ['lmerMod']
## Formula: y ~ ind_x + (1 | team_id)
##    Data: ml
## 
## REML criterion at convergence: 848.2
## 
## Scaled residuals: 
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -2.9124 -0.6449 -0.0343  0.6069  2.6983 
## 
## Random effects:
##  Groups   Name        Variance Std.Dev.
##  team_id  (Intercept) 0.4797   0.6926  
##  Residual             0.5368   0.7327  
## Number of obs: 348, groups:  team_id, 30
## 
## Fixed effects:
##             Estimate Std. Error t value
## (Intercept)  2.34552    0.13263   17.68
## ind_x        0.45169    0.04173   10.82
## 
## Correlation of Fixed Effects:
##       (Intr)
## ind_x -0.001

vc1 <- VarCorr(fit1)
var_b <- attr(vc1$team_id, "stddev")^2
var_w <- attr(vc1, "sc")^2
icc1 <- var_b / (var_b + var_w)
icc1

## (Intercept) 
##   0.4718956


If the ICC is above .05, a multilevel model is justified — there is meaningful variation between teams.
Exercise 2

fit2 <- lmer(y ~ ind_x + team_x + (1 | team_id), data = ml)
summary(fit2)

## Linear mixed model fit by REML ['lmerMod']
## Formula: y ~ ind_x + team_x + (1 | team_id)
##    Data: ml
## 
## REML criterion at convergence: 842.9
## 
## Scaled residuals: 
##      Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
## -2.88841 -0.65618 -0.03109  0.60856  2.68643 
## 
## Random effects:
##  Groups   Name        Variance Std.Dev.
##  team_id  (Intercept) 0.371    0.6091  
##  Residual             0.537    0.7328  
## Number of obs: 348, groups:  team_id, 30
## 
## Fixed effects:
##             Estimate Std. Error t value
## (Intercept)  2.51366    0.13162  19.098
## ind_x        0.44701    0.04169  10.722
## team_x       0.37428    0.12879   2.906
## 
## Correlation of Fixed Effects:
##        (Intr) ind_x 
## ind_x  -0.013       
## team_x  0.440 -0.029

vc2 <- VarCorr(fit2)
var_b2 <- attr(vc2$team_id, "stddev")^2
var_w2 <- attr(vc2, "sc")^2
var_b2 / (var_b2 + var_w2)

## (Intercept) 
##   0.4085704

anova(fit1, fit2)

## Data: ml
## Models:
## fit1: y ~ ind_x + (1 | team_id)
## fit2: y ~ ind_x + team_x + (1 | team_id)
##      npar    AIC    BIC  logLik -2*log(L)  Chisq Df Pr(>Chisq)   
## fit1    4 849.51 864.91 -420.75    841.51                        
## fit2    5 843.61 862.87 -416.80    833.61 7.8965  1   0.004953 **
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1


Adding team_x should reduce the between-team variance because some of the team-level differences are explained by team_x. The anova() test tells you whether team_x significantly improves the model.
Exercise 3

fit3 <- lmer(y ~ ind_x + team_x + (1 + ind_x | team_id), data = ml)
anova(fit2, fit3)

## Data: ml
## Models:
## fit2: y ~ ind_x + team_x + (1 | team_id)
## fit3: y ~ ind_x + team_x + (1 + ind_x | team_id)
##      npar    AIC    BIC  logLik -2*log(L)  Chisq Df Pr(>Chisq)  
## fit2    5 843.61 862.87 -416.80    833.61                       
## fit3    7 838.97 865.93 -412.48    824.97 8.6428  2    0.01328 *
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1


A significant result means the random slope improves the model — the effect of ind_x varies across teams. A non-significant result means the simpler random-intercept model is sufficient.
Exercise 4
Using the output from Exercise 2, write: “A multilevel model was fitted with employees nested within teams (N = 348, 30 teams). The intraclass correlation coefficient was ICC = …, indicating that …% of the variance in y was between teams. The effect of ind_x was \(b = ...\), \(p < .001\). The effect of team_x was \(b = ...\), \(p = ...\). The between-team variance was … and the within-team variance was ….”

title: Multilevel models
text:
Summary & next tutorial
You can now fit multilevel regression models with lme4::lmer(), interpret the variance components and ICC, and recognise the syntax for two-level SEM in lavaan. For most MSc applications, lme4 is the right tool; lavaan’s two-level SEM extends this to latent variables at each level. In the next tutorial we cover growth curve models — a specific type of multilevel model for longitudinal data, where repeated measures are nested within individuals.

title: Multilevel models
text:
Further reading

R Core Team (2024) — see the documentation for nlme and lme4 packages.
Wickham (2019) — for the deeper R programming underlying mixed-effects models.
Hoyle (2012) — the Handbook of Structural Equation Modeling includes chapters on multilevel SEM.

title: Mediation — practical
text:
By the end of this tutorial you will be able to:

label regression paths in lavaan with * and define the indirect effect with :=
fit a path mediation model with bootstrap standard errors and extract BCa confidence intervals
extend the mediation model to a full latent-variable SEM with three factors
report mediation results in APA format, including the indirect effect and its confidence interval

title: Mediation — practical
text:
By the end of this tutorial you will be able to:

label regression paths in lavaan with * and define the indirect effect with :=
fit a path mediation model with bootstrap standard errors and extract BCa confidence intervals
extend the mediation model to a full latent-variable SEM with three factors
report mediation results in APA format, including the indirect effect and its confidence interval

title: Mediation — practical
text:
Setup


Setup code (you have seen this before)


library(haven)
library(lavaan)
library(tidySEM)

title: Mediation — practical
text:
The mediation model
We test whether positive affect (PA) mediates the relationship between job control and creativity:

\(X\) = job control (con)
\(M\) = positive affect (pa)
\(Y\) = creativity (creat)

We fit this model in two forms:

Path mediation — manifest variables (mean scores), as a direct extension of Tutorial 09
Latent SEM mediation — latent variables with item-level indicators, as an extension of Tutorial 10

Both use the same := syntax to define the indirect effect.

title: Mediation — practical
section: Worked example 1: Path mediation with manifest variables
text:
Worked example 1: Path mediation with manifest variables

Computing the manifest variables

ps$con   <- (ps$con1 + ps$con3 + ps$con6 + ps$con8 + ps$con9 + ps$con10) / 6
ps$pa    <- (ps$pa1 + ps$pa2 + ps$pa3) / 3
ps$creat <- (ps$creat1 + ps$creat2 + ps$creat3) / 3



Specifying the model with labelled paths
The key innovation is labelling the regression paths with * so we can refer to them in := definitions:

model_med_path <- '
  creat ~ b * pa + c * con
  pa    ~ a * con

  indirect := a * b
  direct   := c
  total    := c + (a * b)
'

Reading the syntax:

creat ~ b * pa + c * con — the path from pa to creat is labelled b; the path from con to creat is labelled c
pa ~ a * con — the path from con to pa is labelled a
indirect := a * b — defines the indirect effect as the product \(ab\)
direct := c — defines the direct effect as \(c'\)
total := c + (a * b) — defines the total effect as \(c' + ab\)



Fitting with bootstrap standard errors
To test the indirect effect, we use bootstrap confidence intervals. This requires two changes:

Switch the estimator from MLR to ML (MLR is incompatible with se = "boot")
Request bootstrap standard errors with se = "boot" and bootstrap = 1000


set.seed(1234)
fit_med_path <- sem(model_med_path, data = ps,
                    estimator = "ML", se = "boot", bootstrap = 1000)







ImportantEstimator constraint with bootstrapping



When you request se = "boot", lavaan requires estimator = "ML" — the robust MLR estimator cannot be combined with bootstrapping. This is because the bootstrap builds its own empirical sampling distribution, making the Satorra–Bentler scaling correction unnecessary. The point estimates are the same under ML and MLR; only the standard errors differ, and for the indirect effect we use the bootstrap standard errors.




Reading the output

summary(fit_med_path, standardized = TRUE, rsquare = TRUE)

## lavaan 0.6-21 ended normally after 1 iteration
## 
##   Estimator                                         ML
##   Optimization method                           NLMINB
##   Number of model parameters                         5
## 
##                                                   Used       Total
##   Number of observations                           332         343
## 
## Model Test User Model:
##                                                       
##   Test statistic                                 0.000
##   Degrees of freedom                                 0
## 
## Parameter Estimates:
## 
##   Standard errors                            Bootstrap
##   Number of requested bootstrap draws             1000
##   Number of successful bootstrap draws            1000
## 
## Regressions:
##                    Estimate  Std.Err  z-value  P(>|z|)   Std.lv  Std.all
##   creat ~                                                               
##     pa         (b)    0.185    0.042    4.399    0.000    0.185    0.235
##     con        (c)   -0.091    0.069   -1.324    0.186   -0.091   -0.073
##   pa ~                                                                  
##     con        (a)    0.229    0.081    2.816    0.005    0.229    0.144
## 
## Variances:
##                    Estimate  Std.Err  z-value  P(>|z|)   Std.lv  Std.all
##    .creat             0.690    0.046   15.162    0.000    0.690    0.945
##    .pa                1.150    0.072   15.970    0.000    1.150    0.979
## 
## R-Square:
##                    Estimate
##     creat             0.055
##     pa                0.021
## 
## Defined Parameters:
##                    Estimate  Std.Err  z-value  P(>|z|)   Std.lv  Std.all
##     indirect          0.042    0.018    2.294    0.022    0.042    0.034
##     direct           -0.091    0.069   -1.324    0.186   -0.091   -0.073
##     total            -0.049    0.073   -0.668    0.504   -0.049   -0.039


The output now includes a Defined Parameters section at the bottom, showing the estimates for indirect, direct, and total. These are the derived parameters we defined with :=.


Extracting bootstrap confidence intervals
For clean BCa confidence intervals, use parameterEstimates() with boot.ci.type = "bca.simple":

pe_med <- parameterEstimates(fit_med_path, standardized = TRUE,
                             boot.ci.type = "bca.simple")
pe_med[pe_med$lhs %in% c("indirect", "direct", "total"),
       c("lhs", "est", "se", "z", "pvalue", "ci.lower", "ci.upper")]

##        lhs    est    se      z pvalue ci.lower ci.upper
## 7 indirect  0.042 0.018  2.294  0.022    0.012    0.085
## 8   direct -0.091 0.069 -1.324  0.186   -0.226    0.043
## 9    total -0.049 0.073 -0.668  0.504   -0.195    0.096


The columns:

est — the estimate of the defined parameter
se — the bootstrap standard error
z, pvalue — the Wald statistic and p-value (based on the bootstrap SE)
ci.lower, ci.upper — the BCa 95% confidence interval

The key row is indirect. If the BCa 95% CI for the indirect effect does not include zero, the indirect effect is significant at the .05 level — we have evidence of mediation.


Interpreting the results
Read the three defined parameters:

indirect (\(ab\)): the mediated effect of con on creat through pa. If the CI excludes zero, mediation is present.
direct (\(c'\)): the direct effect of con on creat, controlling for pa. If this is not significant, mediation is full; if it is significant, mediation is partial.
total (\(c' + ab\)): the total effect of con on creat. This may or may not be significant — a non-significant total effect does not rule out mediation (see Tutorial 11).







NoteThe total effect can be non-significant even when mediation is present



If the indirect effect is positive and the direct effect is negative (or vice versa), they can cancel each other out, producing a non-significant total effect. This is called inconsistent mediation or a suppressor effect. Always examine the indirect effect directly, not just the total effect.

title: Mediation — practical
text:
Worked example 2: Latent SEM mediation
Now we extend the mediation model to use latent variables for all three constructs. This is the full SEM version, combining the measurement model from Tutorial 10 with the mediation structure from Tutorial 11.

The model

# Build parcels for job control (6 items -> 3 parcels)
ps$p1 <- (ps$con9 + ps$con10) / 2
ps$p2 <- (ps$con1 + ps$con8)  / 2
ps$p3 <- (ps$con3 + ps$con6)  / 2

model_med_latent <- '
  # Measurement model
  con   =~ p1 + p2 + p3
  pa    =~ pa1 + pa2 + pa3
  creat =~ creat1 + creat2 + creat3

  # Structural model with labelled paths
  creat ~ b * pa + c * con
  pa    ~ a * con

  # Defined parameters
  indirect := a * b
  direct   := c
  total    := c + (a * b)
'



Fitting with bootstrap

set.seed(1234)
fit_med_latent <- sem(model_med_latent, data = ps,
                      estimator = "ML", se = "boot", bootstrap = 1000)



Reading the output

summary(fit_med_latent, standardized = TRUE, fit.measures = TRUE, rsquare = TRUE)

The output now has all the sections you met in Tutorial 10 (Latent variables, Regressions, Variances, R-Square) plus the Defined Parameters section at the bottom.


Bootstrap confidence intervals

pe_med_latent <- parameterEstimates(fit_med_latent, standardized = TRUE,
                                    boot.ci.type = "bca.simple")
pe_med_latent[pe_med_latent$lhs %in% c("indirect", "direct", "total"),
              c("lhs", "est", "se", "z", "pvalue", "ci.lower", "ci.upper",
                "std.all")]

##         lhs    est    se      z pvalue ci.lower ci.upper std.all
## 25 indirect  0.047 0.022  2.082  0.037    0.011    0.098   0.043
## 26   direct -0.102 0.070 -1.442  0.149   -0.241    0.041  -0.094
## 27    total -0.055 0.075 -0.734  0.463   -0.208    0.091  -0.051


Compare the indirect effect from the latent SEM to the indirect effect from the path mediation. The latent SEM estimate should be larger in absolute value — disattenuation removes the measurement error from all three constructs, giving an unbiased estimate of the mediated effect.


Fit indices
The latent SEM is overidentified (unlike the path mediation, which was just-identified), so we can examine fit:

fitMeasures(fit_med_latent, c("chisq", "df", "pvalue",
                              "cfi", "tli",
                              "rmsea", "rmsea.ci.lower", "rmsea.ci.upper",
                              "srmr"))

##          chisq             df         pvalue            cfi            tli 
##         25.528         24.000          0.378          0.999          0.998 
##          rmsea rmsea.ci.lower rmsea.ci.upper           srmr 
##          0.014          0.000          0.047          0.028


If the fit indices meet the Hu and Bentler cutoffs, we have evidence that the model — both its measurement and structural parts — is consistent with the data.

title: Mediation — practical
text:
Drawing the mediation model

tidySEM::graph_sem(fit_med_latent, std = TRUE)




Path diagram for the latent-variable mediation model. Job control (con) is a latent factor measured by three parcels; positive affect (pa) and creativity (creat) are latent factors measured by three items each. The indirect effect of con on creat through pa is a * b.

title: Mediation — practical
text:
Writing up the results
An APA-style write-up of the latent SEM mediation:

Structural equation modelling was used to test whether positive affect (PA) mediated the relationship between job control and creativity. Job control was modelled as a latent factor measured by three parcels (p1, p2, p3); PA and creativity were modelled as latent factors measured by their three respective items. The structural paths were estimated with maximum likelihood and 1,000 bootstrap replications; bias-corrected and accelerated (BCa) confidence intervals are reported for the indirect effect.
The model fit the data well, \(\chi^2(24) = ...\), CFI = …, TLI = …, RMSEA = …, 90% CI […, …], SRMR = …. The indirect effect of job control on creativity through PA was \(ab = ...\), 95% BCa CI […, …], indicating significant mediation. The direct effect of job control on creativity was \(c' = ...\), \(p = ...\), suggesting [full/partial] mediation. The total effect was \(c = ...\), \(p = ...\). Positive affect thus [fully/partially] mediated the relationship between job control and creativity.

(Replace the ... with the actual values from the output above.)






NoteReporting bootstrap CIs



When you report a bootstrap CI, state:

The number of bootstrap replications (e.g. 1,000)
The type of CI (BCa is preferred)
The estimate and its CI
Whether the CI includes zero

Do not report a p-value for the indirect effect as your primary test — the bootstrap CI is the test. The p-value in the parameterEstimates() output is based on the bootstrap SE and is less accurate than the CI itself.

title: Mediation — practical
text:
Common errors






Warninglavaan WARNING: bootstrap only supported with estimator = ML



You requested se = "boot" but left estimator = "MLR" (or did not specify). Change to estimator = "ML". The point estimates are identical; only the standard errors change.

set.seed() not called
Without set.seed(), the bootstrap results will differ every time you run the model. Always set a seed before fitting a bootstrap model so your results are reproducible. We use set.seed(1234) throughout this series.


Very wide bootstrap CIs
If the bootstrap CI for the indirect effect is very wide, it may indicate a small sample, a weak indirect effect, or high variability in the bootstrap samples. Increasing the number of bootstrap replications (e.g. to 5,000) will narrow the CI slightly but will not fix a fundamentally weak effect.


Negative variance (Heywood case) in the latent SEM
If the latent SEM produces a Heywood case — a negative variance for one of the latent factors or indicators — check the measurement model’s fit on its own (fit a CFA first). A poorly-fitting measurement model can distort the structural estimates.

title: Mediation — practical
text:
Exercises

Fit the path mediation model from the tutorial (manifest variables, ML with se = "boot", bootstrap = 1000). Set set.seed(1234). Extract the BCa confidence interval for the indirect effect. Is the indirect effect significant? Is the mediation full or partial?
Fit the latent SEM mediation model from the tutorial. Compare the indirect effect estimate to the path mediation estimate from Exercise 1. Is the latent SEM estimate larger? Why?
Add a second mediator to the path mediation model: negative affect (neg). The model becomes: creat ~ pa + neg + con, pa ~ con, neg ~ con. Define two specific indirect effects (indirect_pa := a1 * b1 and indirect_neg := a2 * b2) and the total indirect effect. Fit with bootstrap. Which mediator shows a significant indirect effect?
Using the latent SEM mediation model from Exercise 2, write a complete APA-style results paragraph. Report the model fit, the indirect effect with its BCa CI, the direct effect, and the total effect. State whether the mediation is full or partial.







CautionSolutions





Exercise 1

set.seed(1234)
fit_ex1 <- sem(model_med_path, data = ps,
               estimator = "ML", se = "boot", bootstrap = 1000)
pe1 <- parameterEstimates(fit_ex1, boot.ci.type = "bca.simple")
pe1[pe1$lhs %in% c("indirect", "direct", "total"),
    c("lhs", "est", "ci.lower", "ci.upper")]

##        lhs    est ci.lower ci.upper
## 7 indirect  0.042    0.012    0.085
## 8   direct -0.091   -0.226    0.043
## 9    total -0.049   -0.195    0.096


Check whether the ci.lower and ci.upper for the indirect row exclude zero. If they do, the indirect effect is significant. Then check whether the direct row’s CI includes zero — if it does, the mediation is full; if not, it is partial.
Exercise 2

set.seed(1234)
fit_ex2 <- sem(model_med_latent, data = ps,
               estimator = "ML", se = "boot", bootstrap = 1000)
pe2 <- parameterEstimates(fit_ex2, boot.ci.type = "bca.simple")
pe2[pe2$lhs %in% c("indirect", "direct", "total"),
    c("lhs", "est", "ci.lower", "ci.upper")]

##         lhs    est ci.lower ci.upper
## 25 indirect  0.047    0.011    0.098
## 26   direct -0.102   -0.241    0.041
## 27    total -0.055   -0.208    0.091


The latent SEM indirect effect should be larger in absolute value than the path mediation indirect effect. This is disattenuation — the latent variables remove measurement error, producing an unbiased estimate of the mediated effect.
Exercise 3

ps$neg <- (ps$na1 + ps$na2 + ps$na3) / 3

model_2med <- '
  creat ~ b1 * pa + b2 * neg + c * con
  pa    ~ a1 * con
  neg   ~ a2 * con

  indirect_pa  := a1 * b1
  indirect_neg := a2 * b2
  total_indirect := (a1 * b1) + (a2 * b2)
  direct        := c
  total         := c + (a1 * b1) + (a2 * b2)
'

set.seed(1234)
fit_2med <- sem(model_2med, data = ps,
                estimator = "ML", se = "boot", bootstrap = 1000)
pe_2med <- parameterEstimates(fit_2med, boot.ci.type = "bca.simple")
pe_2med[pe_2med$lhs %in% c("indirect_pa", "indirect_neg",
                           "total_indirect", "direct", "total"),
        c("lhs", "est", "ci.lower", "ci.upper")]

##               lhs    est ci.lower ci.upper
## 10    indirect_pa  0.060    0.022    0.123
## 11   indirect_neg -0.064   -0.137   -0.018
## 12 total_indirect -0.004   -0.071    0.056
## 13         direct -0.055   -0.186    0.077
## 14          total -0.060   -0.204    0.079


Check the CIs for indirect_pa and indirect_neg. The mediator whose CI excludes zero shows a significant specific indirect effect.
Exercise 4
Using the output from Exercise 2, write the paragraph following the template in the “Writing up the results” section above. Replace each ... with the actual value from the parameterEstimates() and fitMeasures() output. State whether the indirect effect’s BCa CI excludes zero (significant mediation) and whether the direct effect is significant (full vs. partial mediation).

title: Mediation — practical
text:
Summary & next tutorial
You can now fit mediation models in lavaan — both path mediation with manifest variables and full latent-variable SEM mediation — using labelled paths and the := operator to define the indirect effect. You can extract BCa bootstrap confidence intervals and report the results in APA format. This completes the core lavaan toolkit: you can specify, estimate, and report CFA, path analysis, SEM, and mediation models. In the next tutorial we turn to the question of reporting — how to export lavaan results to publication-quality tables and figures, with a focus on the texreg package.

title: Mediation — practical
text:
Further reading

Hayes (2018) — the standard reference for mediation analysis, including bootstrap CIs.
Rosseel (2012) — the lavaan reference paper, covering defined parameters and bootstrap.
Kelloway (2015) — chapters 4 and 5 cover mediation in SEM.
Sobel (1990) — the original Sobel test paper, for historical context on why we bootstrap instead.

title: SEM with latent variables
text:
By the end of this tutorial you will be able to:

explain why modelling latent variables is preferable to modelling manifest scores
build a SEM that combines a measurement model (=~) with a structural model (~)
count degrees of freedom for a SEM by hand
compare path analysis estimates (manifest variables) with SEM estimates (latent variables) and understand disattenuation
fit a full latent-variable SEM with three factors and a mediation structure

title: SEM with latent variables
text:
By the end of this tutorial you will be able to:

explain why modelling latent variables is preferable to modelling manifest scores
build a SEM that combines a measurement model (=~) with a structural model (~)
count degrees of freedom for a SEM by hand
compare path analysis estimates (manifest variables) with SEM estimates (latent variables) and understand disattenuation
fit a full latent-variable SEM with three factors and a mediation structure

title: SEM with latent variables
text:
Setup


Setup code (you have seen this before)


library(haven)
library(lavaan)
library(tidySEM)

title: SEM with latent variables
text:
Why latent variables?
In Tutorial 09 we fitted a path model with manifest variables — mean scores computed from the items. For example, pa was the mean of pa1, pa2, pa3. This is simple, but it has a cost: the mean score contains measurement error.
Measurement error has two consequences for regression:

Attenuation. The regression coefficient of a variable measured with error is biased toward zero. If pa is an imperfect measure of the true positive-affect construct, the path from pa to creat will underestimate the true effect.
Bias in other paths. When multiple predictors are measured with different amounts of error, the relative sizes of their coefficients are distorted — the more reliable predictor looks relatively stronger.

Modelling the latent variable behind the items removes these biases. The latent variable is the error-free part of the construct; the residual variances of the individual items absorb the measurement error. This is the central advantage of SEM over path analysis with manifest variables.

The trade-off
The cost of modelling latent variables is model complexity. A latent factor with three indicators adds parameters (loadings, residual variances) and the model must be identified. In return, you get:

Unbiased estimates of the structural paths
A testable measurement model — you can check whether the items load well on their factors
More degrees of freedom, which makes the overall model testable

title: SEM with latent variables
section: From path analysis to SEM: parceled SEM
text:
From path analysis to SEM: parceled SEM
Our first SEM uses a parceled job-control factor (from Tutorial 08) alongside manifest pa and creat. This is a half-way house: one latent variable, two manifest variables. It is a common practical choice when you have many items for one construct but only three items for the others.

Building the parcels

ps$p1 <- (ps$con9 + ps$con10) / 2
ps$p2 <- (ps$con1 + ps$con8)  / 2
ps$p3 <- (ps$con3 + ps$con6)  / 2
ps$pa <- (ps$pa1 + ps$pa2 + ps$pa3) / 3
ps$creat <- (ps$creat1 + ps$creat2 + ps$creat3) / 3



The model

model_sem_parcel <- '
  # Measurement model for job control (latent)
  con =~ p1 + p2 + p3

  # Structural model
  creat ~ pa + con
  pa    ~ con
'

The # lines are comments inside the model string — lavaan ignores them. We use comments to separate the measurement and structural parts of the model, which makes the syntax easier to read as models grow.
We fit with sem():

fit_sem_parcel <- sem(model_sem_parcel, data = ps, estimator = "MLR")
summary(fit_sem_parcel, standardized = TRUE, fit.measures = TRUE, rsquare = TRUE)

##    .creat             0.689    0.047   14.712    0.000    0.689    0.943
##    .pa                1.146    0.073   15.757    0.000    1.146    0.976
##     con               0.472    0.062    7.603    0.000    1.000    1.000
## 
## R-Square:
##                    Estimate
##     p1                0.595
##     p2                0.635
##     p3                0.630
##     creat             0.057
##     pa                0.024




Counting degrees of freedom
With \(q = 5\) observed variables (p1, p2, p3, pa, creat), the covariance matrix has \(5 \times 6 / 2 = 15\) non-redundant elements.
Free parameters:

2 loadings on con (first fixed to 1 by marker-variable method)
2 regression paths in the creat equation (pa, con)
1 regression path in the pa equation (con)
3 residual variances of the parcels
1 residual variance of pa
1 residual variance of creat
1 latent variance of con

That is \(t = 11\). So \(\text{df} = 15 - 11 = 4\).


Reading the output
The output now has both a Latent variables section (the con =~ p1 + p2 + p3 loadings) and a Regressions section (the structural paths). Compare the structural paths to those from the path analysis in Tutorial 09:

The path from con to pa should be slightly larger in the SEM than in the path analysis — disattenuation removes the bias from con being measured with error.
The path from pa to creat should be similar — pa is still a manifest variable in this model.
The path from con to creat (direct effect) may also change.







NoteDisattenuation



When a predictor is measured with error, its regression coefficient is biased toward zero. Modelling the predictor as a latent variable (with the measurement error absorbed into the residual variances of its indicators) removes this bias. The latent-variable coefficient is typically larger in absolute value than the manifest-variable coefficient. This is called disattenuation — the coefficient has been “corrected for attenuation” due to measurement error.

title: SEM with latent variables
text:
Full latent-variable SEM
Now we make the full jump: all three constructs are latent variables, each measured by its own indicators.

The model

model_sem_full <- '
  # Measurement model
  con   =~ p1 + p2 + p3
  pa    =~ pa1 + pa2 + pa3
  creat =~ creat1 + creat2 + creat3

  # Structural model
  creat ~ pa + con
  pa    ~ con
'

We parcel only the job-control items (into p1, p2, p3) because there are six of them and we want a simpler measurement model. Positive affect and creativity are modelled at the item level with three indicators each.


Fitting the model

fit_sem_full <- sem(model_sem_full, data = ps, estimator = "MLR")
summary(fit_sem_full, standardized = TRUE, fit.measures = TRUE, rsquare = TRUE)

Counting degrees of freedom
With \(q = 9\) observed variables (p1, p2, p3, pa1, pa2, pa3, creat1, creat2, creat3), the covariance matrix has \(9 \times 10 / 2 = 45\) non-redundant elements.
Free parameters:

2 loadings on con + 2 loadings on pa + 2 loadings on creat = 6 loadings
2 regression paths in creat equation + 1 in pa equation = 3 structural paths
3 parcel residual variances + 3 pa residual variances + 3 creat residual variances = 9 indicator residual variances
1 latent variance of con + 1 residual variance of pa + 1 residual variance of creat = 3 latent (residual) variances

That is \(t = 6 + 3 + 9 + 3 = 21\). So \(\text{df} = 45 - 21 = 24\).


Fit indices

fitMeasures(fit_sem_full, c("chisq", "df", "pvalue",
                            "cfi", "tli",
                            "rmsea", "rmsea.ci.lower", "rmsea.ci.upper",
                            "srmr"))

##          chisq             df         pvalue            cfi            tli 
##         25.528         24.000          0.378          0.999          0.998 
##          rmsea rmsea.ci.lower rmsea.ci.upper           srmr 
##          0.014          0.000          0.047          0.028


The model has 24 degrees of freedom — plenty of information to test it. If the fit indices meet the Hu and Bentler cutoffs, the model’s structure is consistent with the data.

title: SEM with latent variables
section: Comparing path analysis, parceled SEM, and full SEM
text:
Comparing path analysis, parceled SEM, and full SEM
Let us compare the key structural paths across the three models:

# Path analysis (manifest variables) — from Tutorial 09
ps$con_m <- (ps$con1 + ps$con3 + ps$con6 + ps$con8 + ps$con9 + ps$con10) / 6
ps$pa_m  <- (ps$pa1 + ps$pa2 + ps$pa3) / 3
ps$cr_m  <- (ps$creat1 + ps$creat2 + ps$creat3) / 3

model_path_m <- '
  cr_m ~ pa_m + con_m
  pa_m ~ con_m
'
fit_path <- sem(model_path_m, data = ps, estimator = "MLR")

# Extract standardised structural paths from each model
get_paths <- function(fit) {
  pe <- parameterEstimates(fit, standardized = TRUE)
  pe[pe$op == "~", c("lhs", "rhs", "std.all")]
}

paths_path  <- get_paths(fit_path)
paths_parcel <- get_paths(fit_sem_parcel)
paths_full   <- get_paths(fit_sem_full)

paths_path

##    lhs   rhs std.all
## 1 cr_m  pa_m   0.235
## 2 cr_m con_m  -0.073
## 3 pa_m con_m   0.144

paths_parcel

##     lhs rhs std.all
## 4 creat  pa   0.237
## 5 creat con  -0.080
## 6    pa con   0.155

paths_full

##      lhs rhs std.all
## 10 creat  pa   0.264
## 11 creat con  -0.094
## 12    pa con   0.163


The comparison shows:

The path from con to pa is larger in the SEM models than in the path analysis — disattenuation corrects for the measurement error in job control.
The path from pa to creat is larger in the full SEM than in the parceled SEM or path analysis — disattenuation corrects for the measurement error in positive affect.
The direct path from con to creat may change in either direction.

This is the core argument for SEM over path analysis with manifest scores: the structural paths are unbiased estimates of the true relationships among the constructs, not the attenuated estimates you get from imperfectly-measured scores.

title: SEM with latent variables
text:
Drawing the full SEM

tidySEM::graph_sem(fit_sem_full, std = TRUE)




Path diagram for the full latent-variable SEM. Job control (con) is a latent factor measured by three parcels; positive affect (pa) and creativity (creat) are latent factors measured by three items each. Structural paths: con -> pa, con -> creat, pa -> creat.




The diagram shows both layers of the model:

Measurement layer: arrows from each latent factor (circle) to its indicators (squares)
Structural layer: arrows among the latent factors — con → pa, con → creat, pa → creat

title: SEM with latent variables
text:
Mathematical foundation (optional)






NoteThe matrix form of a full SEM





A full SEM combines a measurement model with a structural model. In matrix form:
Measurement model:
\[
\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{\Lambda} \mathbf{\eta} + \mathbf{\varepsilon}
\]
This is the same as the CFA matrix form from Tutorial 06 — observed variables (\(\mathbf{y}\)) are linear functions of latent variables (\(\mathbf{\eta}\)) plus measurement error (\(\mathbf{\varepsilon}\)).
Structural model:
\[
\mathbf{\eta} = \mathbf{B} \mathbf{\eta} + \mathbf{\zeta}
\tag{1}\]
Here \(\mathbf{B}\) is a matrix of structural coefficients (the regression paths among latent variables) and \(\mathbf{\zeta}\) is a vector of disturbances — the residual variances of the endogenous latent variables.
The model-implied covariance matrix combines both:
\[
\hat{\Sigma}(\theta) = \mathbf{\Lambda} (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{B})^{-1} \mathbf{\Psi} (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{B})^{-T} \mathbf{\Lambda}' + \mathbf{\Theta}
\tag{2}\]
You do not need to manipulate these matrices to use lavaan. They are included here so you recognise the notation when you encounter it in SEM papers. For the rest of this series we use the simplified lowercase Greek symbols (\(\lambda\), \(\beta\), \(\psi\), \(\theta\), \(\zeta\)).

title: SEM with latent variables
text:
Common errors






Warninglavaan WARNING: model has not converged in full SEM



A full SEM with many indicators and structural paths is more likely to have convergence problems than a simple CFA. Check for Heywood cases (negative variances), check that every factor has at least three indicators, and consider parceling some of the indicators to simplify the measurement model.

Very different estimates between path analysis and SEM
If the SEM estimates are dramatically different from the path analysis estimates, check whether the measurement model fits well on its own (fit a CFA first). A poorly-fitting measurement model can distort the structural estimates.


Negative residual variance for a latent dependent variable
If pa or creat has a negative disturbance variance (\(\zeta\)), the structural model may be misspecified. Consider whether the structural paths are theoretically justified, and whether there are omitted predictors that should be included.

title: SEM with latent variables
text:
Exercises

Fit the parceled SEM from the tutorial (job control as a latent factor measured by p1, p2, p3; pa and creat as manifest mean scores). Compare the standardised path from con to pa to the same path from the path analysis in Tutorial 09. Is the SEM estimate larger? Why?
Fit the full latent-variable SEM (all three constructs as latent factors). Write down the standardised structural paths and the \(R^2\) for creat. How much of the variance in creativity is explained by the model?
Count the degrees of freedom for the full SEM by hand (using the t-rule). Confirm that your count matches the df reported by fitMeasures().
Compare the parceled SEM and the full SEM using lavTestLRT(). Which model fits better? Is the more complex full SEM justified, or does the simpler parceled model fit as well?







CautionSolutions





Exercise 1

fitMeasures(fit_sem_parcel, c("chisq", "df", "pvalue", "cfi", "tli",
                              "rmsea", "srmr"))

##  chisq     df pvalue    cfi    tli  rmsea   srmr 
##  0.965  4.000  0.915  1.000  1.019  0.000  0.008

get_paths(fit_path)

##    lhs   rhs std.all
## 1 cr_m  pa_m   0.235
## 2 cr_m con_m  -0.073
## 3 pa_m con_m   0.144

get_paths(fit_sem_parcel)

##     lhs rhs std.all
## 4 creat  pa   0.237
## 5 creat con  -0.080
## 6    pa con   0.155


The con → pa path from the parceled SEM should be larger than the same path from the path analysis. This is disattenuation — the latent con factor is free of measurement error, so its coefficient is not biased toward zero.

summary(fit_sem_full, standardized = TRUE, rsquare = TRUE)
