The parcel loadings should be higher than most of the individual-item loadings because averaging two items reduces measurement error, making the parcel a more reliable indicator of the factor.
Exercise 2

model_2nd <- '
  PA     =~ pa1 + pa2 + pa3
  neg    =~ na1 + na2 + na3
  affect =~ PA + neg
'
fit_2nd <- cfa(model_2nd, data = ps, estimator = "MLR")
fitMeasures(fit_2nd, c("chisq", "df", "pvalue", "cfi", "tli",
                       "rmsea", "rmsea.ci.lower", "rmsea.ci.upper", "srmr"))

##          chisq             df         pvalue            cfi            tli 
##          9.870          7.000          0.196          0.997          0.994 
##          rmsea rmsea.ci.lower rmsea.ci.upper           srmr 
##          0.035          0.000          0.082          0.015


Compare these to the Tutorial 07 fit indices (\(\chi^2(8) = 7.93\), CFI = 1.00, RMSEA = 0.00, SRMR = 0.01). The second-order model should have slightly more degrees of freedom and very similar fit if the second-order structure is appropriate.
Exercise 3

fit_1st <- cfa("PA =~ pa1 + pa2 + pa3
               neg =~ na1 + na2 + na3
               PA ~~ neg", data = ps, estimator = "MLR")
lavTestLRT(fit_1st, fit_2nd)

## 
## Chi-Squared Difference Test
## 
##         Df    AIC    BIC Chisq Chisq diff RMSEA Df diff Pr(>Chisq)
## fit_2nd  7 5248.3 5301.4  9.87                                    
## fit_1st  8 5246.3 5295.6  9.87                0       1


A non-significant result means the second-order model fits as well as the first-order model — the more parsimonious second-order structure is supported.
Exercise 4
With \(q = 4\) parcels, the covariance matrix has \(4 \times 5 / 2 = 10\) elements. Free parameters: 3 loadings (first fixed to 1) + 1 latent variance + 4 residual variances = 8. So \(\text{df} = 10 - 8 = 2\). The model is overidentified — it can be tested against the data.

title: Parcels and second-order CFA
text:
Summary & next tutorial
You can now construct parcels from individual items and fit CFA models on parceled indicators, and you can specify and fit second-order factor models in lavaan. Both techniques appear frequently in published organisational psychology research, particularly in SEM models where the measurement model is not the primary focus. In the next tutorial we move from measurement models to structural models: we fit a path analysis with observed variables — a system of regression equations — and learn why a model with zero degrees of freedom fits perfectly.

title: Parcels and second-order CFA
text:
Further reading

Beaujean (2014) — covers parceling and second-order factors in detail, with lavaan code.
Kelloway (2015) — the textbook basis for the original Mplus teaching materials; discusses when to parcel and when not to.
Hoyle (2012) — the Handbook of Structural Equation Modeling includes chapters on parceling and hierarchical factor models.

title: CFA — concept
text:
By the end of this tutorial you will be able to:

distinguish latent and observed variables and explain why latent variables need a scale
identify the three kinds of parameter in a CFA — factor loadings (\(\lambda\)), latent (co)variances (\(\psi\)), and residual (co)variances (\(\theta\)) — and locate each in lavaan output
apply the t-rule to determine whether a model is identified
explain the marker-variable method and the std.lv = TRUE alternative for setting a latent variable’s scale
describe what a fit index measures and name the canonical set we will use throughout the series

title: CFA — concept
text:
By the end of this tutorial you will be able to:

distinguish latent and observed variables and explain why latent variables need a scale
identify the three kinds of parameter in a CFA — factor loadings (\(\lambda\)), latent (co)variances (\(\psi\)), and residual (co)variances (\(\theta\)) — and locate each in lavaan output
apply the t-rule to determine whether a model is identified
explain the marker-variable method and the std.lv = TRUE alternative for setting a latent variable’s scale
describe what a fit index measures and name the canonical set we will use throughout the series

title: CFA — concept
text:
Setup
This is a concept tutorial — we will fit one small model for illustration, but the focus is on the ideas. We load lavaan and the PS dataset in the usual way.

library(haven)
library(lavaan)

title: CFA — concept
text:
What is confirmatory factor analysis?
Confirmatory factor analysis (CFA) is a technique for testing whether a hypothesised factor structure fits the observed covariances among a set of items. You, the researcher, specify in advance which items load on which factors. CFA then estimates the parameters of that model and tells you how well the model-implied covariance matrix matches the observed one.
This is the “confirmatory” part: you commit to a structure before looking at the results, and you test it. This distinguishes CFA from exploratory factor analysis (EFA), where the algorithm freely searches for the best-factoring structure. In dissertation work, CFA is what you use when you have a theoretical reason to expect a particular factor structure and you want to test it.

A two-factor example
Throughout this and the next tutorial we work with a two-factor CFA on the six affect items in the PS dataset:

Positive affect (PA) measured by pa1, pa2, pa3
Negative affect (neg) measured by na1, na2, na3

The path diagram looks like this:




A two-factor CFA. Circles are latent factors (PA, neg); squares are observed indicators. Single-headed arrows from factors to indicators are factor loadings (λ). The double-headed arrow between the factors is their covariance (ψ). Small circles on each indicator are residual variances (θ).


We will fit this model in Tutorial 07. First, let us understand what each part of it means.

title: CFA — concept
text:
The parameters of a CFA
A CFA has three kinds of parameter. Every one of them appears in the lavaan summary() output, so it is worth knowing them by name and symbol.

Factor loadings (\(\lambda\))
A factor loading is the regression coefficient linking a latent variable to one of its indicators. It tells you how strongly the indicator reflects the underlying factor. Denoted \(\lambda\) (lambda).
In the diagram, the arrows from PA to pa1, pa2, pa3 are the three loadings of the PA factor. In lavaan syntax, loadings are specified with =~:

PA =~ pa1 + pa2 + pa3

In lavaan output, loadings appear in the Latent variables section. The Estimate column gives the unstandardised \(\lambda\); the std.all column gives the standardised loading — the correlation between the indicator and the factor.


Latent (co)variances (\(\psi\))
The variance of a latent variable — how much the factor varies across participants — is denoted \(\psi\) (psi). The covariance between two latent variables (e.g. between PA and neg) is also \(\psi\).
In the diagram, the double-headed arrow between PA and neg is their covariance. In lavaan syntax, covariances are specified with ~~:

PA ~~ neg

In lavaan output, latent variances and covariances appear in the Variances and Covariances sections respectively. The standardised covariance (std.all) is the correlation between the two factors — a number between -1 and 1.


Residual (co)variances (\(\theta\))
Every observed indicator has a residual variance — the portion of its variance not explained by the latent factor. This is measurement error (plus any item-specific systematic variance), denoted \(\theta\) (theta).
In the diagram, the small circles attached to each observed variable represent \(\theta\). lavaan estimates residual variances automatically for every indicator; you do not need to specify them explicitly. They appear in the Variances section of the output, listed under each indicator’s name.
If two indicators share variance that is not explained by their common factor — perhaps because they share a method or a wording similarity — you can model a residual covariance between them:

pa1 ~~ pa2

This adds a covariance between the residuals of pa1 and pa2. We will use this in Tutorial 07 when we examine modification indices.


Putting the parameters together
A two-factor CFA with three indicators per factor has:

6 factor loadings (\(\lambda\)) — but 2 are fixed to 1 by the marker-variable method, so 4 are freely estimated
2 latent variances (\(\psi\)) — one for PA, one for neg
1 latent covariance (\(\psi\)) — between PA and neg
6 residual variances (\(\theta\)) — one for each indicator

That is \(4 + 2 + 1 + 6 = 13\) free parameters. We will use this count in a moment when we check identification.

title: CFA — concept
section: Setting the scale of a latent variable
text:
Setting the scale of a latent variable
A latent variable is not directly observed, so it has no natural unit of measurement. We must give it one by fixing either a loading or its variance. There are two methods, and you met both in Tutorial 05.

Marker-variable method (lavaan’s default)
Fix one factor loading to 1. The latent variable then has the same scale as that indicator. The latent variance \(\psi\) is freely estimated. This is lavaan’s default — the first indicator listed after =~ has its loading fixed to 1.

PA =~ pa1 + pa2 + pa3     # pa1's loading is fixed to 1



Standardised latent variable
Fix the latent variance to 1. All loadings are freely estimated. The latent variable is in standard-deviation units. Requested with std.lv = TRUE:

cfa(model, data = ps, std.lv = TRUE)

Both methods give identical standardised solutions and identical fit indices. They differ only in how the unstandardised parameters are scaled. We use the marker-variable method throughout this series.

title: CFA — concept
text:
Identification
A model is identified if a unique solution exists for every free parameter. If a model is not identified, the parameter estimates are not meaningful — many different combinations of parameter values would produce the same model-implied covariance matrix, and lavaan cannot choose among them.

The t-rule
The first necessary condition for identification is the t-rule: the number of free parameters \(t\) must be less than or equal to the number of non-redundant elements in the observed covariance matrix.
The number of non-redundant elements in a covariance matrix with \(q\) observed variables is:
\[
q(q + 1) / 2
\]
The degrees of freedom of the model are then:
\[
\text{df} = \frac{q(q+1)}{2} - t
\tag{1}\]
For our two-factor CFA: \(q = 6\) observed variables, so \(q(q+1)/2 = 6 \times 7 / 2 = 21\). We have \(t = 13\) free parameters, so \(\text{df} = 21 - 13 = 8\).
A model with:

df < 0 is underidentified — cannot be estimated.
df = 0 is just-identified (saturated) — fits perfectly by construction, cannot be tested.
df > 0 is overidentified — can be empirically tested against the data.

Our two-factor CFA with 8 df is overidentified, which is what we want — it means the data can potentially reject the model.






NoteJust-identified models



The one-factor CFA with three indicators you fitted in Tutorial 05 had \(q = 3\), so \(q(q+1)/2 = 6\), and \(t = 6\) (2 loadings + 1 latent variance + 3 residual variances), giving \(\text{df} = 0\). That is why the fit was perfect — a just-identified model reproduces the observed covariance matrix exactly. You cannot test a just-identified model; you can only estimate its parameters.




Further conditions for identification
The t-rule is necessary but not sufficient. Two further conditions matter in practice:

Each latent variable has its scale set — by the marker-variable method or std.lv = TRUE. Without this, the latent variable’s units are undefined and the model is not identified.
Each factor has at least three indicators — a factor with two indicators is just-identified on its own (it fits perfectly), and a factor with one indicator is underidentified unless you fix its loading and residual variance to specific values. Three indicators per factor is the practical minimum for an overidentified factor.







WarningEmpirical underidentification



A model can be formally identified but still fail to converge because the data do not contain enough information to distinguish the parameters — for example, when two indicators are nearly identical (correlation near 1) or when a factor has very low variance. This is called empirical underidentification and it is a data problem, not a model problem. lavaan will warn you that the model has not converged.

title: CFA — concept
text:
Model fit
Once a model is identified and estimated, we ask: how well does it reproduce the observed covariance matrix? This is the question of model fit.
The fundamental idea is that the model implies a particular covariance matrix \(\hat{\Sigma}(\theta)\) — the covariances among the observed variables that the model predicts given its parameter estimates. We compare this to the observed sample covariance matrix \(S\). If \(\hat{\Sigma}(\theta)\) is close to \(S\), the model fits well.

The chi-square test
The classic test is the model \(\chi^2\), which tests the null hypothesis:
\[
H_0: S = \hat{\Sigma}(\theta)
\]
A non-significant \(\chi^2\) (e.g. \(p > .05\)) means we cannot reject the model — it fits. A significant \(\chi^2\) means the model is rejected — the implied and observed covariances differ more than we would expect by chance.
The problem with \(\chi^2\) is that it is sensitive to sample size. With a large sample, even a trivially misspecified model will be rejected. With a small sample, even a badly misspecified model will not be. We therefore supplement \(\chi^2\) with approximate fit indices.


The canonical fit indices
Following Hu and Bentler (1999), we report at least two indices throughout this series: one badness-of-fit index (RMSEA or SRMR) and one goodness-of-fit index (CFI or TLI). The canonical set:









Index
Type
Threshold
Interpretation




\(\chi^2\)
Exact
\(p > .05\)
Good fit, but sensitive to sample size


RMSEA
Badness
\(< .08\) acceptable, \(< .05\) close
Lower is better; reported with 90% CI


CFI
Goodness
\(> .95\) good, \(> .90\) acceptable
Higher is better


TLI
Goodness
\(> .95\) good, \(> .90\) acceptable
Higher is better; penalises complexity


SRMR
Badness
\(< .08\)
Lower is better; sensitive to misspecification



To extract all five in one call:

fitMeasures(fit, c("chisq", "df", "pvalue",
                   "cfi", "tli",
                   "rmsea", "rmsea.ci.lower", "rmsea.ci.upper",
                   "srmr"))

We will see these in action in Tutorial 07, when we fit the two-factor CFA on the PS dataset and read the full output.






NoteFit is not variance accounted for



A common mistake is to interpret fit indices as “the model explains X% of the variance”. They are not. Fit indices measure how closely the model-implied covariance matrix matches the observed one — they say nothing directly about variance explained. The variance-explained analogue in SEM is \(R^2\) for each indicator, which we will meet in Tutorial 07.

title: CFA — concept
text:
A quick illustration
Let us fit the two-factor CFA on the PS dataset so the concepts above have a concrete referent. We will read the full output in Tutorial 07; here we just confirm that the model is identified and that it fits.

model_2f <- '
  PA  =~ pa1 + pa2 + pa3
  neg =~ na1 + na2 + na3
  PA  ~~ neg
'

fit_2f <- cfa(model_2f, data = ps, estimator = "MLR")
fitMeasures(fit_2f, c("chisq", "df", "pvalue",
                      "cfi", "tli",
                      "rmsea", "rmsea.ci.lower", "rmsea.ci.upper",
                      "srmr"))

##          chisq             df         pvalue            cfi            tli 
##          9.870          8.000          0.274          0.998          0.996 
##          rmsea rmsea.ci.lower rmsea.ci.upper           srmr 
##          0.027          0.000          0.073          0.015


With \(\text{df} = 8\) (exactly as we calculated above) and \(\chi^2\) non-significant, CFI = TLI = 1.000, RMSEA = 0.000, and SRMR = 0.014, the model fits very well. We will unpack every number in Tutorial 07.

title: CFA — concept
text:
Mathematical foundation (optional)






NoteThe matrix form of a CFA





For those who want to see the underlying mathematics, a CFA can be written in matrix form. The measurement model is:
\[
\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{\Lambda} \mathbf{\eta} + \mathbf{\varepsilon}
\tag{2}\]
where:

\(\mathbf{y}\) is a vector of observed variables
\(\mathbf{\Lambda}\) is a matrix of factor loadings (\(\lambda\))
\(\mathbf{\eta}\) is a vector of latent variables
\(\mathbf{\varepsilon}\) is a vector of residuals (\(\theta\))

The model-implied covariance matrix is then:
\[
\hat{\Sigma}(\theta) = \mathbf{\Lambda} \mathbf{\Psi} \mathbf{\Lambda}' + \mathbf{\Theta}
\tag{3}\]
where \(\mathbf{\Psi}\) is the covariance matrix of the latent variables and \(\mathbf{\Theta}\) is the covariance matrix of the residuals.
You do not need to manipulate these matrices to use lavaan — the package does it for you. But you will see this notation in SEM papers (e.g. Bollen (1989); Hoyle (2012)), and recognising it helps you read the literature. For the rest of this series we use the simplified lowercase Greek symbols (\(\lambda\), \(\psi\), \(\theta\)) rather than the bold matrix form.

title: CFA — concept
text:
Common errors






WarningNegative degrees of freedom



lavaan WARNING: model is underidentified — you have more free parameters than the data can support. The remedy is to fix or constrain parameters (e.g. remove a factor, fix a loading to zero, constrain two loadings equal), not to collect more data.

A factor with two indicators
A factor with only two indicators is just-identified on its own — it will fit perfectly but contributes no degrees of freedom to the overall model. If you must have a two-indicator factor, consider parcelling (Tutorial 08) or constraining the two loadings equal.


Forgetting to set the scale
If you turn off the marker-variable default (auto.fix.first = FALSE) without using std.lv = TRUE, your latent variable has no scale and the model is not identified. Always use one of the two methods.

title: CFA — concept
text:
Exercises

Consider a one-factor CFA with four indicators. How many non-redundant elements are in the covariance matrix? How many free parameters does the model have (using the marker-variable method)? What are the degrees of freedom? Is the model just-identified or overidentified?
Consider a two-factor CFA where factor 1 has two indicators and factor 2 has three indicators, and the two factors are allowed to correlate. How many degrees of freedom does this model have? Is it identified?
You fit a CFA and get \(\chi^2(12) = 28.45\), \(p = .005\), CFI = .94, TLI = .92, RMSEA = .07 (90% CI [.04, .10]), SRMR = .06. Using the Hu and Bentler cutoffs from the table above, write a one-sentence summary of the model’s fit. Which indices suggest acceptable fit, and which suggest the model should be rejected?
A researcher fits a CFA with a single factor and two indicators. They report “excellent fit” with \(\chi^2 = 0\), df = 0, CFI = 1, RMSEA = 0. Why is this report misleading?







CautionSolutions





Exercise 1
With \(q = 4\) indicators, the covariance matrix has \(q(q+1)/2 = 4 \times 5 / 2 = 10\) non-redundant elements. The free parameters are: 3 loadings (the first is fixed to 1 by the marker-variable method) + 1 latent variance + 4 residual variances = 8. So \(\text{df} = 10 - 8 = 2\). The model is overidentified — it can be tested against the data.
Exercise 2
With \(q = 5\) observed variables, the covariance matrix has \(5 \times 6 / 2 = 15\) elements. The free parameters are: 1 loading on factor 1 (one of two is fixed to 1) + 2 loadings on factor 2 (one of three is fixed to 1) + 2 latent variances + 1 latent covariance + 5 residual variances = 11. So \(\text{df} = 15 - 11 = 4\). The model is overidentified as a whole, but factor 1 with only two indicators is just-identified on its own — it contributes no df. This is a fragile model; if you can add a third indicator to factor 1, do so.
Exercise 3
The \(\chi^2\) is significant (\(p = .005 < .05\)), which would normally reject the model. However, CFI (.94) and TLI (.92) are at or above the .90 acceptable threshold, SRMR (.06) is below .08, and RMSEA (.07) is below .08 with its lower CI bound at .04. A reasonable summary: “The model showed acceptable fit on the approximate indices (CFI = .94, TLI = .92, RMSEA = .07, SRMR = .06), although the significant \(\chi^2(12) = 28.45\), \(p = .005\) suggests some model misspecification.” The \(\chi^2\) and the upper bound of the RMSEA CI (.10) are the indices suggesting rejection; the rest suggest acceptable fit.
Exercise 4
A one-factor model with two indicators has \(q = 2\), so \(q(q+1)/2 = 3\), and \(t = 3\) (1 loading + 1 latent variance + 2 residual variances), giving \(\text{df} = 0\). The model is just-identified — it fits perfectly by construction, not because it is a good model. Reporting “excellent fit” for a just-identified model is misleading because the fit is guaranteed; the model has not been tested against the data at all. Any two-indicator one-factor CFA will give \(\chi^2 = 0\), CFI = 1, RMSEA = 0 regardless of how well or badly the items measure the factor.

title: CFA — concept
text:
Summary & next tutorial
You now understand the building blocks of CFA: latent variables measured by indicators, three kinds of parameter (\(\lambda\), \(\psi\), \(\theta\)), the t-rule for identification, the two methods for setting a latent variable’s scale, and the canonical set of fit indices. In the next tutorial we put all of this to work on the PS dataset — fitting the two-factor CFA on positive and negative affect, reading the full summary() output, extracting fit indices, examining modification indices, and interpreting the results in APA format.

title: CFA — concept
text:
Further reading

Bollen (1989) — the canonical reference for SEM identification and the matrix form of the measurement model.
Beaujean (2014) — a modern, lavaan-based introduction to latent variable modelling; the notation conventions in this series follow Beaujean.
Hoyle (2012) — the Handbook of Structural Equation Modeling, a comprehensive reference for every aspect of SEM.
Hu & Bentler (1999) — the source of the fit-index cutoff criteria we use throughout this series.

title: Simple inference: t-test, ANOVA, lm
text:
By the end of this tutorial you will be able to:

run one-sample, independent-samples, and paired t-tests in R and interpret the output
run a one-way ANOVA with aov() and follow up with pairwise comparisons using TukeyHSD()
fit simple and multiple regression models with lm() and read the summary() output
produce your first ggplot2 scatter plot with a fitted regression line
check regression assumptions with the base plot(fit) diagnostic panels

title: Simple inference: t-test, ANOVA, lm
text:
By the end of this tutorial you will be able to:

run one-sample, independent-samples, and paired t-tests in R and interpret the output
run a one-way ANOVA with aov() and follow up with pairwise comparisons using TukeyHSD()
fit simple and multiple regression models with lm() and read the summary() output
produce your first ggplot2 scatter plot with a fitted regression line
check regression assumptions with the base plot(fit) diagnostic panels

title: Simple inference: t-test, ANOVA, lm
text:
Setup
This tutorial uses the PS dataset we imported in Tutorial 03, plus ggplot2 for the first time. We also load psych for descriptive statistics. The setup chunk sets the knit root directory to the project folder so the data/ paths work.

library(haven)
library(ggplot2)
library(psych)

We import the PS dataset from its SPSS file, strip the labels, convert -99 to NA, and turn gender and age into factors — the same workflow you met in Tutorial 03. For the rest of this tutorial we use two derived scores as our running examples:

# Mean positive affect score per participant
ps$pa <- (ps$pa1 + ps$pa2 + ps$pa3) / 3
# Mean creativity score per participant
ps$creat <- (ps$creat1 + ps$creat2 + ps$creat3) / 3
# Mean job control score per participant
ps$con <- (ps$con1 + ps$con3 + ps$con6 + ps$con8 + ps$con9 + ps$con10) / 6

Each is the average of the items that make up the scale, which gives us a single number per participant for that construct. We will use these throughout the tutorial.

title: Simple inference: t-test, ANOVA, lm
text:
t-tests
A t-test compares the means of two groups (or one group against a reference value) and asks whether the difference is statistically significant. R’s t.test() function handles all three flavours.

One-sample t-test
The one-sample t-test compares the mean of a single sample against a hypothesised population mean. Is the mean positive affect score in our sample different from the scale midpoint of 3.5?

t.test(ps$pa, mu = 3.5)

## 
##  One Sample t-test
## 
## data:  ps$pa
## t = -0.16857, df = 331, p-value = 0.8662
## alternative hypothesis: true mean is not equal to 3.5
## 95 percent confidence interval:
##  3.372792 3.607127
## sample estimates:
## mean of x 
##   3.48996


The output gives us the observed mean (mean of x), the t-statistic, degrees of freedom, p-value, the 95% confidence interval for the mean, and a reminder of the hypothesised mu. Read it as: t(r) = …, p = …, with the 95% CI for the mean.


Independent-samples t-test
The independent-samples t-test compares the means of two unrelated groups. Do male and female participants differ in their mean positive affect? We pass a formula — pa ~ gender — which R reads as “model pa as a function of gender”.

t.test(pa ~ gender, data = ps, var.equal = FALSE)

## 
##  Welch Two Sample t-test
## 
## data:  pa by gender
## t = 2.6522, df = 310.12, p-value = 0.008408
## alternative hypothesis: true difference in means between group Male and group Female is not equal to 0
## 95 percent confidence interval:
##  0.08165575 0.55105985
## sample estimates:
##   mean in group Male mean in group Female 
##             3.636704             3.320346


var.equal = FALSE requests Welch’s t-test, which does not assume equal variances in the two groups. This is the safer default and is what R uses unless you say otherwise. Setting var.equal = TRUE gives the classical Student’s t-test, which assumes equal variances — only use this if you have checked the assumption with var.test() or a Levene’s test.






Note



The formula interface y ~ group is the same syntax you will use throughout R for regression, ANOVA, and (from Tutorial 05 onward) lavaan models. Get comfortable with the tilde ~ now — it means “is modelled by” or “depends on”.




Paired t-test
The paired t-test compares two measurements from the same participants — for example, pre- and post-intervention scores. The PS dataset is cross-sectional, so we do not have a natural paired example, but the syntax is:

t.test(pre_scores, post_scores, paired = TRUE)

The paired = TRUE argument tells R to compute the difference score for each participant and test whether the mean difference is zero.

title: Simple inference: t-test, ANOVA, lm
text:
One-way ANOVA
Analysis of variance (ANOVA) generalises the t-test to three or more groups. We test whether mean positive affect differs across the five age bands in the PS dataset.

Fitting the model

fit_aov <- aov(pa ~ age, data = ps)
summary(fit_aov)

##              Df Sum Sq Mean Sq F value Pr(>F)
## age           4    8.1   2.017   1.735  0.142
## Residuals   325  377.8   1.162               
## 13 observations deleted due to missingness


The summary() output shows two rows: age (the between-groups effect) and Residuals (the within-groups variation). The F-statistic tests whether the group means are all equal. The p-value tells you whether to reject that null hypothesis.


Pairwise comparisons
A significant ANOVA tells you that some group differences exist, but not which groups differ. Follow up with Tukey’s Honest Significant Difference test, which performs all pairwise comparisons while controlling the family-wise error rate:

TukeyHSD(fit_aov)

##   Tukey multiple comparisons of means
##     95% family-wise confidence level
## 
## Fit: aov(formula = pa ~ age, data = ps)
## 
## $age
##            diff        lwr        upr     p adj
## 2-1 -0.45873016 -1.1945650 0.27710467 0.4291283
## 3-1 -0.50836551 -1.2121410 0.19540999 0.2773531
## 4-1 -0.62160778 -1.3361364 0.09292082 0.1216173
## 5-1 -0.70158730 -1.5179228 0.11474815 0.1298360
## 3-2 -0.04963535 -0.5010203 0.40174962 0.9981919
## 4-2 -0.16287762 -0.6308515 0.30509626 0.8749829
## 5-2 -0.24285714 -0.8551087 0.36939445 0.8126295
## 4-3 -0.11324227 -0.5289905 0.30250598 0.9451098
## 5-3 -0.19322179 -0.7665446 0.38010103 0.8873041
## 5-4 -0.07997952 -0.6664522 0.50649318 0.9958196


The output lists every pairwise comparison, the difference in means, the lower and upper bounds of the 95% confidence interval, and the adjusted p-value (p adj). A comparison is significant at the .05 level if p adj is below .05.






WarningANOVA requires a factor



If your grouping variable is numeric (e.g. age coded 1, 2, 3, 4, 5), aov() will treat it as a continuous predictor and run a regression instead of an ANOVA. Always confirm that the grouping variable is a factor with class(ps$age) before fitting the model. We converted age to a factor in the setup chunk above for exactly this reason.




Two-way ANOVA
To test the effects of two grouping variables and their interaction, list both on the right-hand side of the formula separated by *:

fit_2way <- aov(pa ~ gender * age, data = ps)
summary(fit_2way)

The * expands to gender + age + gender:age, where the colon denotes the interaction term. We will not pursue two-way ANOVA further here; the principle is the same as one-way, with one extra row in the summary table per additional effect.

title: Simple inference: t-test, ANOVA, lm
text:
Simple linear regression with lm()
Regression models a continuous outcome as a linear function of one or more predictors. The workhorse function is lm() — “linear model”.

Fitting the model
Does positive affect predict creativity? We fit the regression of creat on pa:

fit_lm <- lm(creat ~ pa, data = ps)
summary(fit_lm)

## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = creat ~ pa, data = ps)
## 
## Residuals:
##      Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
## -2.01806 -0.60540  0.00231  0.57600  2.00231 
## 
## Coefficients:
##             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)  2.46712    0.15462   15.96  < 2e-16 ***
## pa           0.17685    0.04231    4.18 3.74e-05 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.8355 on 330 degrees of freedom
##   (11 observations deleted due to missingness)
## Multiple R-squared:  0.05028,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.0474 
## F-statistic: 17.47 on 1 and 330 DF,  p-value: 3.743e-05


The summary() output for an lm object has four sections worth understanding:

Call — the model formula, repeated for reference.
Residuals — a five-number summary of the differences between observed and predicted values. Symmetric residuals (median near zero) are a good sign.
Coefficients — a table with one row per parameter. The (Intercept) row is the predicted creat when pa is zero; the pa row is the slope — the change in creat for a one-unit increase in pa. The columns are the estimate, its standard error, the t-statistic, and the two-tailed p-value.
Model fit — Multiple R-squared is the proportion of variance in creat explained by pa. Adjusted R-squared corrects for the number of predictors. The F-statistic tests the overall model — whether the slope is significantly different from zero.



Confidence intervals for coefficients

confint(fit_lm)

##                  2.5 %    97.5 %
## (Intercept) 2.16294819 2.7712944
## pa          0.09361827 0.2600913


This gives the 95% confidence interval for each coefficient. If the interval for the slope does not include zero, the effect is statistically significant at the .05 level — equivalent to the p-value in the summary() output being below .05.


Plotting the regression with ggplot2
This is our first ggplot2 plot. The grammar takes getting used to, but the payoff is publication-quality figures with consistent styling.

ggplot(ps, aes(x = pa, y = creat)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, colour = "blue") +
  labs(x = "Positive affect (mean of 3 items)",
       y = "Creativity (mean of 3 items)",
       title = "Creativity as a function of positive affect") +
  theme_minimal()








Reading the code:

ggplot(ps, aes(x = pa, y = creat)) — initialise the plot with the data frame and the aesthetic mapping (which variable goes on which axis).
geom_point(alpha = 0.5) — add a scatter of points; alpha = 0.5 makes them semi-transparent so overlapping points are visible.
geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE) — add a fitted regression line; se = TRUE shades the 95% confidence band around the line.
labs(...) — label the axes and title.
theme_minimal() — a clean white background with subtle gridlines.







NoteThe ggplot2 mental model



A ggplot2 figure is built up in layers connected by +. You always start with ggplot(data, aes(...)), then add geoms (point, line, smooth, bar, boxplot), then scales, labels, and a theme. Every ggplot2 plot in this series follows this same layer-by-layer pattern. We will use ggplot2 for all plots from this tutorial onward.




Residual diagnostics
The base plot(fit_lm) call produces four diagnostic plots. We display them in a 2 × 2 grid with par(mfrow = c(2, 2)) — base R graphics, which we use here because these diagnostic plots come straight from lm itself.

par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
plot(fit_lm)







par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

The four panels are:

Residuals vs Fitted — checks linearity. A roughly horizontal red line with no clear pattern is what you want. A curved line suggests a non-linear relationship.
Normal Q–Q — checks normality of residuals. Points should lie close to the diagonal dashed line. Systematic departure from the line at the tails indicates non-normal residuals.
Scale–Location — checks homoscedasticity (equal variance). A roughly horizontal line with evenly spread points is what you want. A funnel shape indicates heteroscedasticity.
Residuals vs Leverage — flags influential observations. Points with high leverage and large residuals (top-right corner) are worth inspecting; they may disproportionately influence the fitted line.







TipTry it



Fit a simple regression of creat on con (job control as predictor of creativity). Is the slope significant? What is the R²? Produce a ggplot2 scatter with the fitted line.








CautionSolution






fit_con <- lm(creat ~ con, data = ps)
summary(fit_con)

## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = creat ~ con, data = ps)
## 
## Residuals:
##      Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
## -2.06211 -0.70988 -0.07051  0.61085  1.97988 
## 
## Coefficients:
##             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)  3.26370    0.25919  12.592   <2e-16 ***
## con         -0.05040    0.06778  -0.744    0.458    
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.8562 on 340 degrees of freedom
##   (1 observation deleted due to missingness)
## Multiple R-squared:  0.001623,   Adjusted R-squared:  -0.001313 
## F-statistic: 0.5529 on 1 and 340 DF,  p-value: 0.4577

ggplot(ps, aes(x = con, y = creat)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", colour = "blue") +
  labs(x = "Job control (mean of 6 items)",
       y = "Creativity (mean of 3 items)") +
  theme_minimal()








The slope of con and its p-value appear in the coefficients table of the summary() output. The multiple R-squared is reported further down in the same summary.

title: Simple inference: t-test, ANOVA, lm
text:
Multiple regression
Multiple regression extends lm() to two or more predictors. The formula uses + to add predictors:

fit_multi <- lm(creat ~ pa + con, data = ps)
summary(fit_multi)

## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = creat ~ pa + con, data = ps)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -1.9628 -0.6282  0.0228  0.5525  1.8611 
## 
## Coefficients:
##             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)  2.78140    0.27995   9.935  < 2e-16 ***
## pa           0.18514    0.04271   4.335 1.94e-05 ***
## con         -0.09132    0.06785  -1.346    0.179    
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.8344 on 329 degrees of freedom
##   (11 observations deleted due to missingness)
## Multiple R-squared:  0.05548,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.04974 
## F-statistic: 9.662 on 2 and 329 DF,  p-value: 8.363e-05


Each coefficient is now interpreted as the effect of that predictor holding the other predictors constant. The slope for pa is the change in creat for a one-unit increase in pa, with con held constant — and vice versa.
Compare the slope of pa in this multiple regression to its slope in the simple regression above. It will usually be different (often smaller) because some of the variance pa shared with con has been statistically controlled.

Confidence intervals

confint(fit_multi)

##                  2.5 %    97.5 %
## (Intercept)  2.2306774 3.3321313
## pa           0.1011256 0.2691498
## con         -0.2247964 0.0421528




Comparing nested models
To test whether adding a predictor significantly improves the model fit, use anova() to compare the nested models:

anova(fit_lm, fit_multi)

## Analysis of Variance Table
## 
## Model 1: creat ~ pa
## Model 2: creat ~ pa + con
##   Res.Df    RSS Df Sum of Sq      F Pr(>F)
## 1    330 230.33                           
## 2    329 229.07  1    1.2613 1.8115 0.1792


The output tests the null hypothesis that the additional predictor’s coefficient is zero. A significant p-value means adding con to a model that already contains pa significantly improves the fit.






Note



This anova() call is different from the aov() we used earlier. Here, anova() takes two (or more) fitted lm objects and performs a likelihood-ratio test between them. With aov(), you fit a single ANOVA model and summary() partitions the variance.

title: Simple inference: t-test, ANOVA, lm
text:
From regression to path analysis
The multiple regression model creat ~ pa + con is, in fact, a tiny path model — a system of regression equations with multiple predictors of a single outcome. In Tutorial 09 we will extend this idea to a model with multiple outcomes and multiple equations, fitted with lavaan::sem() instead of lm(). The jump from lm() to sem() is smaller than it looks: the model syntax changes from a formula to a string, but the underlying idea — modelling an outcome as a linear function of predictors — is identical.
Similarly, the regression of creat on pa is the simplest possible building block of the structural models we will fit in Tutorials 10 and 12. Keep the structure of summary(fit_lm) in mind: the coefficient table, the R², and the F-test all reappear in lavaan output, just in slightly different formatting.

title: Simple inference: t-test, ANOVA, lm
text:
Common errors






Warningnot all arguments have the same length



You passed vectors of different lengths to t.test() — usually because one vector has NAs that the other does not, or because you subsetted one but not the other. Use the formula interface (y ~ group, data = df) instead of passing separate vectors; R then handles missing data consistently.

contrasts can be applied only to factors with 2 or more levels
You tried to run an ANOVA with a grouping variable that has only one level — perhaps because you accidentally filtered the data down to a single group first. Check table(ps$age) before fitting the model.


NA coefficients in lm() output
A coefficient shows as NA in the summary() table when the predictor is a linear combination of other predictors (perfect multicollinearity). This happens most often when you include a categorical predictor as both a numeric variable and a factor in the same model.

title: Simple inference: t-test, ANOVA, lm
text:
Exercises

Run an independent-samples t-test comparing male and female participants on mean creativity (creat). Report the t-statistic, degrees of freedom, p-value, and 95% CI in APA format. Use var.equal = FALSE (Welch’s test).
Run a one-way ANOVA testing whether mean job control (con) differs across the five age bands. Is the overall F-test significant? If so, run TukeyHSD() and report which pairwise comparisons are significant at the .05 level.
Fit a multiple regression model predicting creat from pa, con, and age (as a factor). Interpret each coefficient. Use confint() to get the 95% CIs. Which predictors are statistically significant?
Use ggplot2 to produce a scatter plot of con (x-axis) against creat (y-axis), with points coloured by gender. Add a separate fitted regression line for each gender using geom_smooth(method = "lm", aes(colour = gender)).







CautionSolutions





Exercise 1

t.test(creat ~ gender, data = ps, var.equal = FALSE)

## 
##  Welch Two Sample t-test
## 
## data:  creat by gender
## t = 0.29736, df = 320.07, p-value = 0.7664
## alternative hypothesis: true difference in means between group Male and group Female is not equal to 0
## 95 percent confidence interval:
##  -0.1578933  0.2141217
## sample estimates:
##   mean in group Male mean in group Female 
##             3.097378             3.069264


Read the t-statistic, df, p-value, and 95% CI from the output. In APA format: t(df) = value, p = value, 95% CI [lower, upper].
Exercise 2

fit_age <- aov(con ~ age, data = ps)
summary(fit_age)

##              Df Sum Sq Mean Sq F value Pr(>F)
## age           4   2.67  0.6679   1.443  0.219
## Residuals   325 150.39  0.4628               
## 13 observations deleted due to missingness

TukeyHSD(fit_age)

##   Tukey multiple comparisons of means
##     95% family-wise confidence level
## 
## Fit: aov(formula = con ~ age, data = ps)
## 
## $age
##           diff        lwr       upr     p adj
## 2-1 0.06190476 -0.4023827 0.5261922 0.9961691
## 3-1 0.12998713 -0.3140720 0.5740462 0.9295799
## 4-1 0.24526370 -0.2055802 0.6961076 0.5682286
## 5-1 0.30000000 -0.2150807 0.8150807 0.4999212
## 3-2 0.06808237 -0.2167266 0.3528914 0.9654838
## 4-2 0.18335893 -0.1119171 0.4786350 0.4332485
## 5-2 0.23809524 -0.1482153 0.6244058 0.4411274
## 4-3 0.11527657 -0.1470468 0.3776000 0.7482399
## 5-3 0.17001287 -0.1917349 0.5317606 0.6980693
## 5-4 0.05473630 -0.3153086 0.4247812 0.9942803


The summary() output gives the F-test for the overall effect of age on job control. If Pr(>F) is below .05, the omnibus test is significant. TukeyHSD() then lists all pairwise comparisons; significant differences are those with p adj below .05.
Exercise 3

fit_full <- lm(creat ~ pa + con + age, data = ps)
summary(fit_full)

## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = creat ~ pa + con + age, data = ps)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -2.0302 -0.5468  0.0386  0.5961  1.7806 
## 
## Coefficients:
##             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)  3.00638    0.32060   9.377  < 2e-16 ***
## pa           0.17090    0.04207   4.062 6.12e-05 ***
## con         -0.06463    0.06668  -0.969 0.333175    
## age2        -0.20649    0.20139  -1.025 0.305974    
## age3        -0.10678    0.19318  -0.553 0.580806    
## age4        -0.42067    0.19736  -2.132 0.033802 *  
## age5        -0.79722    0.22557  -3.534 0.000469 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.8053 on 323 degrees of freedom
##   (13 observations deleted due to missingness)
## Multiple R-squared:  0.1296, Adjusted R-squared:  0.1134 
## F-statistic: 8.012 on 6 and 323 DF,  p-value: 4.49e-08

confint(fit_full)

##                   2.5 %      97.5 %
## (Intercept)  2.37565348  3.63710502
## pa           0.08811995  0.25367033
## con         -0.19581637  0.06655931
## age2        -0.60269825  0.18971160
## age3        -0.48682286  0.27326059
## age4        -0.80894708 -0.03240084
## age5        -1.24099429 -0.35344875


Each coefficient’s significance is judged by its p-value in the summary() output (or, equivalently, whether its 95% CI in confint() excludes zero). The pa and con slopes are interpreted as the change in creat for a one-unit increase in that predictor, holding the others constant. The age coefficients are differences relative to the reference level (the first level of the factor, here age band 1).
Exercise 4

ggplot(ps, aes(x = con, y = creat, colour = gender)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE) +
  labs(x = "Job control (mean of 6 items)",
       y = "Creativity (mean of 3 items)",
       colour = "Gender") +
  theme_minimal()








The colour = gender aesthetic inside ggplot() maps gender to point colour; passing the same aesthetic to geom_smooth() makes it fit a separate line for each gender.

title: Simple inference: t-test, ANOVA, lm
text:
Summary & next tutorial
You can now run t-tests, one-way ANOVA, and regression with lm(), read the summary() output, and produce your first ggplot2 figures. The regression framework you have met here — a formula y ~ x, a fitted object, a summary() call — is the direct ancestor of the lavaan models we will fit from Tutorial 05 onward. In the next tutorial we make the jump: we introduce lavaan’s model syntax (=~, ~, ~~, :=) and the three functions (cfa(), sem(), lavaan()) that you will use for the rest of this series.

title: Simple inference: t-test, ANOVA, lm
text:
Further reading

Dalgaard (2008) — Chapters 4 and 6 cover t-tests, ANOVA, and regression with worked R examples.
R Core Team (2024) — the official R manuals, including An Introduction to R, which has concise chapters on each test covered here.
Wickham (2019) — for the deeper theory of R’s formula interface, which underpins lm(), aov(), and (from Tutorial 05) lavaan.

title: RStudio
text:
By the end of this tutorial you will be able to:

navigate the four panes of the RStudio IDE and know what each is for
create an RStudio Project (.Rproj) and understand why projects matter for reproducibility
write and run code from a script file versus a Quarto (.qmd) document
install and load the packages you will need for the rest of this tutorial series
use the built-in help system to look up function documentation
configure RStudio’s most important Global Options for a clean, reproducible workflow

title: RStudio
text:
By the end of this tutorial you will be able to:

navigate the four panes of the RStudio IDE and know what each is for
create an RStudio Project (.Rproj) and understand why projects matter for reproducibility
write and run code from a script file versus a Quarto (.qmd) document
install and load the packages you will need for the rest of this tutorial series
use the built-in help system to look up function documentation
configure RStudio’s most important Global Options for a clean, reproducible workflow

title: RStudio
text:
Setup
This tutorial uses only base R — no additional packages are needed for the examples here. We do, however, install the canonical package set you will use from Tutorial 03 onward.

title: RStudio
text:
What is RStudio?
RStudio is an Integrated Development Environment (IDE) for R — a graphical front-end that wraps the R engine in a user-friendly interface. You write code in the editor, send it to R for evaluation, and see the results without ever touching a command-line prompt. For the rest of this tutorial series we assume you are using RStudio Desktop, which is free and available for Windows, macOS, and Linux from https://posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop/.
R itself and RStudio are separate pieces of software:

R is the statistical engine. It does the computation.
RStudio is the cockpit. It sends your commands to R and displays the results.

You must install R before RStudio will be of any use. If you have not yet installed R, do so first from https://cran.r-project.org/.

title: RStudio
text:
The four panes
When you open RStudio you see four rectangular regions arranged in a 2 × 2 grid. Each pane has a job:




The four panes of RStudio. Top-left: Source. Top-right: Environment and History. Bottom-left: Console. Bottom-right: Files, Plots, Packages, Help, Viewer.



Source (top-left)
This is your text editor. It is where you write R scripts (.R files) and Quarto documents (.qmd files). Code here is not executed until you send it to the Console — you can write a long script, edit it, save it, and only run it when you are ready.


Console (bottom-left)
This is live R. Anything you type here is evaluated immediately and the result is printed. You can use the Console for quick calculations or one-off commands, but for anything you want to keep, write it in the Source pane and send it to the Console instead — that way your code is saved in a file.


Environment and History (top-right)
The Environment tab shows every object you have created in the current R session — vectors, data frames, functions, fitted models. Clicking the spreadsheet icon next to a data frame opens it in a viewer. The History tab records every command you have run, useful if you want to retrace your steps.


Files, Plots, Packages, Help, Viewer (bottom-right)
A row of tabs along the bottom-right that you will use constantly:

Files — a file browser for your project folder.
Plots — every plot you generate appears here. Use the arrows to navigate through your plotting history.
Packages — a list of installed packages, with checkboxes to load them. You will usually load packages from the Console instead.
Help — where function documentation appears when you type ?function_name.
Viewer — for interactive HTML widgets, including the path diagrams produced by tidySEM later in this series.

title: RStudio
text:
RStudio Projects
An RStudio Project is a folder on your computer that contains your R scripts, data files, and a small .Rproj file that tells RStudio “this folder is a self-contained unit of work”. Projects are the foundation of a reproducible R workflow.

Why projects matter
Without a project, R’s working directory is wherever it happens to be — your desktop, your home folder, somewhere else. Your code will read data from one location on your machine, and that path will not exist on anyone else’s. With a project, the working directory is always the project folder, so read.csv("data/survey.csv") reads a file sitting in your project’s data/ subfolder — no matter where you or a collaborator opens the project from.






Important



Always work inside a Project. If you do not, your file paths become absolute (e.g. C:/Users/yourname/Documents/...) and your code becomes non-reproducible — it will not run on anyone else’s machine, including the version of “your machine” two months from now.




Creating a Project

In RStudio, click File → New Project…
Choose New Directory to start from scratch, or Existing Directory if you already have a folder.
Pick New Project as the project type.
Name the directory (e.g. my_sem_analysis), choose where to create it, and click Create Project.

RStudio restarts in the new project folder. Look at the Console’s prompt — it now shows the path to your project directory. The Files pane in the bottom-right shows a new .Rproj file alongside any other contents.






TipTry it



Create a new RStudio Project called sem_practice on your computer. Open it. In the Console, run getwd() — you should see the path to your sem_practice folder. Create a subfolder called data (use the New Folder button in the Files pane, or run dir.create("data") in the Console).








CautionSolution





After creating the project through the dialogues, the Console will show your project path. Then:

getwd()
dir.create("data")

You should now see a data/ folder in the Files pane.

title: RStudio
text:
Scripts versus Quarto documents
RStudio supports two main file types you will use throughout this series:

R scripts (.R)
A plain-text file containing R code and # comments. Run code line-by-line by placing the cursor on a line and pressing Ctrl+Enter (Windows/Linux) or Cmd+Enter (macOS). Run the whole script with Ctrl+Shift+S or the Source button.
Scripts are the right choice when you are exploring data interactively or running a one-off analysis.


Quarto documents (.qmd)
A text file that mixes prose (Markdown) with R code chunks, which Quarto renders into a polished HTML or PDF document. Each code chunk looks like this:

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
mean(c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5))
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
## [1] 3
```


:::
:::
When you click Render at the top of the editor, Quarto runs every code chunk in order, weaves the output between your prose paragraphs, and writes a finished document to disk. This very tutorial is a .qmd file rendered into the web page you are reading.






Note



For your own dissertation work, Quarto documents are usually the better choice: they keep your narrative, your code, your output, and your plots in a single file, and render into a shareable report. Scripts are better for short explorations or for functions you will reuse across documents.

title: RStudio
text:
Installing and loading packages
A package is a collection of R functions, data, and documentation that someone else has written and shared. Base R comes with a few dozen packages built in; everything else you must install once and then load in every session that needs it.

Installing (once per computer)
You install a package from CRAN (the Comprehensive R Archive Network) with install.packages(). You only need to do this once per machine — the package is downloaded and stored permanently in your R library folder.

install.packages("lavaan")

To install all the packages you will need for this entire tutorial series in one go, run:

install.packages(c("haven", "lavaan", "ggplot2", "psych",
                   "tidySEM", "DiagrammeR", "readxl", "texreg"))







NoteWhat each package does










Package
Purpose




haven
Reading SPSS .sav and Stata .dta files


lavaan
Confirmatory factor analysis, SEM, mediation


ggplot2
Publication-quality plots


psych
Descriptive statistics and Cronbach’s alpha


tidySEM
Drawing path diagrams from fitted lavaan models


DiagrammeR
Hand-authoring concept diagrams via Graphviz


readxl
Reading Excel .xlsx files


texreg
Exporting regression and lavaan tables to HTML/LaTeX







Loading (every session)
Installing a package downloads it onto your computer. Loading a package with library() makes its functions available in your current R session. You must load a package in every R session that uses it — restarting R clears the list of loaded packages.

library(haven)
library(lavaan)
library(ggplot2)







Warninginstall.packages() vs library()



The most common beginner confusion is running install.packages("lavaan") in a script and then re-running it every time the script is opened. install.packages() downloads the package from the internet — slow, and unnecessary after the first time. library() loads an already-installed package. Put library() calls at the top of every script; run install.packages() once, in the Console, then forget about it.




Package installation pitfalls






WarningChoose a CRAN mirror



The first time you call install.packages(), R may prompt you to choose a CRAN mirror — a server that hosts the package files. Pick one geographically close to you (for the UK, the “Cloud” or “Bristol” mirrors are fast). You can set a default mirror in Tools → Global Options → Packages so you are not asked every time.

Binary versus source
On Windows and macOS, R will usually download a pre-built binary package — fast to install. On Linux, packages are often built from source, which requires compilers and can be slow. If installation fails with a message about a missing system library, install that system library through your package manager (e.g. apt install libxml2-dev on Ubuntu) and try again.


Compiling from source
If you see installation of package 'X' had non-zero exit status, scroll up in the Console output — the actual error is usually a missing dependency listed a few lines earlier.

title: RStudio
text:
The Help system
R has excellent built-in documentation. To read the help page for any function, type ? followed by the function name in the Console:

?mean
?lm
?c

The help page opens in the bottom-right Help pane. Every help page follows the same structure:

Description — one paragraph summarising what the function does.
Usage — the function signature, showing all arguments and their defaults.
Arguments — what each argument does, in order.
Details — additional notes, often including the maths or algorithm.
Value — what the function returns.
References — the source paper or book.
Examples — runnable code at the bottom of the page. You can run them all at once with example(mean).







TipTry it



Open the help page for sd() (the standard deviation function). What argument does it take, and what does na.rm do?








CautionSolution





Run ?sd in the Console. The help page shows that sd(x, na.rm = FALSE) takes a numeric vector x and a logical na.rm argument. Setting na.rm = TRUE removes missing values before computing the standard deviation — the same argument we met in Tutorial 01 for mean().




Vignettes
Many packages include vignettes — long-form tutorials that walk through the package’s main features. List them with vignette(package = "lavaan") and open one with, e.g., vignette("lavaan"). Vignettes are the best way to learn a new package once you have the basics.

title: RStudio
text:
Global Options worth setting
A few settings in Tools → Global Options will save you hours of confusion over a dissertation-length project.

General → Advanced
Set Save workspace to .RData into a project directory on exit to Never. The default “Ask” encourages you to save a hidden .RData file that quietly restores yesterday’s objects next time you open the project — a recipe for confusing bugs. Always start each session with a clean Environment.


Code → Saving
Tick Strip trailing horizontal whitespace when saving. Prevents meaningless git diffs from trailing spaces.


Appearance
Pick a comfortable editor theme and font size. Light themes are easier for tutorials; dark themes are easier on the eyes for long sessions.






ImportantRestart R frequently



When something behaves unexpectedly — a function is “not found”, an object has the wrong shape — press Ctrl+Shift+F10 to restart R. This clears the Environment and loaded packages, giving you a clean slate. If your script still runs top-to-bottom after a restart, your analysis is reproducible. If it does not, you have been depending on objects you forgot to create in the script.

title: RStudio
text:
Keyboard shortcuts
A handful of shortcuts will make you noticeably faster. They are worth memorising now.



Shortcut
Action




Ctrl+Enter / Cmd+Enter
Run the current line in the Console


Ctrl+Shift+Enter
Run the entire current code chunk


Ctrl+Shift+S
Source the current script (run all)


Ctrl+Shift+F10
Restart R


Ctrl+L
Clear the Console


Ctrl+1 / Ctrl+2
Move focus to Source / Console


Ctrl+Shift+K
Render the current Quarto document


Alt+-
Insert the assignment operator <-



Alt+- is the single biggest time-saver on this list — it inserts <- with surrounding spaces, so you never have to type the three characters by hand.

title: RStudio
text:
Common errors






Warningcould not find function "read_sav"



You have not loaded the package that provides the function. Run library(haven) first. If that fails with there is no package called 'haven', you have not installed it: run install.packages("haven") once, then library(haven) in every subsequent session.

Error in library(X) : there is no package called 'X'
You are trying to load a package that is not installed. Run install.packages("X") and try again.


Objects appear “from nowhere”
You wrote a script that uses an object called my_data, but my_data is not created anywhere in the script — it exists in your Environment only because you created it interactively in the Console last week. Restart R (Ctrl+Shift+F10) and run your script from top to bottom. If it fails, find the line that should have created my_data and add it.

title: RStudio
text:
Exercises

Create a new RStudio Project called sem_practice. In the project folder, create a subfolder called data. Create a new R script called setup.R and save it in the project root. In setup.R, write library(haven) and library(lavaan) at the top. Run both lines.
Without looking at the table above, list the four RStudio panes and one thing each is used for.
Open the help page for lavaan::cfa() (run ?cfa after loading lavaan). What are the first three arguments the function takes?
Press Ctrl+Shift+F10 to restart R. Confirm that the Environment is now empty. Re-run your setup.R script — does library(lavaan) still work, or do you need to install anything first?







CautionSolutions





Exercise 1
Follow the File → New Project… steps from the “Creating a Project” section. The Console prompt should now show sem_practice/. Run dir.create("data") to create the subfolder. In the Source pane, click File → New File → R Script, type:

library(haven)
library(lavaan)

Save the file as setup.R (Ctrl+S). Place the cursor on each line and press Ctrl+Enter to run it. If lavaan is not installed, run install.packages("lavaan") in the Console first.
Exercise 2

Source (top-left): where you write and edit scripts and Quarto documents.
Console (bottom-left): where R actually runs your code.
Environment (top-right): lists the objects you have created in this session.
Files / Plots / Packages / Help / Viewer (bottom-right): file browser, plot history, package list, documentation, and HTML widgets.

Exercise 3
After library(lavaan), run ?cfa. The first three arguments are model (the model syntax string), data (the data frame), and ordered (an optional vector of variable names to treat as ordered categorical).
Exercise 4
After Ctrl+Shift+F10, the Environment tab is empty. Re-running setup.R will succeed provided lavaan and haven are installed on this machine — library() loads an installed package; it does not need to be reinstalled. If you see there is no package called 'lavaan', you have not yet run install.packages("lavaan").

title: RStudio
text:
Summary & next tutorial
You now have a working RStudio Project, you can install and load the packages we will use throughout this series, and you know how to find help when a function’s behaviour is unclear. In the next tutorial we put these tools to work: importing data from SPSS, CSV, and Excel files into R data frames, checking that the import worked, and handling missing-value codes.

title: RStudio
text:
Further reading

R Core Team (2024) — the official R documentation, including the R Installation and Administration guide.
Wickham (2019) — covers R’s environment model and scoping rules, which underpin why projects matter.
Dalgaard (2008) — an introductory statistics text using R, with examples of importing data and running basic tests.

title: PS dataset — variable definitions
text:
This page documents the variables in the ps_data_reduced teaching dataset, their response scales, and the original source citations for each scale. The dataset is a simulated teaching file used throughout this tutorial series; it is not real participant data.
The dataset ships in two formats in the data/ folder:
Dimensions: 343 rows (participants) × 25 columns (variables). Missing values are coded as -99 in both file formats.

title: PS dataset — variable definitions
text:
Job control
Six items measuring autonomy and control over how work is conducted.








Item
Wording
Response scale

1 = Never, 2, 3, 4, 5 = Very often


con10

1 = Never, 2, 3, 4, 5 = Very often

title: PS dataset — variable definitions
text:
Note on the dataset
This is a simulated teaching dataset originally created by Kevin Daniels (University of East Anglia) for use in the Mplus-based version of this module. The variable structure and response scales follow the published sources cited above, but the individual observations are not real participant data. You can freely use, redistribute, and modify the dataset for teaching purposes.

title: Notation
text: This page lists every Greek symbol used across the tutorial series, its meaning, and where to find the corresponding number in lavaan’s output. The notation follows the simplified lowercase Greek convention used by modern lavaan textbooks (e.g. Beaujean (2014)); we do not use the bold-symbol LISREL matrix form.

title: Notation
text:
Symbols



Symbol
Name
Meaning
Where in lavaan output




\(\lambda\)
lambda
Factor loading — the regression coefficient linking a latent variable to an observed indicator
Estimate column under =~ blocks; std.all column for the standardised loading


\(\beta\)
beta
Structural coefficient — a regression path between variables in the structural model
Estimate column under ~ blocks; std.all for the standardised path


\(\psi\)
psi
Latent (co)variance — variance of a latent variable, or covariance between two latent variables
Variances and Covariances sections of summary(fit)


\(\theta\)
theta
Residual (co)variance of an observed variable — measurement error
Residual Variances and Residual Covariances sections


\(\zeta\)
zeta
Disturbance — residual variance of a latent dependent variable
Variances section, listed under the latent variable’s name


\(\varepsilon\)
epsilon
Residual of an observed variable (synonym for \(\theta\) when referring to a single indicator)
Residual Variances section


\(a\)
—
Path from \(X\) to mediator \(M\) in a mediation model
Labelled path in lavaan; appears under ~ with its label


\(b\)
—
Path from mediator \(M\) to \(Y\) in a mediation model
Labelled path in lavaan; appears under ~ with its label


\(c'\)
c-prime
Direct effect of \(X\) on \(Y\) in a mediation model
Labelled path in lavaan


\(c\)
c
Total effect of \(X\) on \(Y\) (before decomposition)
Usually computed as \(c' + ab\) via :=


\(ab\)
—
Indirect effect — the product of \(a\) and \(b\)
Defined parameter via := in lavaan


\(\hat{\Sigma}(\theta)\)
Sigma-hat
The model-implied covariance matrix — what the model predicts the observed covariances should be
Not printed directly; underlying every fit index


\(S\)
S
The observed sample covariance matrix
SAMPSTAT output; sampleStats in summary()


\(\chi^2\)
chi-squared
Model fit statistic comparing \(S\) and \(\hat{\Sigma}(\theta)\)
fitMeasures(fit)["chisq"]


\(df\)
degrees of freedom
The number of independent pieces of information available to test the model
fitMeasures(fit)["df"]


\(p\)
p-value
Probability of observing a \(\chi^2\) at least as large under the null hypothesis of perfect fit
fitMeasures(fit)["pvalue"]


CFI
Comparative Fit Index
Fit index comparing the model to a null (independence) model; \(> .95\) is good
fitMeasures(fit)["cfi"]


TLI
Tucker–Lewis Index
Fit index penalising model complexity; \(> .95\) is good
fitMeasures(fit)["tli"]


RMSEA
Root mean square error of approximation
Badness-of-fit index; \(< .05\) is close, \(< .08\) is acceptable
fitMeasures(fit)["rmsea"]


SRMR
Standardised root mean square residual
Badness-of-fit index; \(< .08\) is good
fitMeasures(fit)["srmr"]


\(N\)
sample size
Number of participants in the analysis
nobs(fit)


\(R^2\)
R-squared
Proportion of variance in a dependent variable explained by its predictors
summary(fit, rsquare = TRUE) or lavInspect(fit, "rsquare")


MI
Modification index
Estimated drop in \(\chi^2\) if a fixed parameter were freed
modindices(fit) output, mi column

title: Notation
text:
Reading the lavaan output
When you run summary(fit, standardized = TRUE, fit.measures = TRUE), lavaan prints the parameter estimates in sections:

Latent variables — the =~ blocks. The Estimate column gives the unstandardised \(\lambda\); std.all gives the fully standardised loading.
Regressions — the ~ blocks. The Estimate column gives the unstandardised \(\beta\); std.all gives the standardised path.
Covariances — the ~~ blocks between exogenous variables. These are \(\psi\) (latent) or free covariances (observed).
Variances — ~~ blocks for each variable with itself. Latent variances are \(\psi\); observed residual variances are \(\theta\).
Intercepts — means of observed and latent variables.
Variances (residual) — \(\theta\) for observed variables; \(\zeta\) for latent dependent variables.

For a complete walkthrough of every section, see Tutorial 07 (CFA — practical).

title: Notation
text:
Reading fit indices
The canonical set we use across this series, with the Hu and Bentler (1999) cutoffs:








Index
Threshold
Good or bad?




\(\chi^2\)
\(p > .05\)
Good (non-significant) — but sensitive to sample size


RMSEA
\(< .08\) (acceptable), \(< .05\) (close)
Badness (lower is better)


CFI
\(> .95\) (good), \(> .90\) (acceptable)
Goodness (higher is better)


TLI
\(> .95\) (good), \(> .90\) (acceptable)
Goodness (higher is better)


SRMR
\(< .08\)
Badness (lower is better)



To extract all of these in one call:

fitMeasures(fit, c("chisq", "df", "pvalue",
                   "cfi", "tli",
                   "rmsea", "rmsea.ci.lower", "rmsea.ci.upper",
                   "srmr"))

title: Notation
text:
See also

Glossary — definitions of every term used in the series.
Cheat sheet — lavaan syntax reference.

title: A Step-by-Step Introduction to Structural Equation Modeling with lavaan in R
text:
lavaan-tutorial is a tutorial site covering structural equation modelling (SEM) in R using the lavaan package. It is written for MSc students on the Statistical Skills module of the MSc in Organisational Psychology at the University of East Anglia.


You are new to R, RStudio, and lavaan. You may have used SPSS before, but we will not assume that — every concept is taught from the ground up in R/lavaan. By the end of the 16 tutorials you will be able to specify, estimate, and report confirmatory factor analyses, path models, structural equation models, and mediation models in lavaan, and you will have a path into multilevel, growth, and moderation analyses.










Foundations (1–4) — R basics, RStudio, importing data, simple inference (t-test, ANOVA, lm).
Bridging (5) — Introducing lavaan: declarative model specification.
Core lavaan (6–12) — CFA, parcels and second-order factors, path analysis, SEM with latents, mediation.
Capstone (13) — Reporting and exporting tables, figures, and APA write-ups.
Advanced (14–16) — Multilevel models, growth curve models, moderation.

Start with Tutorial 01 and work through in order; the later tutorials build on the dataset and concepts introduced earlier. You can also jump to any tutorial using the sidebar on the left, or use the search box at the top.



Install R from https://cran.r-project.org/ and RStudio from https://posit.co/products/desktop/rstudio/. Then, in the R console:

install.packages(c("haven", "lavaan", "ggplot2", "psych",
                   "tidySEM", "DiagrammeR", "readxl", "texreg"))




Each tutorial follows the same structure:

Learning objectives — what you will be able to do by the end.
Setup — the package-loading code you need first; in later tutorials this appears in a collapsed <details> block.
Concept — narrative explanation, diagrams, and equations.
Worked example — full code, output, and interpretation paragraph.
Common errors — ::: {.callout-warning} callouts flagging what tends to go wrong.
Exercises — inline ::: {.callout-tip} Try-its during the tutorial; end-of-tutorial exercises with collapsed solutions in ::: {.callout-caution collapse="true"}.
Summary & next tutorial — a one-paragraph bridge to the next page.
Further reading — academic papers, cited APA-style.

Six callout colours encode meaning throughout the site:



Colour
Type
Meaning




Blue
note
Concepts and definitions


Green
tip
Inline Try-it exercises


Orange
warning
Common errors to avoid


Red
important
Critical watch-outs


Yellow
caution
Collapsed exercise solutions












Note



This lavaan tutorial series is a port of teaching materials originally developed by Kevin Daniels (University of East Anglia) for his MSc Organisational Psychology Statistical Skills module, which used Mplus. The R/lavaan adaptation is by George Michaelides (University of East Anglia). The original Mplus materials remain with their author.





If you use these tutorials in your teaching or research, please cite:

Michaelides, G. (2026). A step-by-step introduction to structural equation modeling with lavaan in R. University of East Anglia. https://michaelides.github.io/lavaan-tutorial/




Tutorial content is licensed under CC-BY 4.0. Embedded R/lavaan code snippets are released under the MIT licence.

title: A Step-by-Step Introduction to Structural Equation Modeling with lavaan in R
text: Tutorial content is licensed under CC-BY 4.0. Embedded R/lavaan code snippets are released under the MIT licence.

title: Glossary
text: This glossary collects the key terms used across the tutorial series. Terms are linked from individual tutorials on first use. Where a term has a Greek symbol, the symbol is shown in the notation key.

title: Glossary
text:
A

assignment
Storing a value in a named R object with the <- operator. For example, x <- 5 assigns the value 5 to the object x.


ANOVA
Analysis of variance. A generalisation of the t-test to three or more groups. Tests whether the means of the groups are all equal. In R, fitted with aov().

title: Glossary
text:
B

bootstrap
A resampling method used to estimate the sampling distribution of a statistic — most commonly a regression coefficient or an indirect effect — by repeatedly sampling with replacement from the observed data. In lavaan, requested with se = "boot" and bootstrap = N. Bootstrap confidence intervals are preferred over the Sobel test for mediation analysis because the product of two normally distributed coefficients is not itself normally distributed.


BCa
Bias-corrected and accelerated. A refinement of the bootstrap confidence interval that corrects for both bias and skewness in the bootstrap distribution. Requested in lavaan with parameterEstimates(fit, boot.ci.type = "bca.simple").

title: Glossary
text:
C

CFA
Confirmatory factor analysis. A statistical technique for testing whether a hypothesised factor structure — which observed variables load on which latent factors — fits the observed covariance matrix. Fitted in lavaan with cfa().


common method variance
Variance shared between observed variables due to the measurement method (e.g. all items measured on the same Likert scale in the same questionnaire) rather than due to the latent construct. Can be modelled as residual covariances between items in CFA.


constraining parameters
Forcing two or more model parameters to take the same value. In lavaan, achieved by labelling parameters with the same identifier — for example, y ~ a*x1 and z ~ a*x2 constrains the two regression coefficients to be equal.


convergence
The iterative estimation algorithm has found a solution — the difference between parameter estimates across successive iterations has fallen below a tolerance threshold. If a model does not converge, the parameter estimates are not trustworthy. lavaan prints a warning when convergence fails.

title: Glossary
text:
D

degrees of freedom
The number of independent pieces of information available to estimate model parameters. In SEM, df = q(q+1)/2 - t, where q is the number of observed variables and t is the number of free parameters. A model with df = 0 is just-identified; df > 0 is overidentified; df < 0 is underidentified and cannot be estimated.


direct effect
In a mediation model, the effect of the independent variable \(X\) on the outcome \(Y\) that does not pass through the mediator \(M\). Denoted \(c'\). In lavaan, estimated directly as a regression path.


disturbance
The residual variance of a latent dependent variable — the part of the latent variable’s variance not explained by its predictors in the structural model. Denoted \(\zeta\) (zeta).

title: Glossary
text:
E

endogenous
A variable that is predicted by at least one other variable in the model — a dependent variable. Contrast with exogenous.


estimation
The process of finding the values of a model’s free parameters that best reproduce the observed covariance matrix. The default estimator in lavaan is maximum likelihood (ML); the robust variant MLR is recommended for non-normal data.


exogenous
A variable that is not predicted by any other variable in the model — an independent variable. Exogenous variables have variances and covariances as model parameters but no structural equations predicting them.

title: Glossary
text:
F

factor loading
The regression coefficient linking a latent variable to one of its observed indicators. Denoted \(\lambda\) (lambda). In lavaan, specified with the =~ operator. The first loading of each factor is usually fixed to 1 (the marker-variable method) to set the latent variable’s scale.


FIML
Full information maximum likelihood. An approach to missing data that uses all available information in the dataset — every participant contributes to whichever parameters their observed values inform. The default missing-data treatment in lavaan. Preferred over listwise deletion because it is unbiased under MCAR and MAR.


fit indices
Statistics that measure how well a model reproduces the observed covariance matrix. The canonical set used in this series is \(\chi^2\), RMSEA, CFI, TLI, and SRMR. See Hu and Bentler (1999) for the cutoff criteria.


fixed parameter
A model parameter whose value is set by the researcher rather than estimated from the data. Common examples: a factor loading fixed to 1 (marker variable), a residual covariance fixed to 0 (default). In lavaan, fixed with * followed by a number — e.g. f =~ 1*x1.


free parameter
A model parameter whose value is estimated from the data. Most parameters in a SEM are free.

title: Glossary
text:
H

Heywood case
An impossible parameter estimate — most commonly a negative variance or a standardised factor loading above 1. Usually indicates either a misspecified model, an underidentified model, or a sample that is too small. lavaan prints a warning when it detects a Heywood case.

title: Glossary
text:
I

identification
A model is identified if a unique solution exists for every free parameter. Necessary conditions include positive degrees of freedom (the t-rule) and a set scale for every latent variable. A model that is not identified cannot be meaningfully estimated.


indirect effect
In a mediation model, the effect of the independent variable \(X\) on the outcome \(Y\) that operates through the mediator \(M\). Equal to the product \(ab\) of the two paths \(X \rightarrow M\) and \(M \rightarrow Y\). In lavaan, defined with the := operator — e.g. indirect := a * b.


indicator
An observed variable that measures a latent variable. For example, pa1 is an indicator of the latent factor “positive affect”.

title: Glossary
text:
J

just-identified
A model with zero degrees of freedom — the number of free parameters equals the number of non-redundant elements in the covariance matrix. Such a model fits perfectly by construction and cannot be rejected by the data. Also called a saturated model.

title: Glossary
text:
L

latent variable
An unobserved variable that is inferred from its effects on observed indicators. Represented by circles in path diagrams. Examples in this series: “positive affect”, “job control”, “creativity”.


listwise deletion
