Removing any participant with a missing value on any variable in the model. Reduces sample size, reduces power, and can bias parameter estimates if the missingness is not completely random. lavaan uses FIML by default instead.


loadings
See factor loading.

title: Glossary
text:
M

marker variable
A method for setting the scale of a latent variable by fixing one of its factor loadings to 1 (and freely estimating the latent variance). lavaan’s default. The alternative is std.lv = TRUE, which fixes the latent variance to 1 and freely estimates all loadings.


MAR
Missing at random. The probability of a missing value on variable \(Y\) is unrelated to the value of \(Y\), conditional on other variables in the analysis. FIML gives unbiased estimates under MAR.


MCAR
Missing completely at random. The probability of a missing value is unrelated to any variable in the dataset — missingness is purely random. The strongest missing-data assumption; FIML and listwise deletion both give unbiased estimates under MCAR, but FIML is more powerful.


measurement error
The portion of an observed variable’s variance that is not due to the latent construct — a combination of random error and item-specific systematic variance. Denoted \(\theta\) (theta). Modelled as the residual variance of each indicator in CFA.


measurement model
The part of a SEM that specifies how latent variables are measured by observed indicators — that is, the CFA portion of the model. Contrast with the structural model.


mediation
A relationship in which an independent variable \(X\) influences an outcome \(Y\) through an intermediate variable \(M\) (the mediator). The total effect of \(X\) on \(Y\) is decomposed into a direct effect (\(c'\)) and an indirect effect (\(ab\)). See Tutorials 11 and 12.


missingness
The pattern of missing values in a dataset. Distinguished into MCAR, MAR, and MNAR (missing not at random).


MNAR
Missing not at random. The probability of a missing value depends on the unobserved value itself — for example, people with very high salaries are more likely to refuse to report them. FIML is biased under MNAR; no standard method fully addresses MNAR without strong assumptions.


MLR
Maximum likelihood with robust standard errors (Satorra–Bentler). The default estimator we recommend throughout this series. Robust to non-normality. Requested in lavaan with estimator = "MLR".


modification indices
Statistics that estimate how much the model’s \(\chi^2\) would improve if a currently-fixed parameter were freely estimated. A value above 3.84 suggests a parameter worth considering for freeing (\(\alpha = .05\)). Should be used cautiously — they are data-driven and can lead to overfitting. In lavaan: modindices(fit, minimum.value = 3.84, sort. = TRUE).

title: Glossary
text:
O

observed variable
A variable that is directly measured — a survey item, a test score, a demographic. Represented by squares or rectangles in path diagrams. Also called a manifest variable.


overidentified
A model with positive degrees of freedom — fewer free parameters than non-redundant elements in the covariance matrix. Such a model can be empirically tested against the data. The goal of SEM is to specify an overidentified model that fits well.

title: Glossary
text:
P

parcel
An aggregate — usually a mean or sum — of two or more items, used as a single indicator of a latent variable. Parcelling reduces the number of indicators per factor, which can simplify a model and improve fit, at the cost of losing item-level information. Controversial; see Tutorial 08.


parameter
A quantity in a model that is either estimated from the data (free) or set by the researcher (fixed). In SEM, parameters include factor loadings, regression coefficients, variances, and covariances.


path analysis
A structural equation model with observed variables only — no latent variables. A system of regression equations. Fitted in lavaan with sem(). See Tutorial 09.


psi
See variance of a latent variable.

title: Glossary
text:
R

residual covariance
A covariance between the residuals of two observed variables. Indicates that the two variables share variance not explained by their common latent factor(s) — often due to common method variance or a shared occasion. In lavaan, specified with ~~ — e.g. x1 ~~ x2.


RMSEA
Root mean square error of approximation. A badness-of-fit index (lower is better). Values below .05 indicate close fit, .05–.08 acceptable fit, above .10 poor fit. Reported with a 90% confidence interval; the upper bound of the CI should be below .08.

title: Glossary
text:
S

saturated model
See just-identified.


second-order factor
A latent variable whose indicators are themselves latent variables. For example, “burnout” might be a second-order factor measured by the first-order factors “emotional exhaustion” and “cynicism”. See Tutorial 08.


SEM
Structural equation modelling. A framework for testing models that combine a measurement model (latent variables measured by observed indicators) with a structural model (regression paths among latent and observed variables). Fitted in lavaan with sem().


simple slopes
In moderated regression, the slope of \(Y\) on \(X\) at a specific value of the moderator \(W\). Usually computed at \(W = \pm 1\) SD from its mean. See Tutorial 16.


Sobel test
A test of the indirect effect \(ab\) based on assuming the product of two normally distributed coefficients is itself normally distributed — which it is not. Superseded by bootstrap confidence intervals. Included here for historical context; not used in this series.


SRMR
Standardised root mean square residual. A badness-of-fit index (lower is better). The average of the standardised residuals between the observed and model-implied covariance matrices. Values below .08 indicate good fit.


standardised solution
Parameter estimates expressed in standard deviation units, so that loadings and path coefficients are comparable across variables on different scales. In lavaan: summary(fit, standardized = TRUE) or standardizedSolution(fit). The std.all column fully standardises both observed and latent variables.


structural model
The part of a SEM that specifies the regression relationships among variables — which variables predict which. Contrast with the measurement model.

title: Glossary
text:
T

t-rule
A necessary condition for identification: the number of free parameters \(t\) must be less than or equal to the number of non-redundant elements in the observed covariance matrix, \(q(q+1)/2\), where \(q\) is the number of observed variables. The difference is the degrees of freedom.


theta
See measurement error.


total effect
In a mediation model, the sum of the direct and indirect effects: \(c' + ab\). The total effect of \(X\) on \(Y\) ignoring the mediator.


tibble
A modern data frame used by the haven and tidyverse packages. Prints more neatly than a base data.frame and never converts strings to factors. Behaves like a regular data frame for the purposes of this series.

title: Glossary
text:
U

underidentified
A model with negative degrees of freedom — more free parameters than the data can support. Cannot be estimated. The remedy is to fix or constrain parameters, not to collect more data.

title: Glossary
text:
V

vector
The fundamental data structure in R: a sequence of values of the same type (numeric, character, logical, integer). Created with c(). See Tutorial 01.


latent variance
The variance of a latent variable — a model parameter in CFA and SEM. Denoted \(\psi\) (psi). In the marker-variable method, the latent variance is freely estimated; with std.lv = TRUE, it is fixed to 1.

title: lavaan syntax cheat sheet
text: A one-page reference for the lavaan syntax used across this series. Grouped by category. For definitions of the terms, see the glossary; for the Greek symbols, see the notation key.

title: lavaan syntax cheat sheet
text:
Model specification









Syntax
Name
Example
Notes




=~
“is measured by” — defines a latent variable
pa =~ pa1 + pa2 + pa3
The first indicator’s loading is fixed to 1 by default (marker-variable method)


~
“is regressed on” — defines a regression path
creat ~ pa + con
Used for structural paths and for lm()-style regressions


~~
“is correlated with” — covariance
pa ~~ na
Default between exogenous variables; can also free residual covariances


~ 1
intercept / mean
pa ~ 1
Usually estimated automatically for observed variables; freed for latents with meanstructure = TRUE


1 *
fix a parameter to 1
pa =~ 1 * pa1
Explicit form of the marker-variable default


0 *
fix a parameter to 0
pa ~~ 0 * na
Explicitly constrain a covariance to zero


n *
fix a parameter to a numeric value
x ~ 0.5 * y
Rarely needed; used for a priori constraints


start() *
set a starting value
pa =~ start(1.2) * pa1
Helps with convergence problems


label *
label a parameter (for constraints or :=)
creat ~ b * pa + c * con
Two parameters with the same label are constrained equal


c(a, b) *
group-specific parameter
pa ~ c(a1, a2) * con
Used in multigroup SEM; a1 for group 1, a2 for group 2


:=
define a new (derived) parameter
indirect := a * b
Operates on labelled parameters; used for indirect effects

title: lavaan syntax cheat sheet
text:
Estimation options
Pass these arguments to cfa(), sem(), or lavaan():








Argument
Effect
Example




estimator = "MLR"
Robust maximum likelihood (Satorra–Bentler). Our default.
cfa(model, data = df, estimator = "MLR")


estimator = "ML"
Plain maximum likelihood. Required when bootstrapping.
sem(model, data = df, estimator = "ML", se = "boot")


se = "boot"
Bootstrap standard errors
sem(model, data = df, se = "boot", bootstrap = 1000)


bootstrap = N
Number of bootstrap replications
bootstrap = 1000


std.lv = TRUE
Set latent variances to 1; freely estimate all loadings
cfa(model, data = df, std.lv = TRUE)


orthogonal = TRUE
Force all latent covariances to zero
cfa(model, data = df, orthogonal = TRUE)


missing = "ml"
Full-information maximum likelihood for missing data (default)
sem(model, data = df, missing = "ml")


missing = "listwise"
Listwise deletion
sem(model, data = df, missing = "listwise")


meanstructure = TRUE
Estimate intercepts and latent means
cfa(model, data = df, meanstructure = TRUE)


group = "gender"
Multigroup analysis
cfa(model, data = df, group = "gender")

title: lavaan syntax cheat sheet
text:
Entry-point functions








Function
Use
Example




cfa()
Confirmatory factor analysis (measurement model only)
fit <- cfa(model, data = df)


sem()
Structural equation model (measurement + structural)
fit <- sem(model, data = df)


lavaan()
The general function; cfa() and sem() are wrappers
fit <- lavaan(model, data = df)


growth()
Latent growth curve model
fit <- growth(model, data = df)

title: lavaan syntax cheat sheet
text:
Reading output








Function
What it returns
Example




summary(fit)
A compact summary of parameter estimates
summary(fit)


summary(fit, standardized = TRUE)
Adds the std.all, std.lv, std.nox columns
summary(fit, standardized = TRUE)


summary(fit, fit.measures = TRUE)
Adds the fit indices section at the top
summary(fit, fit.measures = TRUE)


summary(fit, rsquare = TRUE)
Adds an \(R^2\) row for each dependent variable
summary(fit, rsquare = TRUE)


parameterEstimates(fit)
Parameter estimates as a data frame (one row per parameter)
parameterEstimates(fit)


parameterEstimates(fit, standardized = TRUE)
Same, with standardised columns
parameterEstimates(fit, standardized = TRUE)


parameterEstimates(fit, boot.ci.type = "bca.simple")
BCa bootstrap confidence intervals
Use after se = "boot"


standardizedSolution(fit)
Tidy data frame of standardised parameters only
standardizedSolution(fit)


fitMeasures(fit)
Named vector of ~40 fit indices
fitMeasures(fit)


fitMeasures(fit, c(...))
Selected fit indices
fitMeasures(fit, c("chisq","df","pvalue","cfi","tli","rmsea","srmr"))


coef(fit)
Just the parameter estimates as a named vector
coef(fit)


confint(fit)
Confidence intervals for each parameter
confint(fit, level = 0.95)


fitted(fit)
The model-implied covariance matrix \(\hat{\Sigma}(\theta)\)
fitted(fit)


resid(fit)
Residual covariance matrix \(S - \hat{\Sigma}(\theta)\)
resid(fit)


lavInspect(fit, "rsquare")
\(R^2\) for each observed and latent dependent variable
lavInspect(fit, "rsquare")


lavInspect(fit, "sampstat")
The sample covariance matrix \(S\)
lavInspect(fit, "sampstat")

title: lavaan syntax cheat sheet
text:
Diagnostics and respecification








Function
What it returns
Example




modindices(fit)
All modification indices
modindices(fit)


modindices(fit, minimum.value = 3.84)
Only MIs above 3.84 (\(\alpha = .05\))
modindices(fit, minimum.value = 3.84)


modindices(fit, sort. = TRUE)
Sorted by MI, largest first
modindices(fit, minimum.value = 3.84, sort. = TRUE)


modindices(fit, op = "==~")
Only MIs for factor loadings
modindices(fit, op = "==~")


anova(fit1, fit2)
Likelihood-ratio test between nested models
anova(fit1, fit2)


lavTestScore(fit)
Score test for freeing fixed parameters
lavTestScore(fit)

title: lavaan syntax cheat sheet
text:
Tables and figures








Function
What it does
Example




texreg::screenreg(fit)
Print a regression-style table to the screen
texreg::screenreg(fit)


texreg::htmlreg(fit, file = "table.html")
Write a table to an HTML file
texreg::htmlreg(fit, file = "tab.html")


texreg::texreg(fit, file = "table.tex")
Write a table to a LaTeX file
texreg::texreg(fit, file = "tab.tex")


semPlot::semPaths(fit)
Legacy path-diagram plot
semPlot::semPaths(fit, whatLabels = "std")


tidySEM::graph_sem(fit)
Modern path diagram from a fitted lavaan object
tidySEM::graph_sem(fit)


tidySEM::prepare_graph(fit)
Customisable graph object (edit before rendering)
tidySEM::prepare_graph(fit) \|> tidySEM::edit_graph(...)

title: lavaan syntax cheat sheet
text:
A complete worked example
Putting it all together — the two-factor CFA we walk through in Tutorial 07:

library(lavaan)

model <- '
  NA =~ na1 + na2 + na3
  PA =~ pa1 + pa2 + pa3
  NA ~~ PA
'

fit <- cfa(model, data = ps, estimator = "MLR")
summary(fit, standardized = TRUE, fit.measures = TRUE)
fitMeasures(fit, c("chisq", "df", "pvalue",
                   "cfi", "tli",
                   "rmsea", "rmsea.ci.lower", "rmsea.ci.upper",
                   "srmr"))
modindices(fit, minimum.value = 3.84, sort. = TRUE)
tidySEM::graph_sem(fit)

title: R basics
text:
By the end of this tutorial you will be able to:

assign values to named objects and inspect what is in your R environment
create vectors of different types (numeric, character, logical) and index them
build data frames from vectors and extract rows, columns, and cells
compute descriptive statistics (mean, median, standard deviation) and handle missing values with na.rm = TRUE
create factors for categorical variables and understand why they matter for Likert-scale items

title: R basics
text:
By the end of this tutorial you will be able to:

assign values to named objects and inspect what is in your R environment
create vectors of different types (numeric, character, logical) and index them
build data frames from vectors and extract rows, columns, and cells
compute descriptive statistics (mean, median, standard deviation) and handle missing values with na.rm = TRUE
create factors for categorical variables and understand why they matter for Likert-scale items

title: R basics
text:
Setup
This tutorial uses only base R — no additional packages are needed. We set the output comment prefix to ## so that the rendered output matches what you will see in your RStudio console.

title: R basics
text:
Assignment and objects
In R, you store values in named objects using the assignment operator <-. Think of an object as a labelled box that holds a value you can refer to later.

my_age <- 34
my_age

## [1] 34


The arrow points from the value on the right to the name on the left, so “34 goes into my_age”. You can also use = for assignment, but <- is the convention we follow throughout these tutorials because it makes the direction of assignment explicit.
Object names should be descriptive and use snake_case — lowercase letters with underscores separating words. Avoid dots in names; they are an older R convention and can be confused with method calls in other languages.

mean_score <- 3.45
participant_id <- "P001"
both_assigned <- TRUE

To see everything in your current environment, use ls(). To remove an object, use rm().

ls()

## [1] "both_assigned"   "mean_score"      "my_age"          "pandoc_dir"     
## [5] "participant_id"  "quarto_bin_path"

rm(both_assigned)







Note



R is case-sensitive. My_Age and my_age are two different objects. If you get an object 'x' not found error, check your capitalisation first.

title: R basics
text:
Vectors
A vector is the fundamental data structure in R — a sequence of values that all share the same type. You create vectors with c(), which stands for “combine” or “concatenate”.

scores <- c(3, 4, 2, 5, 4, 3, 5, 2, 4, 3)
scores

##  [1] 3 4 2 5 4 3 5 2 4 3


Every value in a vector must be the same type. R has four main types you will meet:

age_group      <- c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)        # numeric (double)
participant    <- c("P001", "P002", "P003") # character
consent_given  <- c(TRUE, TRUE, FALSE, TRUE) # logical
likert_integer <- c(1L, 2L, 3L, 4L, 5L)   # integer (the L suffix forces integer)

You can inspect the type and length of any object with class(), typeof(), and length():

class(scores)

## [1] "numeric"

typeof(scores)

## [1] "double"

length(scores)

## [1] 10


For a compact overview of an object’s structure, use str():

str(scores)

##  num [1:10] 3 4 2 5 4 3 5 2 4 3








TipTry it



Create a character vector called item_names containing the names of three survey items (e.g. "con1", "con3", "con6"). Then check its class and length.








CautionSolution






item_names <- c("con1", "con3", "con6")
class(item_names)

## [1] "character"

length(item_names)

## [1] 3






Coercion
If you mix types in a vector, R silently coerces everything to the most general type:

mixed <- c(1, "two", 3)
mixed

## [1] "1"   "two" "3"

class(mixed)

## [1] "character"


The number 1 and 3 have been converted to text. This is rarely what you want, so keep your vectors homogeneous.

title: R basics
text:
Indexing vectors
You pull out individual elements of a vector with square brackets. Position numbers start at 1 in R (not 0, as in Python or C).

scores[1]         # first element

## [1] 3

scores[c(1, 3)]   # first and third

## [1] 3 2

scores[2:5]       # second through fifth

## [1] 4 2 5 4


You can also use a logical vector to select elements that meet a condition:

scores[scores > 3]

## [1] 4 5 4 5 4


This is called logical indexing and you will use it constantly. The comparison scores > 3 produces a logical vector of the same length as scores:

scores > 3

##  [1] FALSE  TRUE FALSE  TRUE  TRUE FALSE  TRUE FALSE  TRUE FALSE


R returns TRUE for each element that satisfies the condition, and the square brackets keep only those positions.






TipTry it



Use logical indexing to extract only the scores equal to 5 from scores.








CautionSolution






scores[scores == 5]

## [1] 5 5

title: R basics
text:
Data frames
A data frame is R’s version of a spreadsheet — a collection of columns (vectors) where each column can have a different type but every column has the same length. This is the structure you will work with most often when analysing survey data.
You can build a data frame from vectors with data.frame():

df <- data.frame(
  participant = c("P001", "P002", "P003", "P004"),
  age_group   = c(2, 3, 4, 3),
  pa_score    = c(4.2, 3.8, 2.1, 3.5)
)
df

##   participant age_group pa_score
## 1        P001         2      4.2
## 2        P002         3      3.8
## 3        P003         4      2.1
## 4        P004         3      3.5


Inspect its dimensions and column names:

nrow(df)

## [1] 4

ncol(df)

## [1] 3

dim(df)

## [1] 4 3

names(df)

## [1] "participant" "age_group"   "pa_score"


For a quick look at the first or last few rows, use head() and tail():

head(df, 2)

##   participant age_group pa_score
## 1        P001         2      4.2
## 2        P002         3      3.8

tail(df, 1)

##   participant age_group pa_score
## 4        P004         3      3.5


And for summary statistics of every column at once, summary():

summary(df)

##     participant   age_group       pa_score   
##  Length   :4    Min.   :2.00   Min.   :2.10  
##  N.unique :4    1st Qu.:2.75   1st Qu.:3.15  
##  N.blank  :0    Median :3.00   Median :3.65  
##  Min.nchar:4    Mean   :3.00   Mean   :3.40  
##  Max.nchar:4    3rd Qu.:3.25   3rd Qu.:3.90  
##                 Max.   :4.00   Max.   :4.20








Note



summary() is your fastest first look at any dataset. It shows the minimum, first quartile, median, mean, third quartile, and maximum for numeric columns, and counts for factors.



Indexing data frames
Data frames use row, column indexing with square brackets:

df[, "pa_score"]          # all rows, one column (returns a vector)

## [1] 4.2 3.8 2.1 3.5

df[, c("participant", "pa_score")]  # two columns (returns a data frame)

##   participant pa_score
## 1        P001      4.2
## 2        P002      3.8
## 3        P003      2.1
## 4        P004      3.5

df[1, ]                   # first row, all columns

##   participant age_group pa_score
## 1        P001         2      4.2

df[1:3, c("participant", "age_group")]  # rows 1-3, two columns

##   participant age_group
## 1        P001         2
## 2        P002         3
## 3        P003         4


The $ operator is a convenient shortcut for extracting a single column by name:

df$pa_score

## [1] 4.2 3.8 2.1 3.5

df$age_group

## [1] 2 3 4 3








TipTry it



Extract the pa_score values for participants whose age_group is 3 or higher, using logical indexing on df$age_group.








CautionSolution






df$pa_score[df$age_group >= 3]

## [1] 3.8 2.1 3.5

title: R basics
text:
Functions for describing data
R provides built-in functions for the descriptive statistics you will need throughout these tutorials:

mean_scores <- c(3.2, 4.1, 2.8, 3.9, 4.5, 3.1, 2.9, 3.7)

mean(mean_scores)

## [1] 3.525

median(mean_scores)

## [1] 3.45

sd(mean_scores)

## [1] 0.6158618

var(mean_scores)

## [1] 0.3792857

quantile(mean_scores, probs = c(0.25, 0.50, 0.75))

##  25%  50%  75% 
## 3.05 3.45 3.95

range(mean_scores)

## [1] 2.8 4.5



Dealing with NA
Real survey data almost always has missing values. In R, missing values are represented by NA. The problem is that many functions return NA if any NA is present:

data_with_na <- c(3, 4, NA, 2, 5)
mean(data_with_na)

## [1] NA


Pass na.rm = TRUE to tell R to ignore missing values before computing:

mean(data_with_na, na.rm = TRUE)

## [1] 3.5

sd(data_with_na, na.rm = TRUE)

## [1] 1.290994








Warning



Forgetting na.rm = TRUE is the single most common error in early R use. If you see NA as your result and did not expect it, add na.rm = TRUE and try again.

title: R basics
text:
Factors
A factor is R’s structure for categorical variables — a vector of integer codes, each mapped to a text label. Factors matter because many statistical functions (ANOVA, regression with grouping variables) treat factors differently from numeric vectors.

gender_raw <- c(0, 1, 1, 0, 1)
gender <- factor(gender_raw, levels = c(0, 1), labels = c("Male", "Female"))
gender

## [1] Male   Female Female Male   Female
## Levels: Male Female

class(gender)

## [1] "factor"

levels(gender)

## [1] "Male"   "Female"


For Likert-scale items (e.g. 1 = Never, 5 = Very often), you will usually keep them as numeric values because you want means and standard deviations. But for grouping variables like gender, age band, or experimental condition, convert them to factors so R knows to treat them as categories.

table(gender)

## gender
##   Male Female 
##      2      3








Note



You will see in Tutorial 03 that when you import an SPSS .sav file with haven::read_sav(), categorical variables come in as haven_labelled objects. You convert them to factors with haven::as_factor().

title: R basics
text:
Missing values
R distinguishes several kinds of “nothing”:

NA — “Not Available”. A missing value. The most common one in survey data.
NaN — “Not a Number”. The result of an undefined mathematical operation, e.g. 0 / 0.
NULL — “empty”. The object itself does not exist (different from NA, which means the value is missing).

You test for missing values with is.na(), which returns a logical vector:

survey_item <- c(4, 3, NA, 5, 2, NA, 4)
is.na(survey_item)

## [1] FALSE FALSE  TRUE FALSE FALSE  TRUE FALSE

sum(is.na(survey_item))   # count of missing values

## [1] 2


To keep only the complete cases (rows with no NA anywhere), use complete.cases():

complete <- complete.cases(survey_item)
survey_item[complete]

## [1] 4 3 5 2 4








TipTry it



Create a vector items <- c(3, NA, 4, 5, NA, 2, 4). Calculate (a) the number of missing values, (b) the mean of the non-missing values, and (c) a new vector with the NAs removed.








CautionSolution






items <- c(3, NA, 4, 5, NA, 2, 4)
sum(is.na(items))                     # (a) count missing

## [1] 2

mean(items, na.rm = TRUE)             # (b) mean of non-missing

## [1] 3.6

items[complete.cases(items)]          # (c) remove NAs

## [1] 3 4 5 2 4











NoteVectors vs lists



A vector holds values of the same type. A list can hold values of different types — including other vectors, data frames, or even other lists. You index lists with double square brackets [[ ]] or the $ operator. You will encounter lists when fitting lavaan models (a fitted model object is a list containing coefficients, residuals, and fit indices). For now, know the distinction: c() creates a vector; list() creates a list.

title: R basics
text:
Common errors






WarningObject not found



Error: object 'score' not found — you have either not run the line that creates the object, or you have misspelled the name (remember R is case-sensitive).

Could not find function
Error in foo(): could not find function "foo" — the function does not exist, or the package that provides it has not been loaded with library(). In Tutorial 02 we cover package management in detail.


Unexpected NA in results
Your result is NA when you expected a number. Add na.rm = TRUE to the function call (see above).

title: R basics
text:
Exercises

Create a numeric vector reaction_times <- c(245, 312, 198, 401, 287, 356). Calculate the mean, standard deviation, and median. Round the mean to two decimal places using round().
Using the same reaction_times vector, use logical indexing to extract only the reaction times below 300 ms. How many are there?
Create a data frame participants with three columns: id (integers 1 to 5), condition (factor with levels “control” and “treatment”), and score (numeric values of your choice). Use summary() to inspect it.
Add a missing value to your score column (set the third participant’s score to NA). Recalculate the mean score using na.rm = TRUE. How many complete cases are there?







CautionSolutions





Exercise 1

reaction_times <- c(245, 312, 198, 401, 287, 356)
mean(reaction_times)

## [1] 299.8333

sd(reaction_times)

## [1] 73.59189

median(reaction_times)

## [1] 299.5

round(mean(reaction_times), 2)

## [1] 299.83


Exercise 2

fast <- reaction_times[reaction_times < 300]
fast

## [1] 245 198 287

length(fast)

## [1] 3


Exercise 3

participants <- data.frame(
  id        = 1:5,
  condition = factor(c("control", "treatment", "control", "treatment", "control")),
  score     = c(3.2, 4.1, 2.8, 3.9, 3.5)
)
summary(participants)

##        id        condition     score    
##  Min.   :1   control  :3   Min.   :2.8  
##  1st Qu.:2   treatment:2   1st Qu.:3.2  
##  Median :3                 Median :3.5  
##  Mean   :3                 Mean   :3.5  
##  3rd Qu.:4                 3rd Qu.:3.9  
##  Max.   :5                 Max.   :4.1


Exercise 4

participants$score[3] <- NA
mean(participants$score, na.rm = TRUE)

## [1] 3.675

sum(complete.cases(participants))

## [1] 4

title: R basics
text:
Summary & next tutorial
You now have the building blocks of R: objects, vectors, data frames, factors, and missing values. These are the raw materials you will use in every subsequent tutorial. In the next tutorial we set up RStudio — the environment where you will write and run all of your R code — and install the packages (lavaan, haven, ggplot2, and friends) that we will need from Tutorial 03 onward.

title: R basics
text:
Further reading

R Core Team (2024) — the official R documentation, available at https://www.r-project.org/.
Wickham (2019) — a deeper treatment of R’s data structures and semantics, aimed at those who want to understand the language thoroughly.
Dalgaard (2008) — an introductory statistics textbook that uses R throughout; particularly useful if you want to see R applied to the kinds of tests covered in Tutorial 04.

title: Importing data
text:
By the end of this tutorial you will be able to:

read an SPSS .sav file into R with haven::read_sav() and convert labelled columns to factors
read a comma-separated .csv file with read.csv() and an Excel .xlsx file with readxl::read_excel()
inspect an imported dataset to confirm it has loaded correctly
handle missing-value codes (-99, 999, blank cells) so they become R’s NA
write a data frame back to CSV with write.csv()

title: Importing data
text:
By the end of this tutorial you will be able to:

read an SPSS .sav file into R with haven::read_sav() and convert labelled columns to factors
read a comma-separated .csv file with read.csv() and an Excel .xlsx file with readxl::read_excel()
inspect an imported dataset to confirm it has loaded correctly
handle missing-value codes (-99, 999, blank cells) so they become R’s NA
write a data frame back to CSV with write.csv()

title: Importing data
text:
Setup
This tutorial loads the haven and readxl packages. We also load psych for psych::describe(), which gives richer descriptive statistics than base summary().

library(haven)
library(readxl)
library(psych)

title: Importing data
text:
The teaching dataset
Throughout this tutorial series we use a simulated dataset based on a weekly diary study of job demands and employee well-being. The data file is shipped with this site and is also the dataset used in the original Mplus teaching materials this series is based on.
The dataset contains 343 rows (one per participant) and 25 columns covering:

Job control — 6 items (con1, con3, con6, con8, con9, con10)
Workplace social support — 4 items (sup2, sup4, sup5, sup7)
Problem-solving demands — 4 items (prob1–prob4)
Creativity — 3 items (creat1–creat3)
Negative affect — 3 items (na1, na2, na3)
Positive affect — 3 items (pa1, pa2, pa3)
Gender (0 = Male, 1 = Female)
Age band (1 = 25 or under, through 5 = 56 or over)

Full variable definitions, response scales, and source citations are in the data note.
We ship the dataset in two formats so you can practise both import paths:

data/ps_data_reduced.sav — the SPSS file
data/ps_data_reduced.csv — a CSV mirror

If you downloaded this site’s repository, both files are in the data/ folder. The code chunks below assume your working directory is the project root, so data/ps_data_reduced.sav is the correct relative path.

title: Importing data
text:
Reading SPSS files with haven
The haven package reads SPSS, Stata, and SAS files into R without needing SPSS itself installed. The main function is read_sav():

ps_sav <- haven::read_sav("data/ps_data_reduced.sav")

Inspect what you have:

class(ps_sav)

## [1] "tbl_df"     "tbl"        "data.frame"

dim(ps_sav)

## [1] 343  25

names(ps_sav)

##  [1] "con1"   "sup2"   "con3"   "sup4"   "sup5"   "con6"   "sup7"   "con8"  
##  [9] "con9"   "con10"  "prob1"  "prob2"  "prob3"  "prob4"  "creat1" "creat2"
## [17] "creat3" "na1"    "pa1"    "na2"    "pa2"    "na3"    "pa3"    "gender"
## [25] "age"


read_sav() returns a tibble, which is a modern data frame with slightly nicer printing. It behaves like a regular data frame for everything we will do in this series, so you can use $ and [ , ] indexing exactly as in Tutorial 01.

head(ps_sav[, 1:6], 3)

## # A tibble: 3 × 6
##   con1      sup2           con3           sup4      sup5           con6     
##   <dbl+lbl> <dbl+lbl>      <dbl+lbl>      <dbl+lbl> <dbl+lbl>      <dbl+lbl>
## 1 4 [4]     5 [Very often] 5 [Very often] 3 [3]     4 [4]          3 [3]    
## 2 4 [4]     5 [Very often] 3 [3]          4 [4]     5 [Very often] 3 [3]    
## 3 3 [3]     3 [3]          3 [3]          2 [2]     3 [3]          3 [3]


Look closely at the first few columns — they print as <dbl+lbl>. The +lbl indicates that these are labelled vectors: SPSS stores both the numeric value and a text label for each value (for example, 4 [4] or 5 [Very often]). You can see the labels with attr():

attr(ps_sav$con1, "labels")

##      Never          2          3          4 Very often 
##          1          2          3          4          5



Converting labelled columns to factors
Labelled vectors are useful for inspection but cause problems for downstream functions — including lavaan, which we will meet in Tutorial 05. The cleanest fix is to convert labelled columns to factors using haven::as_factor():

ps_sav_factor <- haven::as_factor(ps_sav)
class(ps_sav_factor$con1)

## [1] "factor"

head(ps_sav_factor$con1, 5)

## [1] 4 4 3 4 4
## Levels: Never 2 3 4 Very often


For the Likert items (con1–pa3) you usually want to keep them numeric because you will compute means, standard deviations, and factor loadings — all of which require numbers. The right approach is to strip the labels but keep the underlying numbers. The simplest way is zap_labels():

ps_sav_num <- haven::zap_labels(ps_sav)
class(ps_sav_num$con1)

## [1] "numeric"

head(ps_sav_num$con1, 5)

## [1] 4 4 3 4 4


Now the Likert items are plain numeric vectors. The demographic variables (gender, age) we will convert to factors when we need them, on a case-by-case basis.






Note



For the rest of this tutorial series we work with the numeric version of the PS dataset. The factor conversion is shown here so you know what to do when you have genuinely categorical variables in your own dissertation data.

title: Importing data
text:
Reading CSV files with read.csv()
CSV (comma-separated values) is the lingua franca of data files — every statistics package can read and write CSV. Base R provides read.csv():

ps_csv <- read.csv("data/ps_data_reduced.csv")

Inspect the result:

class(ps_csv)

## [1] "data.frame"

dim(ps_csv)

## [1] 343  25

names(ps_csv)[1:10]

##  [1] "con1"  "sup2"  "con3"  "sup4"  "sup5"  "con6"  "sup7"  "con8"  "con9" 
## [10] "con10"

head(ps_csv[, 1:5], 3)

##   con1 sup2 con3 sup4 sup5
## 1    4    5    5    3    4
## 2    4    5    3    4    5
## 3    3    3    3    2    3


The CSV reader has produced a plain data.frame (not a tibble) with no labels — just numbers. For our purposes this is exactly what we want.

Comparing the two imports
To confirm both import paths produced the same underlying data, compare the dimensions and the first few values of a column:

identical(dim(ps_sav_num), dim(ps_csv))

## [1] TRUE

identical(as.numeric(ps_sav_num$con1), ps_csv$con1)

## [1] FALSE


Both should be TRUE. If they are not, something has gone wrong in one of the imports — usually a missing-value code mismatch (see below).

title: Importing data
text:
Reading Excel files with readxl
Excel .xlsx files are common in dissertation work. The readxl package reads them cleanly. The main function is read_excel():

library(readxl)
df_xlsx <- read_excel("data/my_survey.xlsx")

read_excel() automatically detects whether the file is .xls or .xlsx and reads the first sheet by default. To read a specific sheet, pass its name or position:

df_sheet2 <- read_excel("data/my_survey.xlsx", sheet = "wave2")
df_sheet3 <- read_excel("data/my_survey.xlsx", sheet = 3)

You can also restrict the rows and columns read with the range argument, which takes Excel-style cell references:

df_subset <- read_excel("data/my_survey.xlsx", range = "A1:K343")







WarningExcel columns with mixed types



Excel is happy to mix numbers, dates, and text in the same column. R is not. If a column has a header row but then has text in some cells and numbers in others, read_excel() will silently coerce the whole column to character. Inspect every imported Excel file with str() before doing any analysis.

title: Importing data
text:
Inspecting the imported data
Whatever format you started from, after importing you should always run these four checks before doing anything else:

1. Dimensions

nrow(ps_csv)

## [1] 343

ncol(ps_csv)

## [1] 25

dim(ps_csv)

## [1] 343  25


Do these match what you expect? The PS dataset should have 343 rows and 25 columns. If you see 344 rows, you have accidentally imported the header row as data. If you see 26 columns, you have an extra blank column at the end.


2. Column names

names(ps_csv)

##  [1] "con1"   "sup2"   "con3"   "sup4"   "sup5"   "con6"   "sup7"   "con8"  
##  [9] "con9"   "con10"  "prob1"  "prob2"  "prob3"  "prob4"  "creat1" "creat2"
## [17] "creat3" "na1"    "pa1"    "na2"    "pa2"    "na3"    "pa3"    "gender"
## [25] "age"


Check that every variable name is present and spelled correctly. If a column name comes through as X or X1, you have a missing or duplicated header in the source file.


3. Structure
str() shows the type of every column in a compact form:

str(ps_csv)

## 'data.frame':    343 obs. of  25 variables:
##  $ con1  : int  4 4 3 4 4 5 4 4 3 4 ...
##  $ sup2  : int  5 5 3 4 4 5 2 4 4 4 ...
##  $ con3  : int  5 3 3 4 4 5 3 5 3 2 ...
##  $ sup4  : int  3 4 2 4 3 3 2 3 2 3 ...
##  $ sup5  : int  4 5 3 5 4 4 3 3 4 4 ...
##  $ con6  : int  3 3 3 4 4 4 2 5 2 3 ...
##  $ sup7  : int  2 3 3 4 3 4 2 3 3 2 ...
##  $ con8  : int  4 3 3 5 4 4 3 5 3 2 ...
##  $ con9  : int  4 2 2 5 4 3 3 4 2 2 ...
##  $ con10 : int  4 1 1 4 3 4 3 4 3 3 ...
##  $ prob1 : int  5 5 4 5 4 4 4 3 4 3 ...
##  $ prob2 : int  4 4 2 4 4 3 4 4 3 2 ...
##  $ prob3 : int  2 4 4 3 3 4 4 3 2 1 ...
##  $ prob4 : int  5 1 4 2 2 4 4 4 3 4 ...
##  $ creat1: int  4 5 3 5 2 3 2 3 2 4 ...
##  $ creat2: int  5 3 2 4 3 4 2 3 1 4 ...
##  $ creat3: int  4 4 2 5 3 4 2 3 1 4 ...
##  $ na1   : int  3 3 1 2 4 2 3 3 5 1 ...
##  $ pa1   : int  4 3 4 4 3 4 2 4 1 4 ...
##  $ na2   : int  2 4 2 2 3 1 3 2 4 2 ...
##  $ pa2   : int  5 5 3 5 3 4 3 3 1 3 ...
##  $ na3   : int  3 2 1 3 3 1 4 2 5 3 ...
##  $ pa3   : int  4 5 4 3 3 5 3 2 1 3 ...
##  $ gender: int  1 1 0 0 1 1 0 1 1 1 ...
##  $ age   : int  3 3 3 3 3 4 5 4 3 2 ...


All the Likert columns should be num (numeric). The gender and age columns will also be num here because the CSV file does not carry SPSS labels — you would convert them to factors yourself with factor() when you need to treat them as categorical.


4. Descriptive statistics
summary() gives a quick look at every column:

summary(ps_csv[, c("con1", "pa1", "gender", "age")])

##       con1            pa1            gender            age       
##  Min.   :2.000   Min.   :1.000   Min.   :0.0000   Min.   :1.000  
##  1st Qu.:3.000   1st Qu.:3.000   1st Qu.:0.0000   1st Qu.:2.000  
##  Median :4.000   Median :4.000   Median :0.0000   Median :3.000  
##  Mean   :3.994   Mean   :3.632   Mean   :0.4639   Mean   :3.155  
##  3rd Qu.:5.000   3rd Qu.:4.000   3rd Qu.:1.0000   3rd Qu.:4.000  
##  Max.   :5.000   Max.   :6.000   Max.   :1.0000   Max.   :5.000  
##                  NAs    :1       NAs    :11       NAs    :13


For richer descriptives — skew, kurtosis, standard error of the mean — use psych::describe():

psych::describe(ps_csv[, c("con1", "pa1", "pa2", "pa3")])

##      vars   n mean   sd median trimmed  mad min max range  skew kurtosis   se
## con1    1 343 3.99 0.81      4    4.02 1.48   2   5     3 -0.26    -0.81 0.04
## pa1     2 342 3.63 1.20      4    3.67 1.48   1   6     5 -0.24    -0.51 0.06
## pa2     3 338 3.39 1.28      3    3.40 1.48   1   6     5  0.00    -0.80 0.07
## pa3     4 332 3.45 1.24      3    3.44 1.48   1   6     5  0.10    -0.60 0.07


The describe() output includes n, mean, sd, median, trimmed (10% trimmed mean), mad (median absolute deviation), min, max, skew, kurtosis, and se. The skew and kurtosis columns are particularly useful when you are deciding whether to use the MLR robust estimator in lavaan (Tutorial 05 onwards).






TipTry it



Use psych::describe() on the six job control items (con1, con3, con6, con8, con9, con10). Which item has the largest absolute skew?








CautionSolution






psych::describe(ps_csv[, c("con1", "con3", "con6", "con8", "con9", "con10")])

##       vars   n mean   sd median trimmed  mad min max range  skew kurtosis   se
## con1     1 343 3.99 0.81      4    4.02 1.48   2   5     3 -0.26    -0.81 0.04
## con3     2 343 3.91 0.92      4    3.97 1.48   1   5     4 -0.45    -0.49 0.05
## con6     3 343 3.82 0.91      4    3.87 1.48   2   5     3 -0.20    -0.90 0.05
## con8     4 343 4.00 0.83      4    4.05 1.48   2   5     3 -0.44    -0.51 0.04
## con9     5 343 3.74 0.96      4    3.81 1.48   1   5     4 -0.35    -0.56 0.05
## con10    6 343 3.09 1.17      3    3.11 1.48   1   5     4 -0.08    -0.85 0.06


Look at the skew column. The item with the largest absolute value of skew is the most skewed. In this dataset, all six items are reasonably symmetric (skew near zero), which is typical of 5-point Likert scales with a central tendency.

title: Importing data
text:
Handling missing values
Real survey data has missing values. The PS dataset uses -99 as its missing-value code (you can see this in the original Mplus syntax: MISSING ARE ALL (-99)).

When haven reads SPSS
SPSS stores missing-value codes in the file metadata, so haven::read_sav() automatically converts them to R’s NA — no extra step needed:

sum(is.na(ps_sav_num$con1))

## [1] 0




When you read CSV
A CSV file is just text, so R has no way to know that -99 means “missing” — it will import -99 as the number negative ninety-nine, which is a perfectly valid Likert response if you forget to flag it. You handle this in one of two ways.
Option A: at import time, with the na argument.

ps_csv_na <- read.csv("data/ps_data_reduced.csv",
                      na = c("-99", "999", ""))

The na argument tells R which values to treat as NA. List every missing-value code your dataset uses. The empty string "" handles genuinely blank cells.
Option B: after import, with subsetting.
If you already imported the data without specifying na, you can convert the missing-value code to NA after the fact:

ps_csv[ps_csv == -99] <- NA

This works for every column at once. Be careful: it will convert any -99 in any column, including a column where -99 might genuinely be a valid value (rare in survey data, but worth a moment’s thought).






WarningCheck before you convert



Before replacing -99 with NA, run table(ps_csv$con1, useNA = "ifany") to see the frequency of each value, including NA. If -99 is the most common value in some column, you have probably misunderstood the missing-value code — check the data note for that variable.




Verifying the missing-value conversion
After applying the missing-value code, count the NAs in each column:

col_missing <- sapply(ps_csv, function(x) sum(is.na(x)))
col_missing[col_missing > 0]

##  prob2  prob3  prob4 creat1 creat2 creat3    na1    pa1    na2    pa2    na3 
##      1      1      1      1      1      1      2      1      4      5     11 
##    pa3 gender    age 
##     11     11     13


This shows only the columns that have at least one missing value, alongside the count. If a column you expected to have missing values shows zero, the missing-value code has not been correctly applied.

title: Importing data
text:
Writing data back to CSV
Once you have imported, cleaned, and converted your data, you often want to save the cleaned version so you do not have to repeat the cleaning steps. Use write.csv():

write.csv(ps_csv, "data/ps_clean.csv", row.names = FALSE)

The row.names = FALSE argument is important — without it, R prepends an extra column of row numbers (1, 2, 3, …) to the file, which is rarely useful and breaks SPSS’s CSV importer.






Note



For dissertation work, keeping a clear separation between raw data (never edit it), cleaned data (a CSV you write from a script), and analysis output (a Quarto document) is a habit that pays off. Your raw data file stays pristine; your cleaning steps are documented in code; your analysis is reproducible from a single Render button click.

title: Importing data
text:
Common errors






Warningcannot open file 'data/ps_data_reduced.sav': No such file or directory



Your working directory is not the project root. Run getwd() in the Console. If it does not end in your project folder name, you are in the wrong place — use Session → Set Working Directory → To Project Directory in RStudio, or restart RStudio in the project.

Error: zip file 'data/file.xlsx' cannot be opened
The path is wrong, or the file is not actually an Excel .xlsx — it might be a .xls or a .csv with the wrong extension. Open it in Excel to verify, then re-import with the correct function.


All columns come through as character
Your CSV or Excel file has at least one text value in a numeric column — maybe a "NA" written as text, or a stray comment. Open the file in a spreadsheet, find the text value, and either fix it or list it in the na argument of read.csv().


Missing-value counts do not match what you expected
You imported the CSV without specifying na = c("-99", ...). Re-import with the na argument, or post-process with df[df == -99] <- NA as shown above.

title: Importing data
text:
Exercises

Import the PS dataset from the SPSS file (data/ps_data_reduced.sav) using haven::read_sav(). Use haven::zap_labels() to get plain numeric columns. Confirm that the result has 343 rows and 25 columns.
Import the same dataset from the CSV file (data/ps_data_reduced.csv) using read.csv() with na = c("-99", "999", ""). Compare the number of NA values in con1 between the SPSS import and the CSV import — they should be identical.
Use psych::describe() on the three creativity items (creat1, creat2, creat3). Report the mean and standard deviation of each item, rounded to two decimal places.
The PS dataset has 11 missing-value codes of -99 across its columns (you can find them with sum(is.na(ps_csv))). Write a one-line command that returns the names of every column containing at least one NA.







CautionSolutions





Exercise 1

ps_sav <- haven::read_sav("data/ps_data_reduced.sav")
ps_sav_num <- haven::zap_labels(ps_sav)
dim(ps_sav_num)

## [1] 343  25


dim() returns 343  25 — 343 rows and 25 columns, as expected.
Exercise 2

ps_sav_con1_na <- sum(is.na(ps_sav_num$con1))
ps_csv_na <- read.csv("data/ps_data_reduced.csv", na = c("-99", "999", ""))
ps_csv_con1_na <- sum(is.na(ps_csv_na$con1))
ps_sav_con1_na

## [1] 0

ps_csv_con1_na

## [1] 0

identical(ps_sav_con1_na, ps_csv_con1_na)

## [1] TRUE


Both should report the same count of missing values for con1, and identical() returns TRUE.
Exercise 3

creat_desc <- psych::describe(ps_csv[, c("creat1", "creat2", "creat3")])
round(creat_desc[, c("mean", "sd")], 2)

##        mean   sd
## creat1 3.09 0.98
## creat2 3.07 0.96
## creat3 3.06 0.99


Exercise 4

names(ps_csv)[sapply(ps_csv, function(x) any(is.na(x)))]

##  [1] "prob2"  "prob3"  "prob4"  "creat1" "creat2" "creat3" "na1"    "pa1"   
##  [9] "na2"    "pa2"    "na3"    "pa3"    "gender" "age"


This returns a character vector of column names where at least one value is NA. The sapply() applies the test any(is.na(x)) to each column, returning a logical vector the same length as names(ps_csv); the outer subscript keeps only the TRUE positions.

title: Importing data
text:
Summary & next tutorial
You can now bring data into R from SPSS, CSV, and Excel files; check that the import worked; handle missing-value codes; and save a cleaned data frame back to CSV. In the next tutorial we put this data to work, running simple inferential tests — t-tests, ANOVA, and regression with lm() — and we meet ggplot2 for the first time as a way to visualise the results.

title: Importing data
text:
Further reading

R Core Team (2024) — see the manual R Data Import/Export, available from https://cran.r-project.org/manuals.html.
Wickham (2019) — covers data structures and the role of coercion, relevant for understanding labelled vectors.
Dalgaard (2008) — Chapter 1 covers data entry and import with worked examples.

title: Introducing lavaan
text:
By the end of this tutorial you will be able to:

describe the declarative model-specification mindset that underpins lavaan
write lavaan model syntax using the four core operators: =~, ~, ~~, and ~ 1
label parameters and define new derived quantities with :=
distinguish the three entry-point functions cfa(), sem(), and lavaan() and know when to use each
fit a simple one-factor CFA on the PS dataset with MLR estimation and read the basic summary() output
set the scale of a latent variable using either the marker-variable method or std.lv = TRUE

title: Introducing lavaan
text:
By the end of this tutorial you will be able to:

describe the declarative model-specification mindset that underpins lavaan
write lavaan model syntax using the four core operators: =~, ~, ~~, and ~ 1
label parameters and define new derived quantities with :=
distinguish the three entry-point functions cfa(), sem(), and lavaan() and know when to use each
fit a simple one-factor CFA on the PS dataset with MLR estimation and read the basic summary() output
set the scale of a latent variable using either the marker-variable method or std.lv = TRUE

title: Introducing lavaan
text:
Setup
This is the first tutorial that uses lavaan. We load it alongside haven (for data import) and psych (for descriptives). The setup chunk sets the knit root directory to the project folder so the data/ paths work, and applies the ## output comment prefix we have used since Tutorial 01.

library(haven)
library(lavaan)
library(psych)

We import the PS dataset and strip the SPSS labels, exactly as in Tutorial 03. From this tutorial onward we will use this same setup block at the top of every tutorial that analyses the PS data — in later tutorials it will be hidden inside a collapsed <details> tag so it does not dominate the page.

title: Introducing lavaan
text:
A declarative mindset
In Tutorial 04 you used lm() to fit a regression. You wrote a formula — creat ~ pa + con — that described the model you wanted, and R’s lm() function worked out how to estimate it. You never told R how to compute the coefficients; you only told it what model you wanted.
lavaan works the same way, but it extends the formula idea to a much richer class of models. You write a model syntax string that describes the relationships among your variables — both observed and latent — and lavaan works out how to estimate the parameters. This is called declarative model specification: you declare what the model is, not how to fit it.
The shift in mindset is important. In a menu-driven statistics package you think about which buttons to click and which dialogue boxes to fill in. In lavaan you think about what model you want to specify and you write that specification down as text. The text is the model. If you share your syntax with a colleague, they can see exactly what model you fitted — there is no hidden configuration to explain.

The model syntax string
A lavaan model is written as a single character string — a piece of text enclosed in single or double quotes. Inside that string, you describe the model using a small set of operators. The string can span multiple lines, which makes it readable:

model <- '
  pa =~ pa1 + pa2 + pa3
  creat ~ pa
'

We always use string-literal model syntax in this series — never the unquoted form — because it makes clear that what you are writing is a model specification, not an R expression. It also lets you combine multiple operators cleanly across multiple lines.

title: Introducing lavaan
text:
The four core operators
lavaan’s syntax revolves around four operators. Master these and you can specify almost any model in this tutorial series.

=~ — “is measured by” (latent variable definition)
The =~ operator defines a latent variable by listing its indicators. Read it as “is measured by”.

pa =~ pa1 + pa2 + pa3

This line says: “a latent variable called pa is measured by the observed variables pa1, pa2, and pa3.” In the language of confirmatory factor analysis, pa is a factor with three indicators, and the coefficients linking the factor to its indicators are factor loadings (\(\lambda\)).
The latent variable’s name (pa) is something you choose — it can be any valid R name. The indicator names (pa1, pa2, pa3) must match columns in your data frame.


~ — “is regressed on” (regression path)
The ~ operator defines a regression path. Read it as “is regressed on”. This is the same operator you met in Tutorial 04 with lm(), and it means the same thing.

creat ~ pa

This line says: “the observed variable creat is regressed on pa.” The coefficient is a regression weight (\(\beta\)). The variable on the left is the outcome (endogenous); the variable on the right is the predictor.
You can have multiple predictors on the right, separated by +:

creat ~ pa + con

You can also have a latent variable on either side of ~, which is what makes this a structural equation model rather than a plain regression:

creat ~ pa
pa ~ con



~~ — “is correlated with” (covariance)
The ~~ operator specifies a covariance — a non-directional relationship — between two variables. Read it as “is correlated with”.

pa ~~ na

This line says: “the latent variable pa is correlated with the latent variable na.” The parameter is a covariance, not a regression coefficient. In a CFA, the covariances among factors are usually freely estimated — that is what ~~ does.
A variable can also be correlated with itself:

pa ~~ pa

This specifies the variance of pa. lavaan does this for you automatically for exogenous variables, so you rarely need to write it explicitly. But it is useful to know that variance is just a covariance of a variable with itself.


~ 1 — intercept / mean
The ~ 1 operator specifies an intercept (a mean). You will rarely write this yourself because lavaan estimates intercepts for observed variables automatically. It becomes relevant when you want to estimate or constrain latent means, which we cover briefly in Tutorial 13.

pa ~ 1

This line says: “estimate the mean of pa.” For latent variables, lavaan fixes the mean to 0 by default; you free it by passing meanstructure = TRUE to cfa() or sem() and then writing latent ~ 1.

title: Introducing lavaan
text:
Labelling parameters with *
To constrain, compare, or define new parameters, you need to label individual parameters. You do this by placing a label and a * before the variable name on the right-hand side of =~, ~, or ~~.

creat ~ b * pa + c * con
pa   ~ a * con

Here the path from pa to creat is labelled b, the path from con to creat is labelled c, and the path from con to pa is labelled a. Once a parameter has a label, you can refer to it by that label elsewhere in the model — most commonly to define an indirect effect.
You can also fix a parameter to a specific value with a number and *:

pa =~ 1 * pa1 + pa2 + pa3

This fixes the loading of pa1 on pa to exactly 1 — the marker-variable method for setting the latent variable’s scale. This is lavaan’s default behaviour for the first indicator, so you rarely need to write it out explicitly, but it is worth understanding what the default is doing behind the scenes.

title: Introducing lavaan
text:
Defining new parameters with :=
The := operator defines a new parameter as a function of existing labelled parameters. The classic use is defining an indirect effect in a mediation model.

indirect := a * b
direct   := c
total    := c + (a * b)

These three lines define:

indirect as the product \(a \times b\) (the indirect effect of con on creat through pa)
direct as the coefficient \(c\) (the direct effect of con on creat)
total as the sum \(c + ab\) (the total effect)

lavaan estimates these derived parameters and gives you standard errors, confidence intervals, and p-values for them, just as it does for the directly-specified parameters. We use := extensively in Tutorials 11 and 12 on mediation.






Note



The := operator is a lavaan-specific extension. It does not exist in base R’s formula interface. It is one of the features that makes lavaan well-suited to mediation analysis — you can define any algebraic combination of model parameters and lavaan will estimate it with appropriate uncertainty.

title: Introducing lavaan
text:
The three entry-point functions
lavaan provides three functions that take a model syntax string and a data frame and return a fitted model object. They differ only in their default settings.

cfa() — confirmatory factor analysis
Use cfa() when your model contains only =~ statements (latent variables defined by indicators) and ~~ statements (covariances among latents and residual covariances among indicators). There are no regression paths (~) between variables.

fit <- cfa(model, data = ps)

cfa() sets sensible defaults for a pure measurement model: factor covariances are freely estimated, residual covariances default to zero, and the first loading of each factor is fixed to 1.


sem() — structural equation model
Use sem() when your model contains both a measurement part (=~) and a structural part (~). This is the most general case and covers everything from path analysis with observed variables to full SEM with latent variables.

fit <- sem(model, data = ps)

sem() and cfa() are nearly identical in their defaults; the main difference is that sem() does not assume every latent variable is exogenous, so it will not freely estimate covariances among latent variables that are predicted by other variables in the model. Use sem() for any model that includes ~ paths between latent variables.


lavaan() — the general function
cfa() and sem() are both wrappers around lavaan(), the underlying fitting function. If you want full control over every default, call lavaan() directly. For this series you will not need to — cfa() and sem() cover everything we do.

fit <- lavaan(model, data = ps,
              model.type = "sem",
              auto.fix.first = TRUE,
              auto.var = TRUE,
              ordered = NULL,
              estimator = "MLR",
              se = "standard",
              missing = "ml")







NoteWhich function should I use?



For Tutorials 06–08 (CFA, parcels, second-order) use cfa(). For Tutorials 09–12 (path analysis, SEM, mediation) use sem(). For Tutorial 15 (growth curves) use growth(). You will not need to call lavaan() directly in this series.

title: Introducing lavaan
section: A worked example: one-factor CFA on positive affect
text:
A worked example: one-factor CFA on positive affect
Let us put the syntax together. We fit a one-factor CFA on the three positive-affect items, using the MLR estimator (our default throughout this series — see the callout below).

model_pa <- '
  PA =~ pa1 + pa2 + pa3
'

fit_pa <- cfa(model_pa, data = ps, estimator = "MLR")
summary(fit_pa, standardized = TRUE)

## lavaan 0.6-21 ended normally after 17 iterations
## 
##   Estimator                                         ML
##   Optimization method                           NLMINB
##   Number of model parameters                         6
## 
##                                                   Used       Total
##   Number of observations                           332         343
## 
## Model Test User Model:
##                                               Standard      Scaled
##   Test Statistic                                 0.000       0.000
##   Degrees of freedom                                 0           0
## 
## Parameter Estimates:
## 
##   Standard errors                             Sandwich
##   Information bread                           Observed
##   Observed information based on                Hessian
## 
## Latent Variables:
##                    Estimate  Std.Err  z-value  P(>|z|)   Std.lv  Std.all
##   PA =~                                                                 
##     pa1               1.000                               0.956    0.806
##     pa2               1.036    0.074   14.009    0.000    0.990    0.772
##     pa3               1.100    0.083   13.310    0.000    1.051    0.850
## 
## Variances:
##                    Estimate  Std.Err  z-value  P(>|z|)   Std.lv  Std.all
##    .pa1               0.492    0.082    5.992    0.000    0.492    0.350
##    .pa2               0.665    0.074    9.021    0.000    0.665    0.404
##    .pa3               0.426    0.066    6.412    0.000    0.426    0.278
##     PA                0.914    0.112    8.171    0.000    1.000    1.000



Reading the output
The summary() output has several sections:

Header — lavaan version, optimisation info, number of observations, the estimator, and the model syntax.
Parameter estimates — a table with one row per parameter. The Estimate column gives the unstandardised value, Std.Err its standard error, z the Wald statistic, and P(>|z|) the two-tailed p-value. The std.all column (added because we passed standardized = TRUE) gives the fully standardised estimate — for loadings, this is the correlation between the indicator and the factor.
Variances — the variance of the latent variable (\(\psi\)) and the residual variances of the indicators (\(\theta\)).

The first loading (pa1) is fixed to 1 — you can see it has no standard error or p-value. This is the marker-variable method: the latent variable’s scale is set by fixing one loading, and the latent variance is freely estimated.






NoteWhy MLR?



We use estimator = "MLR" — maximum likelihood with robust (Satorra–Bentler) standard errors — as our default throughout this series. Likert-scale items are rarely perfectly normal, and MLR corrects the standard errors and the model \(\chi^2\) for non-normality. The point estimates are identical to plain ML; only the standard errors and the \(\chi^2\) test change. The one exception is when we use bootstrapping in Tutorial 12, where we must switch back to ML because MLR is incompatible with se = "boot".




Fit indices
So far we have only seen the parameter estimates. To assess whether the model fits the data, we look at fit indices. We extract the canonical set with a named fitMeasures() call:

fitMeasures(fit_pa, c("chisq", "df", "pvalue",
                      "cfi", "tli",
                      "rmsea", "rmsea.ci.lower", "rmsea.ci.upper",
                      "srmr"))

##          chisq             df         pvalue            cfi            tli 
##              0              0             NA              1              1 
##          rmsea rmsea.ci.lower rmsea.ci.upper           srmr 
##              0              0              0              0


A one-factor model with three indicators is just-identified (zero degrees of freedom), so the fit is perfect by construction — the \(\chi^2\) is zero, CFI = TLI = 1, RMSEA = 0, SRMR = 0. We cannot reject this model because it has no degrees of freedom to test. We will meet overidentified models with interpretable fit indices in Tutorial 07.






TipTry it



Fit a one-factor CFA on the three negative-affect items (na1, na2, na3) using the same syntax pattern as above. Read the summary() output. What is the standardised loading of na2 on the neg factor?








CautionSolution






model_na <- '
  neg =~ na1 + na2 + na3
'
fit_na <- cfa(model_na, data = ps, estimator = "MLR")
summary(fit_na, standardized = TRUE)

## lavaan 0.6-21 ended normally after 14 iterations
## 
##   Estimator                                         ML
##   Optimization method                           NLMINB
##   Number of model parameters                         6
## 
##                                                   Used       Total
##   Number of observations                           330         343
## 
## Model Test User Model:
##                                               Standard      Scaled
##   Test Statistic                                 0.000       0.000
##   Degrees of freedom                                 0           0
## 
## Parameter Estimates:
## 
##   Standard errors                             Sandwich
##   Information bread                           Observed
##   Observed information based on                Hessian
## 
## Latent Variables:
##                    Estimate  Std.Err  z-value  P(>|z|)   Std.lv  Std.all
##   neg =~                                                                
##     na1               1.000                               0.944    0.845
##     na2               0.944    0.058   16.298    0.000    0.891    0.823
##     na3               0.986    0.056   17.631    0.000    0.931    0.835
## 
## Variances:
##                    Estimate  Std.Err  z-value  P(>|z|)   Std.lv  Std.all
##    .na1               0.358    0.047    7.642    0.000    0.358    0.287
##    .na2               0.377    0.049    7.624    0.000    0.377    0.322
##    .na3               0.376    0.043    8.771    0.000    0.376    0.303
##     neg               0.891    0.084   10.631    0.000    1.000    1.000


Find the row neg =~ na2 in the parameter estimates table and read the std.all column. That value — between 0 and 1 — is the standardised loading. The closer it is to 1, the more strongly na2 reflects the underlying negative-affect factor.

title: Introducing lavaan
section: Setting the scale of a latent variable
text:
Setting the scale of a latent variable
Every latent variable needs a scale — a unit of measurement — because it is not directly observed. There are two ways to set it, and you must choose one for every latent variable in your model.

Marker-variable method (lavaan’s default)
Fix one factor loading to 1. The latent variable then has the same scale as that indicator. The latent variance is freely estimated. This is what lavaan does by default — it fixes the first indicator’s loading to 1.

PA =~ pa1 + pa2 + pa3     # pa1's loading is fixed to 1 by default



Standardised latent variable (std.lv = TRUE)
Fix the latent variance to 1. All factor loadings are then freely estimated. The latent variable is in standard-deviation units. Use this when you want the loadings themselves to carry the scale information.

fit_pa_stdlv <- cfa(model_pa, data = ps, estimator = "MLR", std.lv = TRUE)
summary(fit_pa_stdlv, standardized = TRUE)

## lavaan 0.6-21 ended normally after 14 iterations
## 
##   Estimator                                         ML
##   Optimization method                           NLMINB
##   Number of model parameters                         6
## 
##                                                   Used       Total
##   Number of observations                           332         343
## 
## Model Test User Model:
##                                               Standard      Scaled
##   Test Statistic                                 0.000       0.000
##   Degrees of freedom                                 0           0
## 
## Parameter Estimates:
## 
##   Standard errors                             Sandwich
##   Information bread                           Observed
##   Observed information based on                Hessian
## 
## Latent Variables:
##                    Estimate  Std.Err  z-value  P(>|z|)   Std.lv  Std.all
##   PA =~                                                                 
##     pa1               0.956    0.058   16.343    0.000    0.956    0.806
##     pa2               0.990    0.054   18.503    0.000    0.990    0.772
##     pa3               1.051    0.056   18.705    0.000    1.051    0.850
## 
## Variances:
##                    Estimate  Std.Err  z-value  P(>|z|)   Std.lv  Std.all
##    .pa1               0.492    0.082    5.992    0.000    0.492    0.350
##    .pa2               0.665    0.074    9.021    0.000    0.665    0.404
##    .pa3               0.426    0.066    6.412    0.000    0.426    0.278
##     PA                1.000                               1.000    1.000


Notice that with std.lv = TRUE, all three loadings are estimated (none is fixed to 1), and the latent variance is fixed at 1. The standardised loadings (std.all) are identical between the two methods — only the unstandardised estimates differ.






Note



For the rest of this series we use the marker-variable method (lavaan’s default). We mention std.lv = TRUE here so you recognise it when you see it in papers or in the cheat sheet. Both methods give the same standardised solution and the same fit indices; they differ only in how the unstandardised parameters are scaled.

title: Introducing lavaan
text:
A concept diagram
The model we just fitted — one latent factor measured by three indicators — looks like this:




A one-factor CFA with three indicators. The circle is the latent variable PA; the squares are the observed indicators. Arrows from the latent factor to the indicators are the factor loadings (λ). The small circles on each indicator are the residual variances (θ).


In path diagrams throughout this series:

Circles represent latent variables — unobserved factors.
Squares represent observed variables — the items in your dataset.
Single-headed arrows represent regression paths (factor loadings or structural coefficients).
Small circles on observed variables represent measurement error (\(\theta\)) — the residual variance of each indicator.

This diagrammatic language is consistent across the SEM literature and we will use it throughout the series. We will produce these diagrams from fitted lavaan objects with tidySEM::graph_sem() from Tutorial 07 onward, and hand-author them with DiagrammeR::grViz() when we want to illustrate a concept before fitting the model.

title: Introducing lavaan
text:
Common errors






WarningError in lavaanify(...): syntax error in model syntax



There is a typo in your model string. Check that every =~, ~, ~~, and := is correctly placed, that variable names match columns in your data frame exactly, and that every line ends correctly (no trailing + with nothing after it).

Error: model is syntax-invalid
A more general syntax error. Run lavaanify(model) on your model string to see where lavaan fails to parse it.


lavaan WARNING: some observed variances are (at least) a factor 1000 times larger than others
Your variables are on wildly different scales — one might be in milliseconds and another in Likert units. Standardise the variables with scale() before fitting, or check whether you have accidentally included an ID column as a variable.


lavaan WARNING: model has not converged
The estimation algorithm could not find a stable solution. Usually a sign of model misspecification, underidentification, or a Heywood case (negative variance). We cover diagnosis and fixes in Tutorial 07.

title: Introducing lavaan
text:
Exercises

Write a lavaan model syntax string that defines two latent factors: PA measured by pa1, pa2, pa3, and neg measured by na1, na2, na3. Allow the two factors to correlate. Fit it with cfa() and MLR. Read the summary() output — what is the standardised correlation between PA and neg?
Refit the same two-factor model using std.lv = TRUE. Compare the standardised loadings to those from the marker-variable method. Are they the same or different? Why?
Extract the canonical set of fit indices (chisq, df, pvalue, cfi, tli, rmsea with its CI, and srmr) for the two-factor model from Exercise 1. How many degrees of freedom does this model have? Is the model just-identified or overidentified?
Write a model syntax string that adds a regression path from PA to NA. Fit it with sem() rather than cfa(). What changes in the output compared to Exercise 1? (Note: this is not a theoretically sensible model — it is just to practise the syntax.)







CautionSolutions





Exercise 1

model_2f <- '
  PA =~ pa1 + pa2 + pa3
  neg =~ na1 + na2 + na3
  PA ~~ neg
'
fit_2f <- cfa(model_2f, data = ps, estimator = "MLR")
summary(fit_2f, standardized = TRUE)

## lavaan 0.6-21 ended normally after 23 iterations
## 
##   Estimator                                         ML
##   Optimization method                           NLMINB
##   Number of model parameters                        13
## 
##                                                   Used       Total
##   Number of observations                           330         343
## 
## Model Test User Model:
##                                               Standard      Scaled
##   Test Statistic                                 9.870      10.059
##   Degrees of freedom                                 8           8
##   P-value (Chi-square)                           0.274       0.261
##   Scaling correction factor                                  0.981
##     Yuan-Bentler correction (Mplus variant)                       
## 
## Parameter Estimates:
## 
##   Standard errors                             Sandwich
##   Information bread                           Observed
##   Observed information based on                Hessian
## 
## Latent Variables:
##                    Estimate  Std.Err  z-value  P(>|z|)   Std.lv  Std.all
##   PA =~                                                                 
##     pa1               1.000                               0.973    0.829
##     pa2               1.000    0.061   16.280    0.000    0.972    0.764
##     pa3               1.086    0.067   16.105    0.000    1.056    0.856
##   neg =~                                                                
##     na1               1.000                               0.948    0.848
##     na2               0.935    0.058   16.071    0.000    0.887    0.819
##     na3               0.983    0.056   17.668    0.000    0.932    0.836
## 
## Covariances:
##                    Estimate  Std.Err  z-value  P(>|z|)   Std.lv  Std.all
##   PA ~~                                                                 
##     neg              -0.402    0.069   -5.856    0.000   -0.436   -0.436
## 
## Variances:
##                    Estimate  Std.Err  z-value  P(>|z|)   Std.lv  Std.all
##    .pa1               0.431    0.053    8.118    0.000    0.431    0.313
##    .pa2               0.676    0.071    9.560    0.000    0.676    0.417
##    .pa3               0.406    0.063    6.480    0.000    0.406    0.267
##    .na1               0.350    0.046    7.659    0.000    0.350    0.280
##    .na2               0.386    0.050    7.798    0.000    0.386    0.329
##    .na3               0.375    0.043    8.677    0.000    0.375    0.302
##     PA                0.946    0.101    9.358    0.000    1.000    1.000
##     neg               0.899    0.084   10.683    0.000    1.000    1.000


Look at the Covariances section of the output. The std.all value for PA ~~ neg is the standardised correlation between the two factors. It should be moderately negative — positive and negative affect tend to be inversely related.
Exercise 2

fit_2f_stdlv <- cfa(model_2f, data = ps, estimator = "MLR", std.lv = TRUE)
summary(fit_2f_stdlv, standardized = TRUE)

## lavaan 0.6-21 ended normally after 15 iterations
## 
##   Estimator                                         ML
##   Optimization method                           NLMINB
##   Number of model parameters                        13
## 
##                                                   Used       Total
##   Number of observations                           330         343
## 
## Model Test User Model:
##                                               Standard      Scaled
##   Test Statistic                                 9.870      10.059
##   Degrees of freedom                                 8           8
##   P-value (Chi-square)                           0.274       0.261
##   Scaling correction factor                                  0.981
##     Yuan-Bentler correction (Mplus variant)                       
## 
## Parameter Estimates:
## 
##   Standard errors                             Sandwich
##   Information bread                           Observed
##   Observed information based on                Hessian
## 
## Latent Variables:
##                    Estimate  Std.Err  z-value  P(>|z|)   Std.lv  Std.all
##   PA =~                                                                 
##     pa1               0.973    0.052   18.716    0.000    0.973    0.829
##     pa2               0.972    0.052   18.869    0.000    0.972    0.764
##     pa3               1.056    0.053   19.880    0.000    1.056    0.856
##   neg =~                                                                
##     na1               0.948    0.044   21.366    0.000    0.948    0.848
##     na2               0.887    0.052   16.990    0.000    0.887    0.819
##     na3               0.932    0.050   18.632    0.000    0.932    0.836
## 
## Covariances:
##                    Estimate  Std.Err  z-value  P(>|z|)   Std.lv  Std.all
##   PA ~~                                                                 
##     neg              -0.436    0.059   -7.364    0.000   -0.436   -0.436
## 
## Variances:
##                    Estimate  Std.Err  z-value  P(>|z|)   Std.lv  Std.all
##    .pa1               0.431    0.053    8.118    0.000    0.431    0.313
##    .pa2               0.676    0.071    9.560    0.000    0.676    0.417
##    .pa3               0.406    0.063    6.480    0.000    0.406    0.267
##    .na1               0.350    0.046    7.659    0.000    0.350    0.280
##    .na2               0.386    0.050    7.798    0.000    0.386    0.329
##    .na3               0.375    0.043    8.677    0.000    0.375    0.302
##     PA                1.000                               1.000    1.000
##     neg               1.000                               1.000    1.000


The std.all loadings are identical to those from the marker-variable method. The unstandardised loadings differ: in the marker-variable method, the first loading of each factor is fixed to 1; with std.lv = TRUE, all loadings are freely estimated and the latent variances are fixed to 1. Standardisation puts both parameterisations on the same footing — they describe the same model, just in different units.
Exercise 3

fitMeasures(fit_2f, c("chisq", "df", "pvalue",
                      "cfi", "tli",
                      "rmsea", "rmsea.ci.lower", "rmsea.ci.upper",
                      "srmr"))

##          chisq             df         pvalue            cfi            tli 
##          9.870          8.000          0.274          0.998          0.996 
##          rmsea rmsea.ci.lower rmsea.ci.upper           srmr 
##          0.027          0.000          0.073          0.015


With 6 observed variables, the covariance matrix has \(6 \times 7 / 2 = 21\) non-redundant elements. The model estimates 13 free parameters (4 loadings + 2 latent variances + 1 covariance + 6 residual variances), so \(df = 21 - 13 = 8\). A model with positive degrees of freedom is overidentified — it can be empirically tested against the data.
Exercise 4

model_reg <- '
  PA =~ pa1 + pa2 + pa3
  neg =~ na1 + na2 + na3
  neg ~ PA
'
fit_reg <- sem(model_reg, data = ps, estimator = "MLR")
summary(fit_reg, standardized = TRUE)

## lavaan 0.6-21 ended normally after 20 iterations
## 
##   Estimator                                         ML
##   Optimization method                           NLMINB
##   Number of model parameters                        13
## 
##                                                   Used       Total
##   Number of observations                           330         343
##
