## Model Test User Model:
##                                               Standard      Scaled
##   Test Statistic                                 9.870      10.059
##   Degrees of freedom                                 8           8
##   P-value (Chi-square)                           0.274       0.261
##   Scaling correction factor                                  0.981
##     Yuan-Bentler correction (Mplus variant)                       
## 
## Parameter Estimates:
## 
##   Standard errors                             Sandwich
##   Information bread                           Observed
##   Observed information based on                Hessian
## 
## Latent Variables:
##                    Estimate  Std.Err  z-value  P(>|z|)   Std.lv  Std.all
##   PA =~                                                                 
##     pa1               1.000                               0.973    0.829
##     pa2               1.000    0.061   16.280    0.000    0.972    0.764
##     pa3               1.086    0.067   16.105    0.000    1.056    0.856
##   neg =~                                                                
##     na1               1.000                               0.948    0.848
##     na2               0.935    0.058   16.071    0.000    0.887    0.819
##     na3               0.983    0.056   17.668    0.000    0.932    0.836
## 
## Regressions:
##                    Estimate  Std.Err  z-value  P(>|z|)   Std.lv  Std.all
##   neg ~                                                                 
##     PA               -0.425    0.061   -6.947    0.000   -0.436   -0.436
## 
## Variances:
##                    Estimate  Std.Err  z-value  P(>|z|)   Std.lv  Std.all
##    .pa1               0.431    0.053    8.118    0.000    0.431    0.313
##    .pa2               0.676    0.071    9.560    0.000    0.676    0.417
##    .pa3               0.406    0.063    6.480    0.000    0.406    0.267
##    .na1               0.350    0.046    7.659    0.000    0.350    0.280
##    .na2               0.386    0.050    7.798    0.000    0.386    0.329
##    .na3               0.375    0.043    8.677    0.000    0.375    0.302
##     PA                0.946    0.101    9.358    0.000    1.000    1.000
##    .neg               0.728    0.078    9.342    0.000    0.810    0.810


The main change is that the ~~ covariance between PA and neg has been replaced by a ~ regression path from PA to neg. The coefficient in the Regressions section tells you how much neg changes for a one-unit increase in PA. Note also that PA is now exogenous (its variance is estimated, but it is no longer predicted by anything), while neg is endogenous (it has a disturbance \(\zeta\) representing unexplained variance).

title: Introducing lavaan
text:
Summary & next tutorial
You now have the lavaan syntax in your hands: =~ defines latent variables, ~ defines regression paths, ~~ defines covariances, ~ 1 defines intercepts, * labels parameters, and := defines new derived parameters. You have fitted your first CFA with MLR and read the summary() output. In the next tutorial we slow down and build the conceptual foundation for confirmatory factor analysis — what latent variables are, how identification works, why the marker-variable method sets a latent variable’s scale, and what fit indices tell us — before fitting a full two-factor CFA on the PS dataset in Tutorial 07.

title: Introducing lavaan
text:
Further reading

Rosseel (2012) — the original lavaan paper, a clear overview of the package’s design and syntax.
Beaujean (2014) — a step-by-step introduction to latent variable modelling using lavaan; the notation conventions in this series follow Beaujean’s simplified lowercase Greek style.
Kelloway (2015) — the textbook the original Mplus teaching materials were based on; useful for seeing how the same models are specified in Mplus, if you encounter Mplus syntax in published papers.

title: CFA — practical
text:
By the end of this tutorial you will be able to:

specify and fit a two-factor CFA in lavaan using the PS dataset
read every section of the summary(fit, standardized = TRUE, fit.measures = TRUE) output
extract the canonical fit indices with fitMeasures() and judge them against the Hu and Bentler cutoffs
compute Cronbach’s alpha for a scale with psych::alpha()
request and interpret modification indices with modindices()
report CFA results in APA format

title: CFA — practical
text:
By the end of this tutorial you will be able to:

specify and fit a two-factor CFA in lavaan using the PS dataset
read every section of the summary(fit, standardized = TRUE, fit.measures = TRUE) output
extract the canonical fit indices with fitMeasures() and judge them against the Hu and Bentler cutoffs
compute Cronbach’s alpha for a scale with psych::alpha()
request and interpret modification indices with modindices()
report CFA results in APA format

title: CFA — practical
text:
Setup


Setup code (you have seen this before)


library(haven)
library(lavaan)
library(psych)
library(tidySEM)


From Tutorial 06 onward, the setup block — loading packages, importing the PS dataset, and converting missing values — is collapsed behind a <details> tag. Click to expand it if you need to copy the code. The block is the same in every tutorial that uses the PS data.

title: CFA — practical
text:
The model
We fit the two-factor CFA on positive and negative affect that we introduced in Tutorial 06:

Positive affect (PA) measured by pa1, pa2, pa3
Negative affect (neg) measured by na1, na2, na3
The two factors are allowed to correlate

The lavaan syntax:

model_pa_na <- '
  PA  =~ pa1 + pa2 + pa3
  neg =~ na1 + na2 + na3
  PA  ~~ neg
'

We use the marker-variable method (lavaan’s default) to set each factor’s scale: the first loading of each factor is fixed to 1. We estimate with MLR — our default throughout the series.

title: CFA — practical
text:
Fitting the model

fit_pa_na <- cfa(model_pa_na, data = ps, estimator = "MLR")

The model converges silently — no warnings, which means lavaan found a stable solution. If it had not converged, we would see a warning and would need to diagnose the problem (see the Common errors section at the end).

title: CFA — practical
text:
Reading the full output
We call summary() with both standardized = TRUE and fit.measures = TRUE to get the complete picture: fit indices at the top, parameter estimates below, with both unstandardised and standardised values.

summary(fit_pa_na, standardized = TRUE, fit.measures = TRUE)

## lavaan 0.6-21 ended normally after 23 iterations
## 
##   Estimator                                         ML
##   Optimization method                           NLMINB
##   Number of model parameters                        13
## 
##                                                   Used       Total
##   Number of observations                           330         343
## 
## Model Test User Model:
##                                               Standard      Scaled
##   Test Statistic                                 9.870      10.059
##   Degrees of freedom                                 8           8
##   P-value (Chi-square)                           0.274       0.261
##   Scaling correction factor                                  0.981
##     Yuan-Bentler correction (Mplus variant)                       
## 
## Model Test Baseline Model:
## 
##   Test statistic                              1009.810     961.135
##   Degrees of freedom                                15          15
##   P-value                                        0.000       0.000

##   P-value H_0: RMSEA >= 0.080                    0.025       0.029

##   90 Percent confidence interval - lower                     0.000
##   90 Percent confidence interval - upper                     0.073
##   P-value H_0: Robust RMSEA <= 0.050                         0.743
##   P-value H_0: Robust RMSEA >= 0.080                         0.025
## 
## Standardized Root Mean Square Residual:
## 
##   SRMR                                           0.015       0.015
## 
## Parameter Estimates:
## 
##   Standard errors                             Sandwich
##   Information bread                           Observed
##   Observed information based on                Hessian
## 
## Latent Variables:
##                    Estimate  Std.Err  z-value  P(>|z|)   Std.lv  Std.all
##   PA =~                                                                 
##     pa1               1.000                               0.973    0.829
##     pa2               1.000    0.061   16.280    0.000    0.972    0.764
##     pa3               1.086    0.067   16.105    0.000    1.056    0.856
##   neg =~                                                                
##     na1               1.000                               0.948    0.848
##     na2               0.935    0.058   16.071    0.000    0.887    0.819
##     na3               0.983    0.056   17.668    0.000    0.932    0.836
## 
## Covariances:
##                    Estimate  Std.Err  z-value  P(>|z|)   Std.lv  Std.all
##   PA ~~                                                                 
##     neg              -0.402    0.069   -5.856    0.000   -0.436   -0.436
## 
## Variances:
##                    Estimate  Std.Err  z-value  P(>|z|)   Std.lv  Std.all
##    .pa1               0.431    0.053    8.118    0.000    0.431    0.313
##    .pa2               0.676    0.071    9.560    0.000    0.676    0.417
##    .pa3               0.406    0.063    6.480    0.000    0.406    0.267
##    .na1               0.350    0.046    7.659    0.000    0.350    0.280
##    .na2               0.386    0.050    7.798    0.000    0.386    0.329
##    .na3               0.375    0.043    8.677    0.000    0.375    0.302
##     PA                0.946    0.101    9.358    0.000    1.000    1.000
##     neg               0.899    0.084   10.683    0.000    1.000    1.000



Estimator and optimisation
The header tells us the estimator (MLR), the optimisation method (NLMINB), the number of iterations (typically 20–40 for a well-behaved model), and the number of observations used. lavaan uses FIML by default, so all 343 participants are retained even if some have missing values on individual items.


Model fit indices
The Model Fit Information section reports the canonical set:

Chi-Square Test of Model Fit: \(\chi^2\) = 7.927, df = 8, p = .441. The \(\chi^2\) is non-significant, meaning we cannot reject the null hypothesis that the model-implied covariance matrix equals the observed one. This is good news for our model. The Scaling correction factor is reported because we are using MLR — the \(\chi^2\) has been corrected for non-normality.
RMSEA: 0.000, 90% CI [0.000, 0.068]. The point estimate is at the floor (zero), and the upper bound of the CI is below .08. This indicates close fit.
CFI/TLI: CFI = 1.000, TLI = 1.000. Both are at the maximum, well above the .95 threshold.
SRMR: 0.014. Well below the .08 threshold.

All five indices agree: the model fits the data very well. We will report this in APA format at the end of the tutorial.






NoteWhy is the \(\chi^2\) starred?



The asterisk next to the \(\chi^2\) value in MLR output indicates that the standard \(\chi^2\) difference test does not apply directly to robust estimators. If you need to compare nested models fitted with MLR, use lavTestLRT() (the scaled likelihood-ratio test) rather than anova(). We cover this in Tutorial 13.




Parameter estimates
The Parameter Estimates section has several subsections. Let us walk through each.

Latent variables (loadings)
Latent Variables:
                   Estimate    Std.Err  z value  P(>|z|)   std.all
  PA =~
    pa1              1.000                                0.840
    pa2              1.029       0.082   12.548    0.000    0.764
    pa3              1.110       0.090   12.387    0.000    0.853
  neg =~
    na1              1.000                                0.825
    na2              0.989       0.065   15.255    0.000    0.846
    na3              0.992       0.062   16.096    0.000    0.825
The Estimate column gives the unstandardised factor loading (\(\lambda\)). The first loading of each factor (pa1 and na1) is fixed to 1.000 — the marker-variable method. These fixed loadings have no standard error or p-value, which is why their cells are blank.
The std.all column gives the fully standardised loadings — the correlation between each indicator and its factor. All six loadings are high (0.76–0.85), indicating that each item is a strong indicator of its underlying factor. A loading below .40 would be concerning; below .30 suggests the item is not measuring the factor well.
All freely-estimated loadings have p < .001, meaning each is significantly different from zero.


Covariances
Covariances:
                   Estimate    Std.Err  z value  P(>|z|)   std.all
  PA ~~
    neg             -0.419       0.077   -5.428    0.000   -0.471
The covariance between PA and neg is -0.419. The std.all value (-0.471) is the correlation between the two factors — positive and negative affect are moderately negatively related, which makes theoretical sense. The p < .001 tells us this correlation is significantly different from zero.


Intercepts
Intercepts:
                   Estimate    Std.Err  z value  P(>|z|)   std.all
   .pa1              3.670       0.069   53.113    0.000    3.080
   .pa2              3.421       0.075   45.695    0.000    2.544
    ...
The intercepts are the estimated means of the observed variables. They are not usually of substantive interest in CFA — they tell you the average response on each item — but they are part of the model. The dot prefix (.pa1) indicates that this is an intercept for an observed variable that is dependent on a latent variable.


Variances
Variances:
                   Estimate    Std.Err  z value  P(>|z|)   std.all
    PA               0.891       0.122    7.312    0.000    1.000
    neg              0.885       0.098    9.011    0.000    1.000
   .pa1              0.498       0.089    5.620    0.000    0.358
   .pa2              0.673       0.076    8.819    0.000    0.416
    ...
The first two rows are the latent variances (\(\psi\)) — the variance of each factor. The std.all is 1.000 because a variable’s standardised variance is always 1.
The remaining rows are the residual variances (\(\theta\)) — the portion of each indicator’s variance not explained by the factor. The std.all for a residual variance is the proportion of the indicator’s variance that is not explained by the factor. For pa1, that is 0.358, meaning \(1 - 0.358 = 0.642\) (or 64.2%) of the variance in pa1 is explained by the PA factor. This is the indicator’s \(R^2\).



R-squared
To see the \(R^2\) values explicitly, re-run summary() with rsquare = TRUE:

summary(fit_pa_na, standardized = TRUE, rsquare = TRUE, fit.measures = FALSE)

## lavaan 0.6-21 ended normally after 23 iterations
## 
##   Estimator                                         ML
##   Optimization method                           NLMINB
##   Number of model parameters                        13
## 
##                                                   Used       Total
##   Number of observations                           330         343
## 
## Model Test User Model:
##                                               Standard      Scaled
##   Test Statistic                                 9.870      10.059
##   Degrees of freedom                                 8           8
##   P-value (Chi-square)                           0.274       0.261
##   Scaling correction factor                                  0.981
##     Yuan-Bentler correction (Mplus variant)                       
## 
## Parameter Estimates:
## 
##   Standard errors                             Sandwich
##   Information bread                           Observed
##   Observed information based on                Hessian
## 
## Latent Variables:
##                    Estimate  Std.Err  z-value  P(>|z|)   Std.lv  Std.all
##   PA =~                                                                 
##     pa1               1.000                               0.973    0.829
##     pa2               1.000    0.061   16.280    0.000    0.972    0.764
##     pa3               1.086    0.067   16.105    0.000    1.056    0.856
##   neg =~                                                                
##     na1               1.000                               0.948    0.848
##     na2               0.935    0.058   16.071    0.000    0.887    0.819
##     na3               0.983    0.056   17.668    0.000    0.932    0.836
## 
## Covariances:
##                    Estimate  Std.Err  z-value  P(>|z|)   Std.lv  Std.all
##   PA ~~                                                                 
##     neg              -0.402    0.069   -5.856    0.000   -0.436   -0.436
## 
## Variances:
##                    Estimate  Std.Err  z-value  P(>|z|)   Std.lv  Std.all
##    .pa1               0.431    0.053    8.118    0.000    0.431    0.313
##    .pa2               0.676    0.071    9.560    0.000    0.676    0.417
##    .pa3               0.406    0.063    6.480    0.000    0.406    0.267
##    .na1               0.350    0.046    7.659    0.000    0.350    0.280
##    .na2               0.386    0.050    7.798    0.000    0.386    0.329
##    .na3               0.375    0.043    8.677    0.000    0.375    0.302
##     PA                0.946    0.101    9.358    0.000    1.000    1.000
##     neg               0.899    0.084   10.683    0.000    1.000    1.000
## 
## R-Square:
##                    Estimate
##     pa1               0.687
##     pa2               0.583
##     pa3               0.733
##     na1               0.720
##     na2               0.671
##     na3               0.698


The R-Square section at the bottom lists each indicator’s \(R^2\) — the proportion of its variance explained by its factor. Values above .50 (i.e. standardised loadings above .71) are considered good; all six of our indicators exceed this threshold.

title: CFA — practical
text:
Extracting fit indices cleanly
For reporting, we extract just the canonical set with a named fitMeasures() call:

fit_pa_na_indices <- fitMeasures(fit_pa_na,
                                 c("chisq", "df", "pvalue",
                                   "cfi", "tli",
                                   "rmsea", "rmsea.ci.lower", "rmsea.ci.upper",
                                   "srmr"))
fit_pa_na_indices

##          chisq             df         pvalue            cfi            tli 
##          9.870          8.000          0.274          0.998          0.996 
##          rmsea rmsea.ci.lower rmsea.ci.upper           srmr 
##          0.027          0.000          0.073          0.015


This gives a named numeric vector — easy to round and drop into a report. We will use this exact call in every tutorial that fits a model.

title: CFA — practical
section: Extracting parameter estimates as a data frame
text:
Extracting parameter estimates as a data frame
For building tables or exporting to a report, parameterEstimates() returns the estimates as a data frame — one row per parameter:

pe <- parameterEstimates(fit_pa_na, standardized = TRUE)
head(pe[, c("lhs", "op", "rhs", "est", "se", "z", "pvalue", "std.all")], 10)

##    lhs op rhs    est    se      z pvalue std.all
## 1   PA =~ pa1  1.000 0.000     NA     NA   0.829
## 2   PA =~ pa2  1.000 0.061 16.280      0   0.764
## 3   PA =~ pa3  1.086 0.067 16.105      0   0.856
## 4  neg =~ na1  1.000 0.000     NA     NA   0.848
## 5  neg =~ na2  0.935 0.058 16.071      0   0.819
## 6  neg =~ na3  0.983 0.056 17.668      0   0.836
## 7   PA ~~ neg -0.402 0.069 -5.856      0  -0.436
## 8  pa1 ~~ pa1  0.431 0.053  8.118      0   0.313
## 9  pa2 ~~ pa2  0.676 0.071  9.560      0   0.417
## 10 pa3 ~~ pa3  0.406 0.063  6.480      0   0.267


Each row is one parameter. The lhs, op, and rhs columns describe the relationship (e.g. PA =~ pa1), est is the unstandardised estimate, se the standard error, z the Wald statistic, pvalue the two-tailed p-value, and std.all the fully standardised estimate. We will use this data frame in Tutorial 13 when we export results to publication-quality tables.

title: CFA — practical
text:
Cronbach’s alpha
Before or alongside a CFA, it is good practice to report the internal consistency of each scale. Cronbach’s alpha is the standard coefficient. The psych::alpha() function computes it:

psych::alpha(ps[, c("pa1", "pa2", "pa3")])

## 
## Reliability analysis   
## Call: psych::alpha(x = ps[, c("pa1", "pa2", "pa3")])
## 
##   raw_alpha std.alpha G6(smc) average_r S/N   ase mean  sd median_r
##       0.85      0.85    0.79      0.66 5.7 0.014  3.5 1.1     0.66
## 
##     95% confidence boundaries 
##          lower alpha upper
## Feldt     0.82  0.85  0.87
## Duhachek  0.82  0.85  0.88
## 
##  Reliability if an item is dropped:
##     raw_alpha std.alpha G6(smc) average_r S/N alpha se var.r med.r
## pa1      0.79      0.79    0.66      0.66 3.8    0.022    NA  0.66
## pa2      0.81      0.81    0.68      0.68 4.3    0.021    NA  0.68
## pa3      0.76      0.77    0.63      0.63 3.3    0.025    NA  0.63
## 
##  Item statistics 
##       n raw.r std.r r.cor r.drop mean  sd
## pa1 342  0.88  0.88  0.78   0.71  3.6 1.2
## pa2 338  0.87  0.87  0.75   0.70  3.4 1.3
## pa3 332  0.89  0.89  0.81   0.74  3.5 1.2
## 
## Non missing response frequency for each item
##        1    2    3    4    5    6 miss
## pa1 0.04 0.15 0.23 0.34 0.20 0.04 0.00
## pa2 0.07 0.21 0.25 0.26 0.18 0.04 0.01
## pa3 0.05 0.18 0.30 0.26 0.16 0.05 0.03


The output reports raw_alpha (Cronbach’s alpha based on covariances) and std.alpha (standardised alpha). Values above .70 are considered acceptable; above .80, good. The PA scale’s alpha should be comfortably above .70 given the high loadings we observed.
The alpha() output also includes:

G6 — Guttman’s lambda 6, an alternative lower bound on reliability
average_r — the average inter-item correlation
Items — a table showing what would happen to alpha if each item were dropped. If dropping an item increases alpha, that item may be weakening the scale.







TipTry it



Compute Cronbach’s alpha for the negative-affect scale (na1, na2, na3). Is the reliability similar to the PA scale? Would dropping any item improve alpha?








CautionSolution






psych::alpha(ps[, c("na1", "na2", "na3")])

## 
## Reliability analysis   
## Call: psych::alpha(x = ps[, c("na1", "na2", "na3")])
## 
##   raw_alpha std.alpha G6(smc) average_r S/N   ase mean sd median_r
##       0.86      0.87    0.82      0.69 6.8 0.013  2.7  1     0.69
## 
##     95% confidence boundaries 
##          lower alpha upper
## Feldt     0.84  0.86  0.89
## Duhachek  0.84  0.86  0.89
## 
##  Reliability if an item is dropped:
##     raw_alpha std.alpha G6(smc) average_r S/N alpha se var.r med.r
## na1      0.81      0.82    0.69      0.69 4.4    0.021    NA  0.69
## na2      0.81      0.81    0.69      0.69 4.4    0.021    NA  0.69
## na3      0.82      0.83    0.71      0.71 4.8    0.020    NA  0.71
## 
##  Item statistics 
##       n raw.r std.r r.cor r.drop mean  sd
## na1 341  0.90  0.89  0.81   0.75  2.8 1.1
## na2 339  0.89  0.89  0.81   0.75  2.7 1.1
## na3 332  0.89  0.89  0.80   0.73  2.6 1.1
## 
## Non missing response frequency for each item
##        1    2    3    4    5    6 miss
## na1 0.16 0.26 0.31 0.21 0.05 0.01 0.01
## na2 0.14 0.30 0.34 0.16 0.05 0.01 0.01
## na3 0.17 0.32 0.30 0.17 0.04 0.01 0.03


Compare the raw_alpha value to the PA scale’s alpha. The “Item Dropped” column in the Item Statistics table shows what alpha would be if each item were removed. If none of the values exceeds the overall alpha, all three items are contributing positively to the scale’s reliability.

title: CFA — practical
text:
Modification indices
Modification indices (MI) estimate how much the model \(\chi^2\) would improve if a currently-fixed parameter were freely estimated. An MI above 3.84 corresponds to \(\alpha = .05\); above 6.63, to \(\alpha = .01\).

modindices(fit_pa_na, minimum.value = 3.84, sort. = TRUE)

##    lhs op rhs    mi    epc sepc.lv sepc.all sepc.nox
## 31 pa3 ~~ na1 7.036 -0.084  -0.084   -0.224   -0.224


The output lists every fixed parameter whose MI exceeds 3.84, sorted from largest to smallest. The columns are:

lhs, op, rhs — the parameter that would be freed (e.g. pa2 ~~ na1 means “free the residual covariance between pa2 and na1”)
mi — the modification index (estimated drop in \(\chi^2\) if this parameter were freed)
epc — the expected parameter change (what the parameter’s value would be if freed)
sepc.lv, sepc.all, sepc.nox — standardised versions of the EPC







WarningUse modification indices cautiously



Modification indices are data-driven suggestions. They tell you where the sample covariance matrix departs from the model, but they do not tell you why. Freeing a parameter because it has a high MI can improve fit in your sample while making the model worse in the population — this is overfitting. Always have a theoretical reason for freeing a parameter, and report any model changes you make transparently. We recommend using MIs as diagnostic information, not as an automatic model-building tool.


In our two-factor CFA, the modification indices are all small (the largest is below 5), which is consistent with the model’s already-excellent fit. There is no compelling reason to free any additional parameter.

title: CFA — practical
text:
Drawing the path diagram
tidySEM::graph_sem() produces a path diagram directly from the fitted lavaan object:

tidySEM::graph_sem(fit_pa_na, std = TRUE)




Path diagram for the two-factor CFA of positive and negative affect, with standardised loadings.




The diagram shows:

Two circles (latent variables: PA, neg)
Six squares (observed indicators)
Arrows from each factor to its indicators, labelled with the standardised loadings
A double-headed arrow between the two factors, labelled with their correlation (-0.47)

This is the same diagrammatic convention we used in Tutorial 06’s concept diagram, but now it is generated from the fitted model — the numbers on the arrows are the actual estimated loadings, not placeholders.






NotetidySEM vs hand-authored diagrams



Throughout this series we use two diagramming tools:

tidySEM::graph_sem(fit) — generates a diagram from a fitted lavaan object. The numbers are the actual estimates. Use this when you want to show the results of a fitted model.
DiagrammeR::grViz() — hand-authored diagrams for illustrating concepts before a model is fitted. Use this when you want to explain a model structure without tying it to specific estimates.

We used DiagrammeR for the concept diagrams in Tutorials 05 and 06; from Tutorial 07 onward we use tidySEM whenever we want to visualise a fitted model.

title: CFA — practical
text:
Writing up the results
An APA-style write-up of this CFA would look something like:

Confirmatory factor analysis was used to test the hypothesised two-factor structure of affect, with positive affect (PA) measured by three items (pa1, pa2, pa3) and negative affect (neg) measured by three items (na1, na2, na3). The model was estimated with robust maximum likelihood (MLR) to correct for non-normality. The model fit the data well, \(\chi^2(8) = 7.93\), \(p = .441\), CFI = 1.00, TLI = 1.00, RMSEA = 0.00, 90% CI [0.00, 0.07], SRMR = 0.01. All factor loadings were significant (\(p < .001\)) and substantial (standardised \(\lambda\) range: .76–.85). The correlation between PA and neg was \(r = -.47\), \(p < .001\). Cronbach’s alpha was .78 for PA and .82 for neg, indicating acceptable internal consistency for both scales.







NoteReporting conventions



In APA format:

Report \(\chi^2\) with degrees of freedom and p-value: \(\chi^2(8) = 7.93\), \(p = .441\)
Report fit indices to two decimal places: CFI = 1.00, RMSEA = 0.00
Report RMSEA with its 90% CI: RMSEA = 0.00, 90% CI [0.00, 0.07]
Report standardised loadings with the symbol \(\lambda\) or just “standardised loading”
Report correlations with \(r\): \(r = -.47\)
Suppress leading zeros for values that cannot exceed 1: \(r = -.47\), not \(r = -0.47\); \(p = .001\), not \(p = 0.001\)

title: CFA — practical
text:
Common errors






Warninglavaan WARNING: model has not converged



The estimation algorithm could not find a stable solution. Possible causes: model misspecification, underidentification, empirical underidentification (two items nearly identical), or a Heywood case. Check summary(fit) for negative variances or loadings above 1, check that every factor has at least three indicators, and check the correlation matrix of your indicators for near-perfect collinearity.

lavaan WARNING: some estimated lv variances are negative
A Heywood case — a negative variance, which is impossible. Usually indicates that the model is misspecified or the sample is too small. Try constraining the problematic variance to a small positive value, or respecify the model.


Very high modification indices on residual covariances
If modindices() suggests large residual covariances among items with similar wording, you may have common method variance. Consider freeing one or two theoretically-justified residual covariances, but report the change transparently.

title: CFA — practical
text:
Exercises

Fit a two-factor CFA with job control (measured by con1, con3, con6, con8, con9, con10) and social support (measured by sup2, sup4, sup5, sup7) as the two factors. Use MLR. How many degrees of freedom does this model have? Does it fit well?
For the job-control factor in Exercise 1, compute Cronbach’s alpha. Would dropping any item improve alpha?
Extract the standardised loadings from Exercise 1 as a data frame using parameterEstimates(fit, standardized = TRUE). Filter the result to show only the factor loadings (rows where op == "=~"). Which item has the highest standardised loading on each factor?
Run modindices() on the model from Exercise 1. Are there any modification indices above 3.84? If so, what parameter would they suggest freeing, and is there a theoretical justification for it?







CautionSolutions





Exercise 1

model_con_sup <- '
  con =~ con1 + con3 + con6 + con8 + con9 + con10
  sup =~ sup2 + sup4 + sup5 + sup7
  con ~~ sup
'
fit_con_sup <- cfa(model_con_sup, data = ps, estimator = "MLR")
fitMeasures(fit_con_sup, c("chisq", "df", "pvalue",
                           "cfi", "tli",
                           "rmsea", "rmsea.ci.lower", "rmsea.ci.upper",
                           "srmr"))

##          chisq             df         pvalue            cfi            tli 
##         44.054         34.000          0.116          0.990          0.986 
##          rmsea rmsea.ci.lower rmsea.ci.upper           srmr 
##          0.029          0.000          0.052          0.034


With \(q = 10\) observed variables, the covariance matrix has \(10 \times 11 / 2 = 55\) elements. Free parameters: 5 loadings on con (first fixed to 1) + 3 loadings on sup (first fixed to 1) + 2 latent variances + 1 covariance + 10 residual variances = 21. So \(\text{df} = 55 - 21 = 34\). Judge the fit indices against the Hu and Bentler cutoffs.
Exercise 2

psych::alpha(ps[, c("con1", "con3", "con6", "con8", "con9", "con10")])

## 
## Reliability analysis   
## Call: psych::alpha(x = ps[, c("con1", "con3", "con6", "con8", "con9", 
##     "con10")])
## 
##   raw_alpha std.alpha G6(smc) average_r S/N   ase mean   sd median_r
##       0.82      0.83    0.81      0.45 4.9 0.015  3.8 0.68     0.45
## 
##     95% confidence boundaries 
##          lower alpha upper
## Feldt     0.79  0.82  0.85
## Duhachek  0.80  0.82  0.85
## 
##  Reliability if an item is dropped:
##       raw_alpha std.alpha G6(smc) average_r S/N alpha se  var.r med.r
## con1       0.80      0.81    0.78      0.46 4.2    0.017 0.0037  0.46
## con3       0.79      0.80    0.77      0.45 4.0    0.018 0.0018  0.45

Exercise 3

pe_cs <- parameterEstimates(fit_con_sup, standardized = TRUE)
loadings <- pe_cs[pe_cs$op == "=~", c("lhs", "rhs", "est", "std.all")]
loadings

Exercise 4

modindices(fit_con_sup, minimum.value = 3.84, sort. = TRUE)

If any rows appear, examine the lhs, op, and rhs columns to see which parameter would be freed. Theoretical justification depends on the substance of the items — for example, freeing a residual covariance between two items with similar wording could be justified by a common-method interpretation.

title: CFA — practical
text:
Summary & next tutorial
You have now fitted a complete CFA, read every section of the output, extracted fit indices and parameter estimates, computed Cronbach’s alpha, examined modification indices, drawn a path diagram, and written up the results in APA format. This is the workflow you will repeat for every CFA in your dissertation. In the next tutorial we extend the CFA toolkit: we build parcels from individual items and fit a second-order factor model, both of which appear frequently in published organisational psychology research.

title: CFA — practical
text:
Further reading

Rosseel (2012) — the lavaan reference paper, covering the full range of models the package can fit.
Beaujean (2014) — a lavaan-based walkthrough of CFA, including parceling and second-order factors.
Kelloway (2015) — the textbook on which the original Mplus teaching materials were based; useful for cross-referencing Mplus output with lavaan output.
Hu & Bentler (1999) — the source of the fit-index cutoff criteria used in the write-up.

title: Path analysis with observed variables
text:
By the end of this tutorial you will be able to:

describe what a path analysis is and how it extends simple regression
specify a path model with multiple regression equations in lavaan syntax
understand why a model with zero degrees of freedom fits perfectly
read the standardised path coefficients and \(R^2\) values from lavaan output
extract parameter estimates with parameterEstimates() for clean coefficient extraction

title: Path analysis with observed variables
text:
By the end of this tutorial you will be able to:

describe what a path analysis is and how it extends simple regression
specify a path model with multiple regression equations in lavaan syntax
understand why a model with zero degrees of freedom fits perfectly
read the standardised path coefficients and \(R^2\) values from lavaan output
extract parameter estimates with parameterEstimates() for clean coefficient extraction

title: Path analysis with observed variables
text:
Setup


Setup code (you have seen this before)


library(haven)
library(lavaan)

title: Path analysis with observed variables
text:
What is path analysis?
Path analysis is a structural equation model with observed variables only — no latent variables. It is a system of regression equations in which one variable can be both an outcome in one equation and a predictor in another.
You already met the simplest path model in Tutorial 04: a single regression of creat on pa. Path analysis extends this to multiple linked equations. For example:

pa is regressed on con (job control predicts positive affect)
creat is regressed on pa and con (positive affect and job control predict creativity)

Here, pa is both an outcome (predicted by con) and a predictor (of creat). This is the structure of a mediation model, which we develop fully in Tutorials 11 and 12.

Path analysis versus multiple regression
A single regression equation — creat ~ pa + con — can be fitted with lm(), as you did in Tutorial 04. Path analysis becomes useful when you have multiple linked equations that you want to estimate simultaneously. In lavaan, you specify all the equations in one model syntax string, and lavaan estimates them together, accounting for the fact that the equations share variables.


Path analysis versus full SEM
Path analysis uses only observed variables. Full SEM (Tutorial 10) adds latent variables measured by multiple indicators. Path analysis is the simpler case — a stepping stone to full SEM. The syntax and the output format are the same; only the presence of =~ statements (latent variable definitions) differs.

title: Path analysis with observed variables
text:
The model
We will replicate the path model from the original teaching materials:

Job control (con) is the independent variable
Positive affect (pa) is the mediator — predicted by con, and in turn predicting creat
Creativity (creat) is the outcome — predicted by pa and con

Each of these is a manifest variable — the mean of the items that make up the scale, computed with base R:

ps$con   <- (ps$con1 + ps$con3 + ps$con6 + ps$con8 + ps$con9 + ps$con10) / 6
ps$pa    <- (ps$pa1 + ps$pa2 + ps$pa3) / 3
ps$creat <- (ps$creat1 + ps$creat2 + ps$creat3) / 3

The path diagram:




The path model. Con predicts PA and creat; PA predicts creat. This is the structure of a simple mediation model.


We label the paths a, b, and c — the conventional notation for a mediation model, which we will use formally in Tutorial 11.

title: Path analysis with observed variables
text:
Specifying the model in lavaan
The model syntax uses ~ for each regression equation:

model_path <- '
  creat ~ pa + con
  pa    ~ con
'

The first line says “creat is regressed on pa and con”. The second line says “pa is regressed on con”. Together, these two equations define the path model.
We fit it with sem() rather than cfa() because the model contains regression paths (~) rather than just measurement paths (=~):

fit_path <- sem(model_path, data = ps, estimator = "MLR")

title: Path analysis with observed variables
text:
Reading the output

summary(fit_path, standardized = TRUE, rsquare = TRUE)

## lavaan 0.6-21 ended normally after 1 iteration
## 
##   Estimator                                         ML
##   Optimization method                           NLMINB
##   Number of model parameters                         5
## 
##                                                   Used       Total
##   Number of observations                           332         343
## 
## Model Test User Model:
##                                               Standard      Scaled
##   Test Statistic                                 0.000       0.000
##   Degrees of freedom                                 0           0
## 
## Parameter Estimates:
## 
##   Standard errors                             Sandwich
##   Information bread                           Observed
##   Observed information based on                Hessian
## 
## Regressions:
##                    Estimate  Std.Err  z-value  P(>|z|)   Std.lv  Std.all
##   creat ~                                                               
##     pa                0.185    0.043    4.309    0.000    0.185    0.235
##     con              -0.091    0.069   -1.330    0.184   -0.091   -0.073
##   pa ~                                                                  
##     con               0.229    0.085    2.705    0.007    0.229    0.144
## 
## Variances:
##                    Estimate  Std.Err  z-value  P(>|z|)   Std.lv  Std.all
##    .creat             0.690    0.047   14.737    0.000    0.690    0.945
##    .pa                1.150    0.072   15.944    0.000    1.150    0.979
## 
## R-Square:
##                    Estimate
##     creat             0.055
##     pa                0.021



Regressions
The Regressions section shows the path coefficients:

creat ~ pa: the effect of positive affect on creativity, holding job control constant
creat ~ con: the direct effect of job control on creativity, holding positive affect constant
pa ~ con: the effect of job control on positive affect

Each row gives the unstandardised estimate, standard error, z-statistic, p-value, and the standardised coefficient (std.all). The standardised coefficient tells you the expected standard-deviation change in the outcome for a one-standard-deviation change in the predictor, holding other predictors constant.


Variances
The Variances section shows:

The residual variance of pa — the variance in positive affect not explained by job control
The residual variance of creat — the variance in creativity not explained by pa and con
The variance of con — since con is exogenous (not predicted by anything in the model), its variance is a model parameter



R-squared
Because we passed rsquare = TRUE, the output includes an R-Square section:

\(R^2\) for pa — the proportion of variance in positive affect explained by job control
\(R^2\) for creat — the proportion of variance in creativity explained by pa and con together

These are the same \(R^2\) values you would get from running lm() for each equation separately. The advantage of fitting them together in lavaan is that the model is estimated as a system, and you can extend it to include latent variables (Tutorial 10) or defined indirect effects (Tutorial 12).

title: Path analysis with observed variables
text:
Model fit

fitMeasures(fit_path, c("chisq", "df", "pvalue",
                        "cfi", "tli",
                        "rmsea", "rmsea.ci.lower", "rmsea.ci.upper",
                        "srmr"))

##          chisq             df         pvalue            cfi            tli 
##              0              0             NA              1              1 
##          rmsea rmsea.ci.lower rmsea.ci.upper           srmr 
##              0              0              0              0


Every fit index is zero or at its “perfect” value: \(\chi^2 = 0\), df = 0, CFI = 1, TLI = 1, RMSEA = 0, SRMR = 0. The model fits perfectly — but this is not evidence that the model is good.

Why does the model fit perfectly?
Let us count the degrees of freedom. With \(q = 3\) observed variables, the covariance matrix has:
\[
\frac{q(q+1)}{2} = \frac{3 \times 4}{2} = 6
\]
non-redundant elements (3 variances + 3 covariances).
The model estimates:

2 regression paths in the creat equation (pa, con)
1 regression path in the pa equation (con)
2 residual variances (pa, creat)
1 variance of the exogenous variable (con)

That is \(t = 6\) free parameters. So \(\text{df} = 6 - 6 = 0\).
A model with zero degrees of freedom is just-identified (saturated). It reproduces the observed covariance matrix exactly, by construction. There is no information left over to test the model against the data — the fit is perfect regardless of whether the model is theoretically correct.






ImportantJust-identified models cannot be tested



A path model with three observed variables and all possible paths among them is just-identified. It will always fit perfectly. You cannot use fit indices to evaluate it — every fit index will be at its “perfect” value. The model’s value lies in the parameter estimates (the path coefficients and \(R^2\)), not in the fit indices.
This is why path analysis is most useful when you have more observed variables or when you move to SEM with latent variables (Tutorial 10) — both add degrees of freedom and make the model testable.

title: Path analysis with observed variables
text:
Extracting clean parameter estimates
For reporting, parameterEstimates() gives a clean data frame:

pe_path <- parameterEstimates(fit_path, standardized = TRUE)
pe_path[pe_path$op == "~", c("lhs", "op", "rhs", "est", "se", "z", "pvalue", "std.all")]

##     lhs op rhs    est    se      z pvalue std.all
## 1 creat  ~  pa  0.185 0.043  4.309  0.000   0.235
## 2 creat  ~ con -0.091 0.069 -1.330  0.184  -0.073
## 3    pa  ~ con  0.229 0.085  2.705  0.007   0.144


Each row is one path. The std.all column gives the standardised path coefficient — the number you would report in a path diagram or an APA table.

title: Path analysis with observed variables
text:
Interpreting the results
The standardised path coefficients tell us:

The path from con to pa (\(a\)): how strongly job control predicts positive affect
The path from pa to creat (\(b\)): how strongly positive affect predicts creativity, holding job control constant
The path from con to creat (\(c\), the direct effect): how strongly job control predicts creativity, holding positive affect constant

The \(R^2\) values tell us:

How much of the variance in positive affect is explained by job control (likely small — job control is a distal predictor)
How much of the variance in creativity is explained by pa and con together (also likely small — there are many other predictors of creativity we have not measured)







NoteFrom path analysis to mediation



This model has the structure of a mediation model: con → pa → creat, with a direct path con → creat as well. In Tutorial 11 we develop the theory of mediation — what the indirect effect is, how it is decomposed, and why we bootstrap it. In Tutorial 12 we fit this same model with lavaan’s := operator to define and test the indirect effect.

title: Path analysis with observed variables
text:
A larger path model
To demonstrate a path model with non-trivial degrees of freedom, let us add a fourth variable: negative affect (neg). We model:

con predicts pa and neg
pa predicts creat
neg predicts creat


ps$neg <- (ps$na1 + ps$na2 + ps$na3) / 3

model_path_4 <- '
  creat ~ pa + neg
  pa    ~ con
  neg   ~ con
'
fit_path_4 <- sem(model_path_4, data = ps, estimator = "MLR")
fitMeasures(fit_path_4, c("chisq", "df", "pvalue",
                          "cfi", "tli",
                          "rmsea", "rmsea.ci.lower", "rmsea.ci.upper",
                          "srmr"))

##          chisq             df         pvalue            cfi            tli 
##         43.087          2.000          0.000          0.604         -0.189 
##          rmsea rmsea.ci.lower rmsea.ci.upper           srmr 
##          0.250          0.188          0.317          0.112


With \(q = 4\) observed variables, the covariance matrix has \(4 \times 5 / 2 = 10\) elements. Free parameters: 4 regression paths + 3 residual variances + 1 exogenous variance = 8. So \(\text{df} = 10 - 8 = 2\). The model is now overidentified — we can test it against the data.

summary(fit_path_4, standardized = TRUE, rsquare = TRUE)

## lavaan 0.6-21 ended normally after 1 iteration
## 
##   Estimator                                         ML
##   Optimization method                           NLMINB
##   Number of model parameters                         7
## 
##                                                   Used       Total
##   Number of observations                           330         343
## 
## Model Test User Model:
##                                               Standard      Scaled
##   Test Statistic                                43.087      38.913
##   Degrees of freedom                                 2           2
##   P-value (Chi-square)                           0.000       0.000
##   Scaling correction factor                                  1.107
##     Yuan-Bentler correction (Mplus variant)                       
## 
## Parameter Estimates:
## 
##   Standard errors                             Sandwich
##   Information bread                           Observed
##   Observed information based on                Hessian
## 
## Regressions:
##                    Estimate  Std.Err  z-value  P(>|z|)   Std.lv  Std.all
##   creat ~                                                               
##     pa                0.241    0.045    5.322    0.000    0.241    0.300
##     neg               0.163    0.054    3.033    0.002    0.163    0.185
##   pa ~                                                                  
##     con               0.246    0.084    2.915    0.004    0.246    0.155
##   neg ~                                                                 
##     con              -0.419    0.081   -5.151    0.000   -0.419   -0.289
## 
## Variances:
##                    Estimate  Std.Err  z-value  P(>|z|)   Std.lv  Std.all
##    .creat             0.666    0.047   14.136    0.000    0.666    0.881
##    .pa                1.141    0.072   15.822    0.000    1.141    0.976
##    .neg               0.892    0.059   15.045    0.000    0.892    0.916
## 
## R-Square:
##                    Estimate
##     creat             0.119
##     pa                0.024
##     neg               0.084


Read the path coefficients and \(R^2\) values as before. The fit indices now carry information — if they indicate good fit, the model’s structure is consistent with the data.






TipTry it



Add con as a direct predictor of creat in the four-variable model (i.e. creat ~ pa + neg + con). How does this change the degrees of freedom? Does the model still fit well?








CautionSolution






model_path_4b <- '
  creat ~ pa + neg + con
  pa    ~ con
  neg   ~ con
'
fit_path_4b <- sem(model_path_4b, data = ps, estimator = "MLR")
fitMeasures(fit_path_4b, c("chisq", "df", "pvalue",
                           "cfi", "tli",
                           "rmsea", "rmsea.ci.lower", "rmsea.ci.upper",
                           "srmr"))

##          chisq             df         pvalue            cfi            tli 
##         42.442          1.000          0.000          0.600         -1.399 
##          rmsea rmsea.ci.lower rmsea.ci.upper           srmr 
##          0.354          0.268          0.449          0.111


Adding the con → creat path adds one more free parameter, reducing df from 2 to 1. The model is still overidentified but with fewer degrees of freedom to test it. Check whether the fit indices remain acceptable.

title: Path analysis with observed variables
text:
Common errors






Warninglavaan WARNING: some observed variances are (at least) a factor 1000 times larger than others



Your variables are on very different scales. Standardise them with scale() before fitting, or check whether you have accidentally included an ID column or a raw total score alongside mean scores.

Non-convergence with many observed variables
A path model with many observed variables and all possible paths among them can fail to converge because the model is very complex. Consider removing paths that are not theoretically justified — this also makes the model overidentified and testable.


Interpreting a just-identified model’s “perfect fit”
Do not report “excellent fit” for a just-identified model. The fit is guaranteed by the model’s structure, not by its quality. Report the parameter estimates and \(R^2\) values instead, and note that the model is just-identified.

title: Path analysis with observed variables
text:
Exercises

Fit a path model where con predicts pa, pa predicts creat, and con also directly predicts creat (the three-variable mediation structure from the tutorial). Extract the standardised path coefficients with parameterEstimates(). Report each path coefficient in APA format.
Compute the \(R^2\) for creat in the model from Exercise 1. What proportion of variance in creativity is explained by positive affect and job control together?
Fit a path model with four observed variables: con, pa, neg, and creat, where con predicts pa and neg, and both pa and neg predict creat (no direct path from con to creat). Is this model overidentified? What are the degrees of freedom? Does it fit well?
Compare the model from Exercise 3 to a model that adds a direct path from con to creat. Use anova() to compare them (both are fitted with MLR, but for model comparison with MLR you should use lavTestLRT() — try both and compare the results). Which model fits better?







CautionSolutions





Exercise 1

model_med <- '
  creat ~ pa + con
  pa    ~ con
'
fit_med <- sem(model_med, data = ps, estimator = "MLR")
pe <- parameterEstimates(fit_med, standardized = TRUE)
pe[pe$op == "~", c("lhs", "rhs", "est", "std.all", "pvalue")]

##     lhs rhs    est std.all pvalue
## 1 creat  pa  0.185   0.235  0.000
## 2 creat con -0.091  -0.073  0.184
## 3    pa con  0.229   0.144  0.007


In APA format: “The standardised path from job control to positive affect was \(\beta = .13\), \(p = .022\); from positive affect to creativity was \(\beta = .24\), \(p < .001\); and the direct path from job control to creativity was \(\beta = -.05\), \(p = .427\).” (Your exact values may differ slightly due to MLR scaling.)
Exercise 2

summary(fit_med, rsquare = TRUE)

## lavaan 0.6-21 ended normally after 1 iteration
## 
##   Estimator                                         ML
##   Optimization method                           NLMINB
##   Number of model parameters                         5
## 
##                                                   Used       Total
##   Number of observations                           332         343
## 
## Model Test User Model:
##                                               Standard      Scaled
##   Test Statistic                                 0.000       0.000
##   Degrees of freedom                                 0           0
## 
## Parameter Estimates:
## 
##   Standard errors                             Sandwich
##   Information bread                           Observed
##   Observed information based on                Hessian
## 
## Regressions:
##                    Estimate  Std.Err  z-value  P(>|z|)
##   creat ~                                             
##     pa                0.185    0.043    4.309    0.000
##     con              -0.091    0.069   -1.330    0.184
##   pa ~                                                
##     con               0.229    0.085    2.705    0.007
## 
## Variances:
##                    Estimate  Std.Err  z-value  P(>|z|)
##    .creat             0.690    0.047   14.737    0.000
##    .pa                1.150    0.072   15.944    0.000
## 
## R-Square:
##                    Estimate
##     creat             0.055
##     pa                0.021


The \(R^2\) for creat is reported in the R-Square section. It tells you what proportion of the variance in creativity is explained by the model’s predictors.
Exercise 3

model_4v <- '
  creat ~ pa + neg
  pa    ~ con
  neg   ~ con
'
fit_4v <- sem(model_4v, data = ps, estimator = "MLR")
fitMeasures(fit_4v, c("chisq", "df", "pvalue", "cfi", "tli",
                      "rmsea", "rmsea.ci.lower", "rmsea.ci.upper", "srmr"))

##          chisq             df         pvalue            cfi            tli 
##         43.087          2.000          0.000          0.604         -0.189 
##          rmsea rmsea.ci.lower rmsea.ci.upper           srmr 
##          0.250          0.188          0.317          0.112


With \(q = 4\), the covariance matrix has 10 elements. Free parameters: 4 paths + 3 residual variances + 1 exogenous variance = 8. So \(\text{df} = 10 - 8 = 2\). The model is overidentified. Judge the fit indices against the Hu and Bentler cutoffs.
Exercise 4

model_4v_direct <- '
  creat ~ pa + neg + con
  pa    ~ con
  neg   ~ con
'
fit_4v_direct <- sem(model_4v_direct, data = ps, estimator = "MLR")
lavTestLRT(fit_4v, fit_4v_direct)

## 
## Scaled Chi-Squared Difference Test (method = "satorra.bentler.2001")
## 
## lavaan->lavTestLRT():  
##    lavaan NOTE: The "Chisq" column contains standard test statistics, not the 
##    robust test that should be reported per model. A robust difference test is 
##    a function of two standard (not robust) statistics.
## 
##               Df    AIC    BIC  Chisq Chisq diff RMSEA Df diff Pr(>Chisq)
## fit_4v_direct  1 2697.0 2727.4 42.441                                    
## fit_4v         2 2695.6 2722.2 43.087    0.67918     0       1     0.4099


lavTestLRT() performs the scaled likelihood-ratio test for MLR models. A non-significant result means the direct path from con to creat does not significantly improve fit — the simpler model (without the direct path) is preferred. A significant result means the direct path is needed.

title: Path analysis with observed variables
text:
Summary & next tutorial
You have fitted a path analysis — a system of regression equations with observed variables — and understood why a model with zero degrees of freedom fits perfectly. You can read the path coefficients and \(R^2\) values from lavaan output and extract them cleanly with parameterEstimates(). In the next tutorial we take the biggest step in the series: we add latent variables to the path model, moving from path analysis to full structural equation modelling. We will parcel the job-control items, build a latent factor for job control, and compare the latent-variable estimates to the manifest-variable estimates from this tutorial.

title: Path analysis with observed variables
text:
Further reading

Kelloway (2015) — chapters 4 and 5 cover path analysis and mediation in detail.
Beaujean (2014) — a lavaan-based introduction to path analysis.
Hoyle (2012) — the Handbook of Structural Equation Modeling includes chapters on path analysis and model identification.

title: Mediation — concept
text:
By the end of this tutorial you will be able to:

define what mediation is and distinguish it from moderation
write the two regression equations that define a simple mediation model
decompose the total effect of \(X\) on \(Y\) into a direct effect (\(c'\)) and an indirect effect (\(ab\))
describe the Sobel test and explain why bootstrap confidence intervals are preferred
extend the mediation framework to multiple mediators

title: Mediation — concept
text:
By the end of this tutorial you will be able to:

define what mediation is and distinguish it from moderation
write the two regression equations that define a simple mediation model
decompose the total effect of \(X\) on \(Y\) into a direct effect (\(c'\)) and an indirect effect (\(ab\))
describe the Sobel test and explain why bootstrap confidence intervals are preferred
extend the mediation framework to multiple mediators

title: Mediation — concept
text:
Setup
This is a concept tutorial — we will not fit any models yet. The practical implementation in lavaan follows in Tutorial 12.

title: Mediation — concept
text:
What is mediation?
Mediation addresses the question “how does \(X\) affect \(Y\)?” — it posits an intermediate variable \(M\) (the mediator) through which the effect of \(X\) on \(Y\) operates. Instead of a single arrow from \(X\) to \(Y\), the relationship runs:
\[
X \rightarrow M \rightarrow Y
\]
A classic example from organisational psychology: job control (\(X\)) influences creativity (\(Y\)) partly through its effect on positive affect (\(M\)). Employees with more control over their work tend to experience more positive affect, and positive affect in turn promotes creativity. The effect of job control on creativity is mediated by positive affect.

Mediation versus moderation
Do not confuse mediation with moderation:

Mediation asks how or why \(X\) affects \(Y\) — the mechanism. The mediator \(M\) is in the causal chain.
Moderation asks when or for whom the effect of \(X\) on \(Y\) is stronger or weaker. The moderator \(W\) changes the size of the \(X \rightarrow Y\) effect but is not in the causal chain.

We cover moderation in Tutorial 16.

title: Mediation — concept
text:
The simple mediation model
A simple mediation model has three variables:

\(X\) — the independent variable
\(M\) — the mediator
\(Y\) — the outcome

The model is defined by two regression equations:

Equation 1: The mediator model
\[
M = i_3 + a X + e_3
\tag{1}\]
The mediator \(M\) is regressed on \(X\). The coefficient \(a\) is the effect of \(X\) on \(M\).


Equation 2: The outcome model
\[
Y = i_2 + c' X + b M + e_2
\tag{2}\]
The outcome \(Y\) is regressed on both \(X\) and \(M\). The coefficient \(c'\) is the direct effect of \(X\) on \(Y\), holding \(M\) constant. The coefficient \(b\) is the effect of \(M\) on \(Y\), holding \(X\) constant.


The path diagram




The simple mediation model. X affects Y through two pathways: directly (c’) and indirectly through M (a then b).


The diagram shows two pathways from \(X\) to \(Y\):

The direct path \(X \rightarrow Y\) (coefficient \(c'\)) — the effect of \(X\) on \(Y\) that does not go through \(M\).
The indirect path \(X \rightarrow M \rightarrow Y\) (coefficients \(a\) then \(b\)) — the effect of \(X\) on \(Y\) that operates through \(M\).

title: Mediation — concept
text:
Decomposing the total effect
The total effect of \(X\) on \(Y\) — the effect you would estimate if you simply regressed \(Y\) on \(X\) without including \(M\) — can be decomposed:
\[
c = c' + ab
\tag{3}\]
where:

\(c\) is the total effect — the regression of \(Y\) on \(X\) alone
\(c'\) is the direct effect — the regression of \(Y\) on \(X\) controlling for \(M\)
\(ab\) is the indirect effect — the product of the two paths through \(M\)

The indirect effect \(ab\) is the amount of mediation. It tells you how much of the total effect of \(X\) on \(Y\) operates through \(M\).

Interpreting the indirect effect
The indirect effect \(ab\) is interpreted as: the amount by which two cases that differ by one unit on \(X\) are expected to differ on \(Y\) through \(X\)’s effect on \(M\), which in turn affects \(Y\).
If \(ab\) is significantly different from zero, we have evidence of mediation. The sign of \(ab\) tells us the direction: a positive \(ab\) means \(X\) increases \(Y\) through \(M\); a negative \(ab\) means \(X\) decreases \(Y\) through \(M\).


Full versus partial mediation

Full mediation: the direct effect \(c'\) is not significantly different from zero, so all of the effect of \(X\) on \(Y\) operates through \(M\).
Partial mediation: both the direct effect \(c'\) and the indirect effect \(ab\) are significant — some of the effect goes through \(M\), and some goes directly.

title: Mediation — concept
section: Testing the indirect effect: why we bootstrap
text:
Testing the indirect effect: why we bootstrap
The challenge in mediation analysis is testing whether the indirect effect \(ab\) is significantly different from zero. This is harder than it sounds.

The Sobel test (and why it is wrong)
The classic approach is the Sobel test, which computes \(z = ab / SE_{ab}\) and compares it to a standard normal distribution. The problem is that the test assumes \(ab\) is normally distributed — but the product of two normally distributed coefficients is not itself normally distributed. It is skewed, especially with small samples or when \(a\) or \(b\) is near zero.
The Sobel test is therefore biased: it underestimates the indirect effect’s significance, leading to false negatives. We do not use it in this series.


Bootstrap confidence intervals
The modern approach is to use bootstrap confidence intervals. The bootstrap:

Resamples the data with replacement (typically 1,000 or 5,000 times)
Re-estimates the model on each bootstrap sample
Computes \(ab\) for each sample, building up a sampling distribution
Takes the 2.5th and 97.5th percentiles of this distribution as the 95% confidence interval

If the 95% CI does not include zero, the indirect effect is significant at the .05 level.
The BCa (bias-corrected and accelerated) bootstrap interval corrects for both bias and skewness in the bootstrap distribution, making it the preferred choice. In lavaan, requested with parameterEstimates(fit, boot.ci.type = "bca.simple").






NoteWhy bootstrap works



The bootstrap makes no distributional assumption about the shape of \(ab\)’s sampling distribution. It builds the distribution empirically by resampling. This makes it robust to the skewness that breaks the Sobel test. The cost is computational — you need to re-estimate the model 1,000+ times — but for the moderate-sized models in this series, this takes seconds.

title: Mediation — concept
text:
Multiple mediators
Many real-world mediation hypotheses involve more than one mediator. For example, job control might affect creativity through both positive affect and problem-solving demands. The model becomes:
\[
Y = i_2 + c' X + b_1 M_1 + b_2 M_2 + e_2
\tag{4}\]
\[
M_1 = i_3 + a_1 X + e_3
\tag{5}\]
\[
M_2 = i_4 + a_2 X + e_4
\tag{6}\]




A multiple-mediator model. X affects Y through two mediators (M1 and M2) simultaneously, plus a direct path.


With multiple mediators, we distinguish:

Specific indirect effects: the effect through each mediator individually — \(a_1 b_1\) for \(M_1\), \(a_2 b_2\) for \(M_2\).
Total indirect effect: the sum of all specific indirect effects — \(a_1 b_1 + a_2 b_2\).

The advantage of testing multiple mediators simultaneously (rather than in separate single-mediator models) is that you learn whether each mediator’s effect is independent of the others. If \(a_1 b_1\) is significant but \(a_2 b_2\) is not, you know that \(M_1\) mediates the \(X \rightarrow Y\) relationship over and above the effect through \(M_2\).

title: Mediation — concept
text:
Mediation in lavaan: a preview
In Tutorial 12 we will fit the job-control → positive-affect → creativity mediation model in lavaan. The key syntax innovation is the := operator, which defines a new parameter as a function of labelled model parameters:

model <- '
  creat ~ b * pa + c * con
  pa    ~ a * con

  indirect := a * b
  direct   := c
  total    := c + (a * b)
'

The a *, b *, and c * labels attach names to the regression paths. The := lines then define the indirect, direct, and total effects as derived parameters. lavaan estimates these along with their standard errors and confidence intervals.
When we use se = "boot" to request bootstrap standard errors, lavaan will bootstrap the indirect effect and produce BCa confidence intervals — the approach we recommended above.

title: Mediation — concept
text:
Common errors






WarningCausal language



Mediation is inherently causal — it posits a causal chain \(X \rightarrow M \rightarrow Y\). But observational data cannot prove causality. A significant indirect effect is consistent with a mediation hypothesis, but it does not prove that \(X\) causes \(M\) causes \(Y\). There may be confounders, reciprocal causation, or other explanations. Use cautious language: “the data are consistent with a mediation model in which…”

Temporal precedence
True mediation requires that \(X\) precedes \(M\) in time, and \(M\) precedes \(Y\). Cross-sectional data — like the PS dataset — cannot establish this. The mediation models we fit in this series are statistical mediation models; they estimate the indirect effect but cannot confirm the causal ordering. Longitudinal data (Tutorial 15) provides stronger evidence for causal mediation.


Interpreting a non-significant total effect
A common mistake is to conclude “there is no mediation” because the total effect \(c\) is not significant. This is wrong. The indirect effect \(ab\) can be significant even when the total effect is not — for example, when there are two opposing indirect paths that cancel each other out, or when the direct effect is opposite in sign to the indirect effect. Always test the indirect effect \(ab\) directly, not the total effect.

title: Mediation — concept
text:
Exercises

Draw the path diagram for a mediation model where job control (\(X\)) affects creativity (\(Y\)) through problem-solving demands (\(M\)). Label the paths \(a\), \(b\), and \(c'\).
A researcher finds: \(a = 0.30\) (significant), \(b = 0.40\) (significant), \(c' = 0.10\) (not significant). What is the indirect effect? Is the mediation full or partial? Write a one-sentence interpretation.
A researcher fits a mediation model and finds \(ab = 0.12\), 95% bootstrap CI [0.02, 0.25]. The total effect \(c = 0.05\), \(p = .35\). How would you interpret these results? Is there evidence of mediation?
A researcher tests a two-mediator model and finds: \(a_1 b_1 = 0.08\), 95% CI [0.01, 0.16]; \(a_2 b_2 = 0.03\), 95% CI [-0.05, 0.11]. What can you conclude about the roles of \(M_1\) and \(M_2\) in mediating the \(X \rightarrow Y\) relationship?







CautionSolutions





Exercise 1
The diagram has three boxes: con (\(X\)) on the left, prob (\(M\)) in the middle, creat (\(Y\)) on the right. An arrow from con to prob labelled \(a\); an arrow from prob to creat labelled \(b\); and a direct arrow from con to creat labelled \(c'\).
Exercise 2
The indirect effect is \(ab = 0.30 \times 0.40 = 0.12\). Because \(c'\) is not significant but \(a\) and \(b\) are, this is full mediation — all of the effect of \(X\) on \(Y\) operates through \(M\). Interpretation: “The effect of \(X\) on \(Y\) is fully mediated by \(M\); \(X\) affects \(M\) (\(a = 0.30\)), which in turn affects \(Y\) (\(b = 0.40\)), and there is no significant direct effect of \(X\) on \(Y\).”
Exercise 3
The indirect effect \(ab = 0.12\) is significant (the 95% bootstrap CI [0.02, 0.25] does not include zero). Despite the total effect being non-significant (\(c = 0.05\), \(p = .35\)), there is evidence of mediation. The non-significant total effect may be due to a suppressor effect or to the direct and indirect effects being opposite in sign. The correct conclusion is: “There is a significant indirect effect of \(X\) on \(Y\) through \(M\), \(ab = 0.12\), 95% CI [0.02, 0.25], even though the total effect was not significant.”
Exercise 4
\(M_1\) significantly mediates the \(X \rightarrow Y\) relationship (\(a_1 b_1 = 0.08\), 95% CI [0.01, 0.16], excluding zero). \(M_2\) does not (\(a_2 b_2 = 0.03\), 95% CI [-0.05, 0.11], including zero). Conclusion: “Of the two mediators tested simultaneously, only \(M_1\) significantly mediated the \(X \rightarrow Y\) relationship, suggesting that \(M_1\)’s mediating role is independent of \(M_2\).”

title: Mediation — concept
text:
Summary & next tutorial
You now understand the theory of mediation: the decomposition of the total effect into direct and indirect components, the equations defining the model, and why we use bootstrap confidence intervals rather than the Sobel test to test the indirect effect. In the next tutorial we put this into practice — we fit the job-control → positive-affect → creativity mediation model in lavaan, first with the manifest variables from Tutorial 09, then with the full latent-variable SEM from Tutorial 10, and we extract bootstrap confidence intervals for the indirect effect.

title: Mediation — concept
text:
Further reading

Hayes (2018) — the standard reference for mediation, moderation, and conditional process analysis.
Kelloway (2015) — chapters 4 and 5 cover mediation in the context of SEM.
Sobel (1990) — the original Sobel test paper, included for historical context.
Hoyle (2012) — the Handbook of Structural Equation Modeling includes chapters on mediation.

title: Reporting lavaan results
text:
By the end of this tutorial you will be able to:

write an APA-style results paragraph for a CFA, path analysis, SEM, or mediation model
export lavaan parameter estimates and fit indices to publication-quality tables with texreg
export path diagrams with tidySEM as PNG or PDF files
compare nested models with lavTestLRT() and report the comparison
assemble a complete results section for a dissertation

title: Reporting lavaan results
text:
By the end of this tutorial you will be able to:

write an APA-style results paragraph for a CFA, path analysis, SEM, or mediation model
export lavaan parameter estimates and fit indices to publication-quality tables with texreg
export path diagrams with tidySEM as PNG or PDF files
compare nested models with lavTestLRT() and report the comparison
assemble a complete results section for a dissertation

title: Reporting lavaan results
text:
Setup


Setup code (you have seen this before)


library(haven)
library(lavaan)
library(psych)
library(texreg)
library(tidySEM)

title: Reporting lavaan results
text:
The canonical workflow
By now you have fitted CFA, path, SEM, and mediation models across Tutorials 06–12. This tutorial pulls together the reporting side: how to go from a fitted lavaan object to a polished results section for a dissertation or paper.
The canonical workflow is:

Fit the model and check convergence
Extract fit indices with fitMeasures()
Extract parameter estimates with summary() or parameterEstimates()
Export a publication-quality table with texreg
Export a path diagram with tidySEM
Write the APA results paragraph

We will demonstrate each step using the two-factor CFA from Tutorial 07 and the latent SEM mediation from Tutorial 12.

title: Reporting lavaan results
text:
Refitting the models
First, we refit the two models we will use as running examples:

# Two-factor CFA (Tutorial 07)
model_cfa <- '
  PA  =~ pa1 + pa2 + pa3
  neg =~ na1 + na2 + na3
  PA  ~~ neg
'
fit_cfa <- cfa(model_cfa, data = ps, estimator = "MLR")

# Latent SEM mediation (Tutorial 12)
model_sem <- '
  con   =~ p1 + p2 + p3
  pa    =~ pa1 + pa2 + pa3
  creat =~ creat1 + creat2 + creat3

  creat ~ b * pa + c * con
  pa    ~ a * con

  indirect := a * b
  direct   := c
  total    := c + (a * b)
'
set.seed(1234)
fit_sem <- sem(model_sem, data = ps,
               estimator = "ML", se = "boot", bootstrap = 1000)

title: Reporting lavaan results
text:
APA-style write-up templates

CFA results template

Confirmatory factor analysis was used to test the hypothesised factor structure of [construct], with [factor 1] measured by [items] and [factor 2] measured by [items]. The model was estimated with robust maximum likelihood (MLR). The model fit the data well, \(\chi^2(df) = value\), \(p = value\), CFI = value, TLI = value, RMSEA = value, 90% CI [lower, upper], SRMR = value. All factor loadings were significant (\(p < .001\)) and substantial (standardised \(\lambda\) range: [low] to [high]). The correlation between [factor 1] and [factor 2] was \(r = value\), \(p < .001\). Cronbach’s alpha was [alpha1] for [factor 1] and [alpha2] for [factor 2], indicating acceptable internal consistency.



SEM mediation results template

Structural equation modelling was used to test whether [mediator] mediated the relationship between [IV] and [DV]. [IV], [mediator], and [DV] were modelled as latent factors measured by [indicators]. The structural paths were estimated with maximum likelihood and [N] bootstrap replications; bias-corrected and accelerated (BCa) confidence intervals are reported for the indirect effect. The model fit the data well, \(\chi^2(df) = value\), CFI = value, TLI = value, RMSEA = value, 90% CI [lower, upper], SRMR = value. The indirect effect of [IV] on [DV] through [mediator] was \(ab = value\), 95% BCa CI [lower, upper], indicating [significant/non-significant] mediation. The direct effect was \(c' = value\), \(p = value\), suggesting [full/partial] mediation. The total effect was \(c = value\), \(p = value\).



Filling in the values
Let us extract the values for the CFA template:

fitMeasures(fit_cfa, c("chisq", "df", "pvalue",
                       "cfi", "tli",
                       "rmsea", "rmsea.ci.lower", "rmsea.ci.upper",
                       "srmr"))

##          chisq             df         pvalue            cfi            tli 
##          9.870          8.000          0.274          0.998          0.996 
##          rmsea rmsea.ci.lower rmsea.ci.upper           srmr 
##          0.027          0.000          0.073          0.015

pe_cfa <- parameterEstimates(fit_cfa, standardized = TRUE)
# Standardised loadings
pe_cfa[pe_cfa$op == "=~", c("rhs", "std.all")]

##   rhs std.all
## 1 pa1   0.829
## 2 pa2   0.764
## 3 pa3   0.856
## 4 na1   0.848
## 5 na2   0.819
## 6 na3   0.836

# Factor correlation
pe_cfa[pe_cfa$op == "~~" & pe_cfa$lhs == "PA" & pe_cfa$rhs == "neg",
       c("lhs", "rhs", "std.all", "pvalue")]

##   lhs rhs std.all pvalue
## 7  PA neg  -0.436      0

# Cronbach's alpha
psych::alpha(ps[, c("pa1", "pa2", "pa3")])$total$raw_alpha

## [1] 0.8482971

psych::alpha(ps[, c("na1", "na2", "na3")])$total$raw_alpha

## [1] 0.8649448


And for the SEM mediation template:

fitMeasures(fit_sem, c("chisq", "df", "pvalue",
                       "cfi", "tli",
                       "rmsea", "rmsea.ci.lower", "rmsea.ci.upper",
                       "srmr"))

##          chisq             df         pvalue            cfi            tli 
##         25.528         24.000          0.378          0.999          0.998 
##          rmsea rmsea.ci.lower rmsea.ci.upper           srmr 
##          0.014          0.000          0.047          0.028

pe_sem <- parameterEstimates(fit_sem, boot.ci.type = "bca.simple")
pe_sem[pe_sem$lhs %in% c("indirect", "direct", "total"),
       c("lhs", "est", "ci.lower", "ci.upper", "pvalue")]

##         lhs    est ci.lower ci.upper pvalue
## 25 indirect  0.047    0.011    0.098  0.037
## 26   direct -0.102   -0.241    0.041  0.149
## 27    total -0.055   -0.208    0.091  0.463

title: Reporting lavaan results
text:
Exporting tables with texreg
The texreg package exports lavaan results to clean, publication-quality tables in three formats: screen (for checking), HTML (for Word documents), and LaTeX (for PDF documents).

Screen output
For a quick check, use screenreg():

texreg::screenreg(fit_cfa,
                  summaries = c("N", "AIC", "BIC"),
                  labels = list(),
                  single.row = TRUE)

## 
## ===================================
##                  Model 1           
## -----------------------------------
## PA =~ pa1           1.00 (0.00)    
## PA =~ pa2           1.00 (0.06) ***
## PA =~ pa3           1.09 (0.07) ***
## neg =~ na1          1.00 (0.00)    
## neg =~ na2          0.94 (0.06) ***
## neg =~ na3          0.98 (0.06) ***
## PA ~~ neg          -0.40 (0.07) ***
## pa1 ~~ pa1          0.43 (0.05) ***
## pa2 ~~ pa2          0.68 (0.07) ***
## pa3 ~~ pa3          0.41 (0.06) ***
## na1 ~~ na1          0.35 (0.05) ***
## na2 ~~ na2          0.39 (0.05) ***
## na3 ~~ na3          0.38 (0.04) ***
## PA ~~ PA            0.95 (0.10) ***
## neg ~~ neg          0.90 (0.08) ***
## -----------------------------------
## agfi                0.97           
## AIC              5246.25           
## BIC              5295.64           
## cfi                 1.00           
## chisq               9.87           
## npar               13.00           
## rmsea               0.03           
## rmsea.conf.high     0.07           
## srmr                0.01           
## tli                 1.00           
## converged           1              
## ngroups             1              
## nobs              330              
## norig             343              
## nexcluded          13              
## ===================================
## *** p < 0.001; ** p < 0.01; * p < 0.05


This prints a compact table to the R console with each parameter as a row, its estimate, standard error, and significance stars. The summaries argument controls which fit statistics appear at the bottom.


HTML output (for Word documents)
To insert a lavaan table into a Word document, export it as HTML and copy-paste, or insert the HTML file into Word:

texreg::htmlreg(fit_cfa, file = "tables/table_cfa.html",
                summaries = c("N", "AIC", "BIC"),
                caption = "Confirmatory Factor Analysis of Positive and Negative Affect",
                caption.above = TRUE)

Open the HTML file in a web browser, select all, copy, and paste into Word — the table formatting is preserved.


LaTeX output (for PDF documents)
For documents typeset in LaTeX (e.g. via Quarto with a PDF output):

texreg::texreg(fit_cfa, file = "tables/table_cfa.tex",
               summaries = c("N", "AIC", "BIC"),
               caption = "Confirmatory Factor Analysis of Positive and Negative Affect",
               caption.above = TRUE,
               label = "tab:cfa")

Include the table in a LaTeX or Quarto document with \input{tables/table_cfa.tex} or via Quarto’s $include$ mechanism.


Comparing multiple models
texreg can display several models side by side, which is useful for comparing nested models (e.g. a first-order CFA vs. a second-order CFA, or a path model vs. a full SEM):

texreg::screenreg(list(fit_cfa, fit_cfa),
                  custom.model.names = c("First-order", "Second-order"),
                  summaries = c("N", "AIC", "BIC"))







Notetexreg and lavaan
