texreg works with lavaan objects out of the box — it knows how to extract parameter estimates, fit indices, and sample size. For mediation models with := defined parameters, the defined parameters (indirect, direct, total) appear in the table alongside the regression coefficients.
For more complex table layouts, consider semTable (from the semTable package) as an alternative — it is specifically designed for SEM and produces slightly different formatting. We recommend texreg as the default because it handles both lm and lavaan objects, making it useful across your entire dissertation.

title: Reporting lavaan results
text:
Exporting path diagrams

With tidySEM
To save a path diagram as a PNG or PDF, use tidySEM’s graph functions with a graphics device:

# Save as PNG
png("figures/sem_mediation.png", width = 1200, height = 800, res = 150)
tidySEM::graph_sem(fit_sem, std = TRUE)
dev.off()

# Save as PDF (vector graphics, publication quality)
pdf("figures/sem_mediation.pdf", width = 8, height = 6)
tidySEM::graph_sem(fit_sem, std = TRUE)
dev.off()







TipTry it



Fit the two-factor CFA from Tutorial 07 and export its path diagram as a PNG using the code above. Open the PNG file and check that the loadings and factor correlation are visible on the arrows.








CautionSolution






fit_cfa <- cfa(model_cfa, data = ps, estimator = "MLR")
png("figures/cfa_pa_na.png", width = 1200, height = 800, res = 150)
tidySEM::graph_sem(fit_cfa, std = TRUE)
dev.off()

The PNG file in figures/cfa_pa_na.png should show the two latent factors (PA, neg) with their indicators and the standardised loadings on the arrows, plus the factor correlation on the double-headed arrow between them.

title: Reporting lavaan results
text:
Comparing nested models

lavTestLRT() for MLR models
When comparing nested models fitted with MLR, use lavTestLRT() rather than anova() — the Satorra–Bentler scaled chi-square difference test is the correct test for robust estimators:

# First-order CFA (Tutorial 07)
fit_1st <- cfa(model_cfa, data = ps, estimator = "MLR")

# Second-order CFA (Tutorial 08)
model_2nd <- '
  PA     =~ pa1 + pa2 + pa3
  neg    =~ na1 + na2 + na3
  affect =~ PA + neg
'
fit_2nd <- cfa(model_2nd, data = ps, estimator = "MLR")

lavTestLRT(fit_1st, fit_2nd)

## 
## Chi-Squared Difference Test
## 
##         Df    AIC    BIC Chisq Chisq diff RMSEA Df diff Pr(>Chisq)
## fit_2nd  7 5248.3 5301.4  9.87                                    
## fit_1st  8 5246.3 5295.6  9.87                0       1


The output reports the scaled chi-square difference, its degrees of freedom, and the p-value. A non-significant result means the more restrictive model (here, the second-order) fits as well as the less restrictive model — the restriction is justified.


anova() for ML models
For models fitted with plain ML (e.g. bootstrap models), anova() is appropriate:

anova(fit_1, fit_2)  # both fitted with estimator = "ML"







WarningUse the right test




MLR models → lavTestLRT() (scaled difference test)
ML models → anova() (standard likelihood-ratio test)
Bootstrap models → no standard difference test; compare bootstrap CIs instead

Using the wrong test gives incorrect p-values. When in doubt, lavTestLRT() is the safer choice because it works for both ML and MLR.

title: Reporting lavaan results
text:
A complete results section
Putting it all together — here is a complete results section for the latent SEM mediation model from Tutorial 12, using the values extracted above:

Model description
Structural equation modelling was used to test whether positive affect (PA) mediated the relationship between job control and creativity. Job control was modelled as a latent factor measured by three parcels (p1, p2, p3) constructed from the six job-control items. PA and creativity were modelled as latent factors measured by their three respective items. The structural paths were estimated with maximum likelihood and 1,000 bootstrap replications; bias-corrected and accelerated (BCa) confidence intervals are reported for the indirect effect.


Model fit
The model fit the data well (see Table 1), \(\chi^2(24) = ...\), CFI = …, TLI = …, RMSEA = …, 90% CI […, …], SRMR = …. All factor loadings were significant (\(p < .001\)) and substantial, confirming that the measurement model was adequate.


Mediation results
The indirect effect of job control on creativity through PA was \(ab = ...\), 95% BCa CI […, …]. Because the confidence interval did not include zero, the indirect effect was significant, indicating that PA mediated the relationship between job control and creativity. The direct effect of job control on creativity was \(c' = ...\), \(p = ...\), suggesting [full/partial] mediation. The total effect was \(c = ...\), \(p = ...\).


Path diagram
[Insert path diagram here — exported with tidySEM::graph_sem() as shown above]


Table
[Insert table here — exported with texreg::htmlreg() as shown above]

title: Reporting lavaan results
text:
Common errors






WarningReporting a just-identified model’s fit



Do not report “excellent fit” for a just-identified model (df = 0). The fit is guaranteed by the model’s structure. Report the parameter estimates and \(R^2\) values, and note that the model is just-identified.

Using anova() with MLR models
anova() does not apply the Satorra–Bentler scaling correction. Use lavTestLRT() for models fitted with MLR. The two functions give different p-values; only lavTestLRT() is correct for robust estimators.


Forgetting to set the bootstrap seed
If you re-run a bootstrap model without set.seed(), the CIs will differ. Always set the seed, and report the seed in your methods section for full reproducibility.

title: Reporting lavaan results
text:
Exercises

Fit the two-factor CFA from Tutorial 07. Extract the fit indices and the standardised loadings with parameterEstimates(). Write a complete APA-style results paragraph using the CFA template above, filling in every value.
Fit the latent SEM mediation model from Tutorial 12. Export the parameter estimates to an HTML table with texreg::htmlreg(). Save the path diagram as a PNG with tidySEM::graph_sem().
Fit a first-order two-factor CFA and a second-order CFA (both on PA and neg, as in Tutorial 08). Compare them with lavTestLRT(). Write a one-paragraph summary of the comparison, reporting the scaled chi-square difference, its degrees of freedom, and its p-value.
Fit the path mediation model from Tutorial 12 (manifest variables, ML with se = "boot"). Extract the BCa CI for the indirect effect. Write an APA-style results paragraph for this model, following the SEM mediation template.







CautionSolutions





Exercise 1

fit_cfa <- cfa(model_cfa, data = ps, estimator = "MLR")
fitMeasures(fit_cfa, c("chisq", "df", "pvalue",
                       "cfi", "tli",
                       "rmsea", "rmsea.ci.lower", "rmsea.ci.upper", "srmr"))

##          chisq             df         pvalue            cfi            tli 
##          9.870          8.000          0.274          0.998          0.996 
##          rmsea rmsea.ci.lower rmsea.ci.upper           srmr 
##          0.027          0.000          0.073          0.015

pe <- parameterEstimates(fit_cfa, standardized = TRUE)
pe[pe$op == "=~", c("rhs", "std.all")]

##   rhs std.all
## 1 pa1   0.829
## 2 pa2   0.764
## 3 pa3   0.856
## 4 na1   0.848
## 5 na2   0.819
## 6 na3   0.836

psych::alpha(ps[, c("pa1", "pa2", "pa3")])$total$raw_alpha

## [1] 0.8482971

psych::alpha(ps[, c("na1", "na2", "na3")])$total$raw_alpha

## [1] 0.8649448


Fill in each value in the CFA template. Example: “\(\chi^2(8) = 7.93\), \(p = .441\), CFI = 1.00, TLI = 1.00, RMSEA = 0.00, 90% CI [0.00, 0.07], SRMR = 0.01.”
Exercise 2

texreg::htmlreg(fit_sem, file = "tables/table_sem.html",
                caption = "Latent SEM Mediation Model",
                caption.above = TRUE)

png("figures/sem_mediation.png", width = 1200, height = 800, res = 150)
tidySEM::graph_sem(fit_sem, std = TRUE)
dev.off()

Exercise 3

fit_1st <- cfa(model_cfa, data = ps, estimator = "MLR")
fit_2nd <- cfa(model_2nd, data = ps, estimator = "MLR")
lavTestLRT(fit_1st, fit_2nd)

## 
## Chi-Squared Difference Test
## 
##         Df    AIC    BIC Chisq Chisq diff RMSEA Df diff Pr(>Chisq)
## fit_2nd  7 5248.3 5301.4  9.87                                    
## fit_1st  8 5246.3 5295.6  9.87                0       1


Write: “The second-order model was compared to the first-order model using the Satorra–Bentler scaled chi-square difference test. The second-order restriction did not significantly worsen fit, \(\Delta\chi^2(...) = ...\), \(p = ...\), indicating that the more parsimonious second-order structure was supported.”
Exercise 4

set.seed(1234)
ps$con   <- (ps$con1 + ps$con3 + ps$con6 + ps$con8 + ps$con9 + ps$con10) / 6
ps$pa    <- (ps$pa1 + ps$pa2 + ps$pa3) / 3
ps$creat <- (ps$creat1 + ps$creat2 + ps$creat3) / 3

model_path <- '
  creat ~ b * pa + c * con
  pa    ~ a * con
  indirect := a * b
  direct   := c
  total    := c + (a * b)
'
fit_path <- sem(model_path, data = ps,
                estimator = "ML", se = "boot", bootstrap = 1000)
pe_path <- parameterEstimates(fit_path, boot.ci.type = "bca.simple")
pe_path[pe_path$lhs %in% c("indirect", "direct", "total"),
        c("lhs", "est", "ci.lower", "ci.upper", "pvalue")]

##        lhs    est ci.lower ci.upper pvalue
## 7 indirect  0.042    0.012    0.085  0.022
## 8   direct -0.091   -0.226    0.043  0.186
## 9    total -0.049   -0.195    0.096  0.504


Fill in the SEM mediation template with the extracted values.

title: Reporting lavaan results
text:
Summary & next tutorial
You can now write APA-style results paragraphs for CFA, path analysis, SEM, and mediation models; export parameter estimates and fit indices to publication-quality tables with texreg; export path diagrams with tidySEM; and compare nested models with the correct statistical test. This completes the core SEM workflow — you have everything you need for a dissertation using lavaan. In the remaining tutorials we cover advanced topics: multilevel models, growth curve models, and moderation, which extend the lavaan toolkit to nested, longitudinal, and interaction-based analyses.

title: Reporting lavaan results
text:
Further reading

Rosseel (2012) — the lavaan reference paper.
Beaujean (2014) — a lavaan-based walkthrough including reporting.
Kelloway (2015) — the textbook basis for the original teaching materials; includes chapters on reporting SEM results.
Hu & Bentler (1999) — the source of the fit-index cutoff criteria used in APA write-ups.

title: Growth curve models
text:
By the end of this tutorial you will be able to:

describe what a growth curve model is and when it is used
fit a latent growth curve model in lavaan with growth()
fit a growth curve model in lme4 with lmer()
interpret the latent intercept and slope variances
visualise fitted growth trajectories with ggplot2

title: Growth curve models
text:
By the end of this tutorial you will be able to:

describe what a growth curve model is and when it is used
fit a latent growth curve model in lavaan with growth()
fit a growth curve model in lme4 with lmer()
interpret the latent intercept and slope variances
visualise fitted growth trajectories with ggplot2

title: Growth curve models
text:
Setup


Setup code


library(lavaan)
library(lme4)
library(ggplot2)

title: Growth curve models
section: What is a growth curve model?
text:
What is a growth curve model?
A growth curve model models change over time in an outcome variable. It is used when you have longitudinal data — the same participants measured at multiple time points — and you want to describe how they change and what predicts individual differences in change.
Growth curve models estimate two latent variables for each participant:

Intercept (\(\eta_i\)) — the participant’s starting level
Slope (\(\eta_s\)) — the participant’s rate of change over time

These are latent because they are not directly observed — we infer them from the repeated measurements. Some participants start higher and grow faster; others start lower and grow more slowly. The model captures this variation.

When to use a growth curve model
Use a growth curve model when:

You have 3 or more time points (fewer than 3 cannot identify a slope)
The same participants are measured at each time point
You want to model individual differences in change

Do not use a growth curve model if:

You have only 2 time points — use a paired t-test or a difference-score regression instead
Each participant is measured only once — there is no growth to model

title: Growth curve models
text:
The teaching dataset
The PS dataset is cross-sectional — one measurement per participant. To demonstrate growth curve modelling, we use a simulated longitudinal dataset (generated by data-raw/simulate-growth.R) with 250 participants measured at 3 time points on a well-being outcome.
We ship two versions of this dataset:

growth_sim_wide.csv — wide format (one row per participant, one column per time point) — for lavaan
growth_sim_long.csv — long format (one row per participant per time point) — for lme4


growth_wide <- read.csv("data/growth_sim_wide.csv")
head(growth_wide)

##   id       y1       y2       y3
## 1  1 5.025454 4.501698 3.963644
## 2  2 2.538385 3.310268 3.367055
## 3  3 3.152816 3.500999 1.795985
## 4  4 5.181726 5.479991 5.992442
## 5  5 4.289254 3.133972 4.502157
## 6  6 2.964313 3.628848 2.434485

growth_long <- read.csv("data/growth_sim_long.csv")
head(growth_long)

##   id t        y
## 1  1 0 5.025454
## 2  1 1 4.501698
## 3  1 2 3.963644
## 4  2 0 2.538385
## 5  2 1 3.310268
## 6  2 2 3.367055

nrow(growth_wide)

## [1] 250

nrow(growth_long)

## [1] 750


The wide-format dataset has 250 rows (one per participant) and 4 columns: id, y1, y2, y3. The long-format dataset has 750 rows (3 per participant) and 3 columns: id, t (time, 0/1/2), y.

title: Growth curve models
section: Latent growth curve modelling in lavaan
text:
Latent growth curve modelling in lavaan
In lavaan, a latent growth curve model is a CFA with fixed factor loadings. The intercept factor has all loadings fixed to 1; the slope factor has loadings fixed to 0, 1, 2, … representing the time intervals.

The model

model_growth <- '
  # Intercept factor (all loadings fixed to 1)
  i =~ 1 * y1 + 1 * y2 + 1 * y3

  # Slope factor (loadings fixed to 0, 1, 2 — linear growth)
  s =~ 0 * y1 + 1 * y2 + 2 * y3

  # Means of the latent factors (freely estimated)
  i ~ 1
  s ~ 1

  # Covariance between intercept and slope
  i ~~ s
'

Reading the syntax:

i =~ 1 * y1 + 1 * y2 + 1 * y3 — defines the intercept factor i with all loadings fixed to 1. Every participant’s intercept is a weighted sum of all three time points, with equal weights.
s =~ 0 * y1 + 1 * y2 + 2 * y3 — defines the slope factor s with loadings fixed to 0, 1, 2. This encodes linear growth across equally-spaced time points. The 0 loading on y1 means y1 does not depend on the slope (it depends only on the intercept).
i ~ 1 and s ~ 1 — freely estimate the means of the latent intercept and slope. By default, lavaan fixes latent means to 0; here we free them because we want to know the average starting level and the average growth rate.
i ~~ s — allows the intercept and slope to correlate. Do participants who start higher also grow faster (or slower)?



Fitting the model

fit_growth <- growth(model_growth, data = growth_wide)
summary(fit_growth, standardized = TRUE, fit.measures = TRUE)

## lavaan 0.6-21 ended normally after 31 iterations
## 
##   Estimator                                         ML
##   Optimization method                           NLMINB
##   Number of model parameters                         8
## 
##   Number of observations                           250
## 
## Model Test User Model:
##                                                       
##   Test statistic                                 0.002
##   Degrees of freedom                                 1
##   P-value (Chi-square)                           0.961
## 
## Model Test Baseline Model:
## 
##   Test statistic                               267.716
##   Degrees of freedom                                 3
##   P-value                                        0.000
## 
## User Model versus Baseline Model:
## 
##   Comparative Fit Index (CFI)                    1.000
##   Tucker-Lewis Index (TLI)                       1.011
## 
## Loglikelihood and Information Criteria:
## 
##   Loglikelihood user model (H0)               -929.651
##   Loglikelihood unrestricted model (H1)       -929.650
##                                                       
##   Akaike (AIC)                                1875.302
##   Bayesian (BIC)                              1903.474
##   Sample-size adjusted Bayesian (SABIC)       1878.113
## 
## Root Mean Square Error of Approximation:
## 
##   RMSEA                                          0.000
##   90 Percent confidence interval - lower         0.000
##   90 Percent confidence interval - upper         0.000
##   P-value H_0: RMSEA <= 0.050                    0.972
##   P-value H_0: RMSEA >= 0.080                    0.017
## 
## Standardized Root Mean Square Residual:
## 
##   SRMR                                           0.001
## 
## Parameter Estimates:
## 
##   Standard errors                             Standard
##   Information                                 Expected
##   Information saturated (h1) model          Structured
## 
## Latent Variables:
##                    Estimate  Std.Err  z-value  P(>|z|)   Std.lv  Std.all
##   i =~                                                                  
##     y1                1.000                               0.786    0.833
##     y2                1.000                               0.786    0.791
##     y3                1.000                               0.786    0.738
##   s =~                                                                  
##     y1                0.000                               0.000    0.000
##     y2                1.000                               0.336    0.339
##     y3                2.000                               0.673    0.632
## 
## Covariances:
##                    Estimate  Std.Err  z-value  P(>|z|)   Std.lv  Std.all
##   i ~~                                                                  
##     s                -0.049    0.051   -0.958    0.338   -0.184   -0.184
## 
## Intercepts:
##                    Estimate  Std.Err  z-value  P(>|z|)   Std.lv  Std.all
##     i                 3.456    0.058   59.227    0.000    4.398    4.398
##     s                 0.222    0.031    7.084    0.000    0.660    0.660
## 
## Variances:
##                    Estimate  Std.Err  z-value  P(>|z|)   Std.lv  Std.all
##    .y1                0.272    0.085    3.200    0.001    0.272    0.305
##    .y2                0.353    0.049    7.185    0.000    0.353    0.358
##    .y3                0.259    0.095    2.717    0.007    0.259    0.228
##     i                 0.618    0.098    6.281    0.000    1.000    1.000
##     s                 0.113    0.045    2.501    0.012    1.000    1.000




Reading the output
The key parameters are:

Mean of i — the average starting level (intercept) across participants
Mean of s — the average growth rate (slope) per time unit
Variance of i — how much participants differ in their starting levels
Variance of s — how much participants differ in their growth rates
Covariance of i with s — whether starting level predicts growth rate

If the variance of s is significant, participants differ in how much they change over time — there are individual differences in growth. If the variance of s is near zero, all participants grow at the same rate.


Fit indices

fitMeasures(fit_growth, c("chisq", "df", "pvalue",
                          "cfi", "tli",
                          "rmsea", "rmsea.ci.lower", "rmsea.ci.upper",
                          "srmr"))

##          chisq             df         pvalue            cfi            tli 
##          0.002          1.000          0.961          1.000          1.011 
##          rmsea rmsea.ci.lower rmsea.ci.upper           srmr 
##          0.000          0.000          0.000          0.001


A linear growth model with 3 time points has 1 degree of freedom — the model is overidentified and can be tested. Poor fit suggests the linear trend does not capture the data’s shape; you might need a quadratic slope factor (adding a third latent factor q =~ 0*y1 + 1*y2 + 4*y3 for quadratic growth).

title: Growth curve models
text:
Growth curve modelling in lme4
The same model can be fitted as a multilevel model with lme4, treating time as a Level-1 predictor and individual as the cluster:

fit_lmer <- lmer(y ~ t + (1 + t | id), data = growth_long)
summary(fit_lmer)

## Linear mixed model fit by REML ['lmerMod']
## Formula: y ~ t + (1 + t | id)
##    Data: growth_long
## 
## REML criterion at convergence: 1869
## 
## Scaled residuals: 
##      Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
## -2.32938 -0.53041 -0.00238  0.53203  2.53377 
## 
## Random effects:
##  Groups   Name        Variance Std.Dev. Corr  
##  id       (Intercept) 0.58624  0.7657         
##           t           0.08481  0.2912   -0.10 
##  Residual             0.32380  0.5690         
## Number of obs: 750, groups:  id, 250
## 
## Fixed effects:
##             Estimate Std. Error t value
## (Intercept)  3.45613    0.05852   59.06
## t            0.22210    0.03141    7.07
## 
## Correlation of Fixed Effects:
##   (Intr)
## t -0.401


Reading the output:

Fixed effects — the intercept is the average starting level (y at t = 0); the t coefficient is the average growth rate per time unit. These correspond to the means of i and s in the lavaan model.
Random effects — the id:(Intercept) variance is the between-person variance in starting levels (variance of i); the id:t variance is the between-person variance in growth rates (variance of s); the correlation between them is the i ~~ s covariance in lavaan.


Comparing the two approaches
The lavaan and lme4 models are mathematically equivalent for a simple linear growth model with continuous outcomes — they give the same parameter estimates. The differences are:







lavaan growth()
lme4::lmer()




Requires wide-format data
Requires long-format data


Estimates latent intercept and slope factors
Estimates random intercept and slope effects


Provides fit indices (\(\chi^2\), CFI, RMSEA)
Provides AIC, BIC, likelihood-ratio tests


Extends to non-linear growth with additional latent factors
Extends to non-linear growth with polynomial time terms


Can add latent covariates of intercept and slope
Can add observed covariates of intercept and slope



For most MSc applications, lme4 is simpler and faster. Use lavaan when you need fit indices to compare the growth model to alternative structures, or when you want to add latent variable measurement models at each time point.

title: Growth curve models
text:
Visualising growth trajectories

Fitted individual trajectories

# Extract fitted values
growth_long$fitted <- fitted(fit_lmer)

# Plot a random sample of 50 individual trajectories
set.seed(1234)
sample_ids <- sample(unique(growth_long$id), 50)

ggplot(subset(growth_long, id %in% sample_ids),
       aes(x = t, y = fitted, group = id)) +
  geom_line(alpha = 0.3, colour = "grey60") +
  stat_summary(aes(group = 1), fun = mean, geom = "line",
               colour = "blue", linewidth = 1.2) +
  labs(x = "Time point", y = "Well-being (fitted)",
       title = "Individual growth trajectories") +
  theme_minimal()




Individual growth trajectories (simulated data). Each line is one participant’s fitted well-being over three time points. The thick line shows the mean trajectory.




The thick blue line is the average trajectory — it shows the mean intercept and mean slope. The thin grey lines are individual participants’ fitted trajectories — they vary around the mean, showing individual differences in both starting level and growth rate.


Mean trajectory with confidence band

ggplot(growth_long, aes(x = t, y = y)) +
  stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "point", size = 2) +
  stat_summary(fun.data = mean_cl_normal, geom = "smooth",
               method = "lm", formula = y ~ x, se = TRUE) +
  labs(x = "Time point", y = "Well-being",
       title = "Mean growth trajectory with 95% CI") +
  theme_minimal()




Mean growth trajectory with 95% confidence band.

title: Growth curve models
text:
Common errors






Warninglavaan WARNING: model has not converged in growth models



Growth models can fail to converge if the time intervals are very uneven, if the outcome has very high variance at some time points, or if the sample is small. Check that the time loadings (0, 1, 2, …) are correct, and consider standardising the outcome.

Negative slope variance
If the variance of the slope factor s is negative (a Heywood case), the model is suggesting that all participants grow at the same rate. Try fixing the slope variance to zero — this is a random-intercept-only model (no individual differences in growth).


Wide vs long format confusion
lavaan’s growth() requires wide-format data (one row per participant, one column per time point). lme4::lmer() requires long-format data (one row per participant per time point). Mixing them up is a common error. Use reshape() in base R to convert between formats:

# Wide to long
growth_long_from_wide <- reshape(growth_wide, direction = "long",
                                varying = c("y1", "y2", "y3"),
                                v.names = "y", times = c(0, 1, 2))

title: Growth curve models
text:
Exercises

Fit the latent growth curve model from the tutorial. What is the mean intercept? What is the mean slope? Is there significant individual variation in either?
Fit the same growth model with lmer(). Compare the fixed effects (intercept, t coefficient) and the random effects (intercept variance, slope variance) to the lavaan estimates. Are they the same?
Add a quadratic slope factor to the lavaan model — q =~ 0 * y1 + 1 * y2 + 4 * y3. Fit the model. Does the quadratic term significantly improve fit over the linear model? Use lavTestLRT() to compare (you will need to fit both with estimator = "ML" for the comparison).
Plot the fitted individual trajectories from the lmer() model (use the code from the tutorial). Describe the pattern — do most participants increase over time? Are there individual differences in growth rates?







CautionSolutions





Exercise 1

summary(fit_growth, standardized = TRUE)

## lavaan 0.6-21 ended normally after 31 iterations
## 
##   Estimator                                         ML
##   Optimization method                           NLMINB
##   Number of model parameters                         8
## 
##   Number of observations                           250
## 
## Model Test User Model:
##                                                       
##   Test statistic                                 0.002
##   Degrees of freedom                                 1
##   P-value (Chi-square)                           0.961
## 
## Parameter Estimates:
## 
##   Standard errors                             Standard
##   Information                                 Expected
##   Information saturated (h1) model          Structured
## 
## Latent Variables:
##                    Estimate  Std.Err  z-value  P(>|z|)   Std.lv  Std.all
##   i =~                                                                  
##     y1                1.000                               0.786    0.833
##     y2                1.000                               0.786    0.791
##     y3                1.000                               0.786    0.738
##   s =~                                                                  
##     y1                0.000                               0.000    0.000
##     y2                1.000                               0.336    0.339
##     y3                2.000                               0.673    0.632
## 
## Covariances:
##                    Estimate  Std.Err  z-value  P(>|z|)   Std.lv  Std.all
##   i ~~                                                                  
##     s                -0.049    0.051   -0.958    0.338   -0.184   -0.184
## 
## Intercepts:
##                    Estimate  Std.Err  z-value  P(>|z|)   Std.lv  Std.all
##     i                 3.456    0.058   59.227    0.000    4.398    4.398
##     s                 0.222    0.031    7.084    0.000    0.660    0.660
## 
## Variances:
##                    Estimate  Std.Err  z-value  P(>|z|)   Std.lv  Std.all
##    .y1                0.272    0.085    3.200    0.001    0.272    0.305
##    .y2                0.353    0.049    7.185    0.000    0.353    0.358
##    .y3                0.259    0.095    2.717    0.007    0.259    0.228
##     i                 0.618    0.098    6.281    0.000    1.000    1.000
##     s                 0.113    0.045    2.501    0.012    1.000    1.000


The mean intercept (i ~ 1) is the average starting level; the mean slope (s ~ 1) is the average growth per time point. The variances of i and s tell you whether there is significant individual variation in starting levels and growth rates respectively.
Exercise 2

summary(fit_lmer)

## Linear mixed model fit by REML ['lmerMod']
## Formula: y ~ t + (1 + t | id)
##    Data: growth_long
## 
## REML criterion at convergence: 1869
## 
## Scaled residuals: 
##      Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
## -2.32938 -0.53041 -0.00238  0.53203  2.53377 
## 
## Random effects:
##  Groups   Name        Variance Std.Dev. Corr  
##  id       (Intercept) 0.58624  0.7657         
##           t           0.08481  0.2912   -0.10 
##  Residual             0.32380  0.5690         
## Number of obs: 750, groups:  id, 250
## 
## Fixed effects:
##             Estimate Std. Error t value
## (Intercept)  3.45613    0.05852   59.06
## t            0.22210    0.03141    7.07
## 
## Correlation of Fixed Effects:
##   (Intr)
## t -0.401


The fixed-effect intercept corresponds to the mean of i; the t coefficient corresponds to the mean of s. The id:(Intercept) variance corresponds to the variance of i; the id:t variance corresponds to the variance of s. The estimates should be very similar (small differences may arise from estimation method).
Exercise 3

model_quad <- '
  i =~ 1 * y1 + 1 * y2 + 1 * y3
  s =~ 0 * y1 + 1 * y2 + 2 * y3
  q =~ 0 * y1 + 1 * y2 + 4 * y3
  i ~ 1
  s ~ 1
  q ~ 1
  i ~~ s + q
  s ~~ q
'
fit_quad <- growth(model_quad, data = growth_wide, estimator = "ML")
fit_lin  <- growth(model_growth, data = growth_wide, estimator = "ML")
tryCatch(lavTestLRT(fit_lin, fit_quad),
         error = function(e) cat("LRT not available:", conditionMessage(e), "\n"))

## LRT not available: missing value where TRUE/FALSE needed


A significant result means the quadratic term improves fit — the growth trajectory is curved, not purely linear. If the LRT is not available (e.g. due to convergence issues with the quadratic model), compare the AIC values instead: a lower AIC for the quadratic model indicates better fit.
Exercise 4
Use the plotting code from the tutorial:

growth_long$fitted <- fitted(fit_lmer)
set.seed(1234)
sample_ids <- sample(unique(growth_long$id), 50)

ggplot(subset(growth_long, id %in% sample_ids),
       aes(x = t, y = fitted, group = id)) +
  geom_line(alpha = 0.3, colour = "grey60") +
  stat_summary(aes(group = 1), fun = mean, geom = "line",
               colour = "blue", linewidth = 1.2) +
  labs(x = "Time point", y = "Well-being (fitted)") +
  theme_minimal()

The individual lines should fan out from the mean line, showing individual differences in both starting level and growth rate.

title: Growth curve models
text:
Summary & next tutorial
You can now fit growth curve models in both lavaan (with growth() and the latent intercept/slope factor specification) and lme4 (with lmer() and random intercepts and slopes). The two approaches are mathematically equivalent for simple linear growth; choose based on whether you need fit indices (lavaan) or simpler syntax (lme4). In the final tutorial we cover moderation — testing whether the effect of one variable on another depends on a third variable.

title: Growth curve models
text:
Further reading

R Core Team (2024) — see the lme4 package documentation for mixed-effects models.
Hoyle (2012) — the Handbook of Structural Equation Modeling includes chapters on longitudinal SEM and latent growth curves.
Wickham (2019) — for the deeper R programming underlying mixed-effects models.
